entries.desc: CASEL framework (updated 2020) defining the five core social-emotional competencies — self-awareness, self-management, social awareness, relationship skills, and responsible decision-making — and their implementation across four settings: classrooms, schools, families and caregivers, and communities. The 2020 update strengthened the framework's equity orientation, emphasizing SEL's potential in promoting educational equity and excellence and its adaptability to local cultural contexts.

entries.title: Engaging First: Supporting Young Learners Through Family Engagement
entries.desc: EdTrust research brief by Ivy Morgan (2024) examining family-school partnerships for early elementary learners based on nationally representative surveys of 600 parents/caregivers and 300 teachers of first and second graders during 2022–23. Finds broadly positive communication overall but significant gaps: only ~two-thirds of parents had access to parent-teacher conferences; low-income and multilingual families faced greater barriers including unwelcoming school staff. Part of EdTrust's broader SEAD research program.

entries.title: Making Assessment Reports More Meaningful for Students & Families
entries.desc: EdTrust report by Munyan-Penney and Levitan (2024) providing guidance for schools on improving communication of federally required annual assessment results to students and families. Advocates for integrating score reports into existing family communications, identifies best practices for accessible report design, and offers policy recommendations for advocates and state leaders to improve assessment reporting at scale.

entries.title: How Student, Family, and Community Engagement Impacts Students' SEAD
entries.desc: EdTrust report by Duchesneau and Wriston (2024) examining how school, family, and community engagement supports students' social, emotional, and academic development (SEAD). Identifies obstacles to meaningful engagement and provides policy recommendations for federal, state, and local decision-makers. Part of EdTrust's broader research on whole-child development and the role of family-school partnerships in student wellbeing and academic outcomes.

entries.title: Funding Gaps 2018
entries.desc: EdTrust report by Morgan and Amerikaner (2018) documenting school funding disparities across U.S. states and districts. Finds districts serving predominantly students of color receive ~$1,800 (13%) less per student in state and local funding than majority-white districts; high-poverty districts receive ~$1,000 (7%) less than low-poverty districts. Gap widens to $2,000 per student (16%) when adjusted for additional costs of supporting low-income students. Documents state-by-state variation and highlights states achieving more equitable funding.

entries.title: Teacher Turnover in the United States: Who Moves, Who Leaves, and Why
entries.desc: This comprehensive analysis examines why approximately 1 in 7 public school teachers annually leave their schools or exit the profession, using national data from 2020–22. The report finds that turnover rates have increased roughly 27% since the early 1990s, with disparities concentrated in schools serving predominantly low-income students and students of color. Three primary factors significantly influence retention: teacher salaries, effective school leadership, and overall job satisfaction tied to classroom autonomy and collegial support.

entries.title: Supporting and Sustaining a Diverse Teacher Workforce
entries.desc: This report examines why teachers of color leave the profession at higher rates than their white counterparts and identifies systemic barriers to their retention. Using national survey data from 2017–2021, researchers found that teachers of color face less access to comprehensive preparation, competitive salaries, mentoring support, and positive working environments. The study emphasizes that while recruiting more educators of color matters, retention through improved conditions and support systems is equally critical to building a stable, diverse teaching force.

entries.title: Untangling the Evidence on Preschool Effectiveness: Insights for Policymakers
entries.desc: This report synthesizes rigorous evaluations of 21 public preschool programs to clarify conflicting findings about whether quality early childhood education produces meaningful gains for students. The research demonstrates that high-quality preschool leaves children better prepared for school, especially in academic skill development, with benefits extending into adulthood through higher graduation rates and earnings. The report identifies six essential quality elements—including small class sizes, well-trained teachers, developmentally appropriate curricula, and family engagement—and notes that effective programs generate up to $17 in social returns for every dollar invested.

entries.title: Community Schools as an Effective School Improvement Strategy: A Review of the Evidence
entries.desc: This comprehensive report synthesizes findings from 143 rigorous research studies examining how community schools impact student and school outcomes, identifying four core pillars of effective implementation: integrated student supports, expanded learning time, family and community engagement, and collaborative leadership. The study demonstrates that community schools help disadvantaged students overcome barriers to learning by integrating academic instruction with health services, youth development, and community partnerships. The evidence base meets federal requirements under the Every Student Succeeds Act, providing policymakers with research-backed guidance for implementing community schools as an equitable school improvement strategy.

entries.title: Coaching at Scale: A Strategy for Strengthening the Early Learning Workforce
entries.desc: This report examines five early childhood coaching systems across Alabama, Washington, and California to understand how comprehensive coaching can be implemented at scale to improve educator practice and program quality. The research identifies that effective coaching relies on strong partnerships, individualized feedback, focused observations, and well-qualified coaches with expertise in child development and adult learning. The report provides policymakers with eight actionable recommendations, including dedicated funding for coaching, establishing coach qualification standards, and developing regional professional development networks to sustain systemic improvements across the early learning workforce.

entries.title: Reducing Chronic Absenteeism: Lessons from Community Schools
entries.desc: This report examines how four California community schools successfully reduced chronic absenteeism following the pandemic, when national rates had nearly doubled to 28% by 2022. Key strategies included strengthening family engagement through outreach and home visits, building student connections through mentorship, systematically tracking attendance data, implementing tiered support systems, and leveraging community partnerships to address underlying barriers like housing and mental health needs. The findings emphasize that proactive and preventive approaches combined with trusting relationships prove more effective than punitive measures in addressing attendance challenges.

entries.title: Strengthening the Educator Pipeline Through Service Scholarships: California's Golden State Teacher Grant Program
entries.desc: This report evaluates California's Golden State Teacher Grant Program, which has distributed over $570 million since 2020–21 to help aspiring teachers afford preparation costs in exchange for committing to teach in high-need schools for two years, supporting more than 28,000 candidates and likely contributing to a 23% increase in California-prepared teachers earning credentials. Nearly 75% of survey respondents cited the grant as influential in their decision to pursue teaching, with particularly strong impact among candidates of color and career changers. The report recommends stable long-term funding benchmarked to cover significant portions of preparation costs, improved implementation support, and better data collection systems to track long-term outcomes.

entries.title: High-Quality Early Childhood Assessment: Learning From States' Use of Kindergarten Entry Assessments
entries.desc: This report examines kindergarten entry assessments (KEAs) across states, noting that as of 2021, 38 states had adopted KEAs—a fivefold increase over a decade—though concerns arose about inappropriate practices like overly scripted assessments or narrow skill-focused measures that exclude essential developmental domains. The report emphasizes that high-quality KEA content aligns with developmentally appropriate kindergarten standards and measures multiple domains including social-emotional, cognitive, language, and physical development in culturally relevant ways. It recommends states select authentic assessments, build systems supporting teacher instruction and family engagement, and use data to strengthen learning systems rather than evaluate individual providers.

entries.title: The Building Blocks of High-Quality Early Childhood Education Programs
entries.desc: This policy brief identifies nine essential components that characterize effective pre-K programs, grounded in research demonstrating that high-quality early education generates approximately $7 in returns for every dollar invested. The report emphasizes that quality matters tremendously—particularly for low-income children and English learners—and establishes benchmarks including well-qualified teachers with bachelor's degrees in early childhood education, ongoing coaching and mentoring, support for diverse learners, meaningful family partnerships, small class sizes, and robust quality rating systems. These interconnected elements work together to foster the meaningful teacher-child interactions that research shows are fundamental to producing measurable gains in academic achievement and social-emotional development.

entries.title: Community Schools Impact on Student Outcomes: Evidence From California
entries.desc: This report evaluates California's $4.1 billion Community Schools Partnership Program, the nation's largest state-level community schools initiative launched in 2021, using a matched difference-in-differences methodology comparing 458 CCSPP schools to similar non-recipient schools from 2018–19 to 2023–24. Researchers found that community school approaches led to a meaningful reduction in chronic absence averaging 30% greater than comparison schools, along with notable improvements in suspension rates and test scores. The most substantial gains benefited historically underserved students including Black students, English learners, and economically disadvantaged youth.

entries.title: Teacher Residencies in Texas: Supporting Successful Implementation
entries.desc: This report examines Texas's High-Quality, Sustainable Residencies Program, which funded over 85 teacher residency programs from 2021–2024 using COVID-19 relief money and provided stipends for more than 2,000 residents. The research identifies essential program features—including full-year classroom placements, $20,000 resident stipends, mentor teachers, and co-teaching models—as critical to successful implementation, while highlighting how strategic staffing models help districts sustain paid residencies beyond grant funding. The report concludes with six policy recommendations including requirements for research-based residency features, support for mentor teacher pipelines, and targeted funding for rural and high-poverty districts facing acute teacher shortages.

entries.title: Diversifying the Teaching Profession: How to Recruit and Retain Teachers of Color
entries.desc: This report examines barriers that impede teachers of color from entering and remaining in the profession, while documenting how such teachers significantly benefit student outcomes. The research reveals that while recruitment has increased, attrition rates remain high, creating persistent supply-demand gaps particularly among Black, Native American, and Latinx educators. The study identifies three primary intervention areas: high-retention pathways into teaching through scholarships and residencies; hiring strategies that include diverse committees and early recruitment; and improving school conditions through better leadership preparation.

entries.title: Design Principles for Teacher Preparation: Enacting the Science of Learning and Development
entries.desc: This report presents five design principles for teacher preparation programs that incorporate findings from neuroscience, developmental psychology, and learning sciences to help future educators support whole child development. The framework emphasizes that teacher candidates must themselves experience the very kinds of teaching strategies they are expected to develop for their pupils, ensuring teachers can authentically model approaches like inquiry-based learning and restorative practices. The principles address curriculum grounded in learning science, equitable educator mindsets, experiential learning opportunities, pedagogical alignment between university coursework and school placements, and the creation of supportive professional learning communities.

entries.title: State Preschool in a Mixed Delivery System: Lessons From Five States
entries.desc: This report examines how five states—Alabama, Michigan, New Jersey, New York, and West Virginia—operate public preschool programs across diverse provider settings including school districts and community-based organizations. The study identifies key policy decisions regarding governance, quality standards, teacher compensation, and enrollment coordination that enable states to maintain consistent educational quality while serving children through multiple delivery channels. The authors recommend that states establish uniform quality benchmarks across all settings, remove barriers to non-LEA participation, provide sustained professional development, ensure competitive teacher compensation, implement coordinated enrollment systems, and collect demographic data to identify access disparities.

entries.title: Developing Assessment Systems That Support Teaching and Learning: Recommendations for Federal Support
entries.desc: This report examines how the federal government can support states in transforming their assessment systems beyond traditional end-of-year multiple-choice tests, noting that more than 20 states are involved in efforts to transform one or more aspects of their assessment systems but face substantial barriers to federal approval. The authors identify three key recommendations for federal executive action: updating the Department of Education's peer review process to align with ESSA's encouragement of innovative assessments, strengthening the Innovative Assessment Demonstration Authority program to reduce burdensome requirements, and expanding funding pathways to incentivize state-level innovation in assessment design.

entries.title: School Funding Effectiveness: Evidence From California's Local Control Funding Formula
entries.desc: This comprehensive study examines how California's 2013 Local Control Funding Formula—which directed $18 billion in increased state support to high-need districts over eight years—affected student outcomes across the state's 1,000 districts and 10,000 schools. Tracking 6.2 million K-12 students from 1995–96 through 2018–19, the research finds that a $1,000 per-pupil spending increase yielded full grade-level improvements in math and reading after three years, boosted high school graduation rates by 8.2 percentage points, and decreased suspensions and expulsions particularly for Black students. Instructional spending—class size reduction, teacher salary increases, and reduced turnover—accounted for roughly 84% of the variation in funding effectiveness.

entries.title: Lost Opportunities: How Disparate School Discipline Continues to Drive Differences in the Opportunity to Learn
entries.desc: This report examines how out-of-school suspensions and other exclusionary discipline practices create substantial learning loss, with particularly severe impacts on students of color, secondary students, and those with disabilities. During the 2015–16 school year alone, the nation lost approximately 11.4 million instructional days due to suspensions—the equivalent of 62,596 years of lost learning. The research documents widespread racial disparities in discipline outcomes and significant failures by districts to report school-policing data as required by federal law.

entries.title: Long-Term English Learners in California
entries.desc: This report examines approximately 330,000 California students classified as English learners for 7 or more years, analyzing their demographics, school contexts, and academic outcomes compared to peers. The research reveals that long-term English learners are disproportionately boys (56%), economically disadvantaged (89%), and have higher special education needs (28%), while attending schools with fewer resources and lower graduation rates. Key recommendations include ensuring adequate schooling resources and whole-child supports, addressing teacher shortages in high-poverty schools, better identifying early learning needs for students with disabilities, and conducting further research on graduation barriers for this vulnerable population.

entries.title: Investing for Student Success: Lessons From State School Finance Reforms
entries.desc: This report examines how U.S. public schools are inequitably funded through reliance on local property taxes and analyzes reform efforts in Connecticut, Massachusetts, New Jersey, and North Carolina that successfully improved educational resources and outcomes. The research demonstrates that when more money is spent on education, especially for students from low-income families, achievement and graduation rates improve alongside life outcomes like employment and wages. The report recommends federal and state actions—including equalizing funding allocations, ensuring qualified teachers reach high-poverty schools, and investing in quality preschool—as essential steps toward creating equitable learning opportunities for all students.

entries.title: Advancing Integration and Equity Through Magnet Schools
entries.desc: This report examines how magnet schools—public schools offering specialized programs to attract diverse student populations—can combat the alarming resegregation occurring in U.S. schools. The authors identify key components for success, including "first door" strategies like intentional family outreach and inclusive enrollment, plus "second door" approaches such as culturally responsive curriculum and restorative discipline practices. The report provides policy recommendations at federal, state, district, and school levels to help create and sustain magnet schools that achieve both integration and positive academic outcomes for all students.

entries.title: Instructionally Relevant Assessment Systems: What is the Role of Performance Assessments?
entries.desc: This report examines how performance assessments—tasks requiring human scoring like constructed responses and essays—can transform state assessment systems to prioritize positive instructional impact alongside accountability functions. The author argues that because teachers inevitably take cues from state-level assessments to shape their instruction, assessment designers should intentionally leverage this influence by incorporating authentic performance tasks that signal high-quality teaching practices. The report provides system leaders with five strategic recommendations for balancing performance assessments with other instruments, emphasizing that well-designed tasks can generate reliable comparable scores while simultaneously improving what students learn and how teachers teach.

entries.title: The Promise of Performance Assessments: Innovations in High School Learning and Higher Education Admissions
entries.desc: This report examines how schools and colleges are adopting performance-based assessments—such as portfolios, capstone projects, and senior defenses—to evaluate deeper learning skills needed for college and career success. The authors document that more than 900 colleges have made standardized tests optional and are increasingly interested in reviewing broader student work samples for admission decisions. A key finding is that these assessments show particular promise for better reflecting the achievements and potential of historically underserved students, potentially addressing longstanding disparities in standardized testing outcomes across racial and socioeconomic lines.

entries.title: Promising Models for Preparing a Diverse, High-Quality Early Childhood Workforce
entries.desc: This report examines innovative pathways for recruiting and preparing early childhood educators with a focus on creating accessible routes for diverse candidates, identifying three successful California programs that demonstrate how to support educators from underrepresented backgrounds through affordable, hands-on preparation models. The analysis reveals significant barriers early educators face when pursuing higher education, notably that low wages often make higher education costs prohibitively expensive. The report recommends that states invest in faculty development, clinical experiences, clear transfer pathways between community and four-year colleges, robust financial assistance, and coherent credential standards to ensure all young children have qualified teachers reflecting their communities' diversity.

entries.title: Community Schools in Lynwood Unified: Building Capacity for Districtwide Implementation
entries.desc: This report examines how Lynwood Unified School District successfully scaled community schools across all 17 campuses by strategically developing community school coordinators as implementation leaders, leveraging $24 million in state funding to expand from a single pilot school to a districtwide initiative. The district achieved measurable improvements including a one-third reduction in chronic absence, enhanced academic performance, and a 15.5% decline in suspensions within two years. The research highlights five critical implementation strategies including data-driven needs assessments, structured professional learning communities, and coaching networks that positioned coordinators as essential change agents rather than peripheral support roles.

entries.title: State Strategies for Investing in Community Schools
entries.desc: This report examines how states are funding and supporting community schools by identifying three primary approaches: ongoing funding through school financing formulas (Maryland, New York), competitive grant programs (California, Illinois, New Mexico, Vermont), and capacity-building supports like certification processes (Florida, Georgia). State investments range from $3.4 million to $4.1 billion, with most states prioritizing schools serving high-poverty populations. Key findings show states are increasingly investing in evidence-based strategies including integrated student supports, expanded learning time, family engagement, and collaborative leadership structures that align with trauma-informed practices.

entries.title: The Road to High-Quality Early Learning: Lessons From the States
entries.desc: This report examines how four states—Michigan, West Virginia, Washington, and North Carolina—have built high-quality early education systems, drawing on policy reviews, program observations, and interviews with 159 stakeholders. The analysis identifies five overarching strategies these states employ: establishing quality standards and improvement systems, investing in educator development, coordinating birth-through-grade-3 programs, combining multiple funding sources, and building broad-based coalitions. The report provides policymakers with actionable insights on defining and using state quality standards, linking funding to ratings, investing in teacher training and coaching, and fostering cross-agency coordination to create seamless educational experiences for young learners.

entries.title: Opportunities Denied: High-Achieving Black and Latino Students Lack Access to Advanced Math
entries.desc: High-achieving Black and Latino students and students from low-income backgrounds who pass Algebra I in eighth grade still enroll in advanced high school math at lower rates than their peers. Developed jointly with Just Equations, this brief documents enrollment disparities and identifies differences in teacher and counselor practices as contributing factors to these access gaps.

entries.title: Social, Emotional, and Academic Development Through an Equity Lens
entries.desc: Schools often narrowly focus on changing student behavior rather than building relationships and supportive learning environments, disproportionately affecting students of color and low-income students. This framework guides school and district leaders to shift attention from "fixing kids" to examining adult beliefs, school policies, and district practices that create equitable learning environments, ensuring all students experience belonging, challenging opportunities, and appropriate supports.

entries.title: Hard Truths: Why Only Race-Conscious Policies Can Fix Racism in Higher Education
entries.desc: This brief addresses misconceptions about affirmative action by demonstrating that Black and Latino students remain underrepresented at selective colleges and public universities across most states. The report argues that addressing racial inequities in higher education requires explicit race-conscious policy interventions, as income-based alternatives have failed to close opportunity gaps for students of color.

entries.title: If You Listen, We Will Stay: Why Teachers of Color Leave and How to Disrupt Teacher Turnover
entries.desc: Released jointly by The Education Trust and Teach Plus, this research examines why educators of color leave the teaching profession at higher rates than their peers despite representing less than 20% of the nation's teaching force. Drawing on focus groups with Black and Latino teachers and case studies of schools successfully retaining faculty of color, the report identifies five key barriers teachers face and five actions effective schools employ to support diverse educators.

entries.title: National & State Trends in Degree Attainment for Black Adults
entries.desc: This analysis examines college degree completion rates among Black adults aged 25–64 across the United States and individual states. The report contends that the nation's colleges and universities are not adequately supporting Black and Latino student success, and that policymakers must prioritize higher education access and completion for historically underserved populations to meet national workforce demands and promote economic growth.

entries.title: Inequities in Advanced Coursework
entries.desc: This report examines barriers preventing Black and Latino students from accessing AP, IB, and dual enrollment programs, finding that while these students succeed when given opportunities, they remain underrepresented due to three compounding factors: schools serving predominantly students of color offering fewer advanced courses, lower overall enrollment in those courses, and racially diverse schools actively denying access. The report provides recommendations for state and district leaders to expand access and improve equity in advanced coursework enrollment.

entries.title: 'Segregation Forever?': The Continued Underrepresentation of Black Undergraduates at the Nation's 122 Most Selective Private Colleges and Universities
entries.desc: EdTrust examined Black student enrollment at 122 selective private colleges and universities in 2000 and 2020, finding that despite nearly 74% of these institutions increasing Black enrollment, 71% still received failing grades for Black student access in 2020. The research reveals that gains were minimal and that elite institutions remain largely inaccessible to Black first-time undergraduates relative to state population demographics.

entries.title: Promising Practices: Smart State Strategies for Building Intensive Tutoring Systems
entries.desc: This brief examines how states are implementing high-impact tutoring programs to address learning disruptions caused by school closures. The publication identifies promising state strategies including workforce development, research-based program standards, and professional development, while emphasizing equitable access to tutoring for underserved students.

entries.title: How School Discipline Impacts Students' Social, Emotional, and Academic Development (SEAD)
entries.desc: This brief examines how school discipline policies affect student development, highlighting that harmful practices like corporal punishment, suspensions, and expulsions disproportionately impact students of color and those with disabilities. The report advocates for evidence-based approaches such as restorative justice and emphasizes the importance of implementing discipline policies through a racial equity lens to create safe, supportive learning environments.

entries.title: 5 Things for Advocates to Know About Chronic Absenteeism
entries.desc: This brief documents the rising epidemic of chronic school absence, noting that 16 million students nationally are now chronically absent — missing 10% or more of school days — with disproportionate impact on low-income students, students of color, multilingual learners, and students with disabilities. The report advocates for evidence-based approaches addressing root causes rather than punitive measures, and highlights the importance of prevention, early intervention, and authentic partnerships with families and communities.

entries.title: How Mental Health Supports Impact Social, Emotional, and Academic Development (SEAD)
entries.desc: This brief examines how school-based mental health services support student success, highlighting significant disparities in access for students of color, low-income students, and students with disabilities. The authors present CDC data showing alarming mental health challenges among high school students and emphasize that district leaders must implement mental health support services rooted in a race-based lens to address these inequities.

entries.title: Future of Assessments: Centering Equity and the Lived Experiences of Students, Families, and Educators
entries.desc: This report examines statewide assessment practices through focus groups with students, families, educators, and administrators, revealing that stakeholders want assessment data from multiple sources, believe assessments are just one measure of college readiness, and seek timely and actionable results alongside improved resource allocation to underserved schools. The report proposes four equity pillars to guide future federal education policy and assessment design.

entries.title: Holding Students Back: An Inequitable and Ineffective Response to Unfinished Learning
entries.desc: This factsheet examines grade retention as a response to pandemic-related learning loss, arguing that while retention may show short-term test score improvements, research demonstrates it produces negative long-term academic, social, and emotional outcomes — particularly for Black, Latino, and Native students. The analysis recommends evidence-based alternatives including targeted tutoring, expanded learning time, and strong teacher-student relationships.

entries.title: How Affordable Are Public Colleges in Your State? (Affordability Gap)
entries.desc: This report examines the affordability gap that low-income students face at public colleges across all states, finding that after accounting for grants, scholarships, and part-time work, students from low-income backgrounds still face thousands of dollars in uncovered annual costs. In nearly every state the affordability gap at four-year institutions exceeds $3,000, with the national average around $6,500.

entries.title: The Future of Statewide Summative Assessments
entries.desc: EdTrust identifies statewide summative assessments as an essential tool to achieve education equity by providing comparable data across students and schools. The organization presents four equity pillars for improving assessments — ensuring consistent expectations, encouraging inclusive assessments, providing timely results, and making results meaningful — offering advocates and policymakers recommendations for enhancing assessment systems to better serve students, families, and educators.

entries.title: Parent PLUS Loans Are a Double-Edged Sword for Black Borrowers
entries.desc: This brief examines how Black parents increasingly rely on Parent PLUS loans to fund their children's college education, despite these loans carrying higher interest rates and less favorable terms than federal student loans. The report reveals that structural racism, including racial wage and wealth gaps, forces Black families to borrow heavily, and that Black parent borrowers struggle significantly with repayment — often deferring payments and drawing from retirement accounts at higher rates than White borrowers, creating a multigenerational toll of debt.

entries.title: Income-Driven Repayment Plans Fail Black Borrowers
entries.desc: Drawing on qualitative data from EdTrust's National Black Student Debt Study, this brief examines how federal income-driven repayment plans are ineffective at reducing debt for Black borrowers. The authors argue that even recent administrative improvements do not address underlying college affordability issues or help future students, and recommend broad debt cancellation plus creation of a new repayment plan with more favorable terms.

entries.title: For Student Parents, The Biggest Hurdles to a Higher Education Are Costs and Finding Child Care
entries.desc: This report examines the financial barriers facing student parents, finding that there is no state in which a student parent can work 10 hours per week at minimum wage and afford both tuition and child care at a public college. Student parents face out-of-pocket costs that are 2 to 5 times higher than peers without children, requiring an average of 52 hours of weekly work per week to cover expenses.

entries.title: Building a Diverse & Strong Educator Workforce for Students
entries.desc: This resource highlights a significant disparity in educator demographics: students of color make up more than 50% of the K-12 public school student population while teachers of color represent less than 20% of educators. Students from underserved backgrounds are four times more likely to attend schools with larger percentages of uncertified and inexperienced teachers, contributing to disparities in discipline rates, graduation outcomes, and educational access.

entries.title: Access Granted: School Funding Between Schools in Districts
entries.desc: EdTrust analyzed newly available school-by-school spending data required by the Every Student Succeeds Act to examine funding equity within districts. The analysis reveals that in 97% of districts included, state and local spending was flat or regressive for all student groups examined, with very few students of color, low-income students, or English learners attending schools where funding is allocated to meet their additional needs.

entries.title: Reassessing ESSA Implementation: An Equity Analysis of School Accountability Systems
entries.desc: EdTrust examined how states implement federal school accountability requirements under the Every Student Succeeds Act, finding that states have a mixed record of centering equity in school ratings. The analysis identifies significant gaps in how states identify low-performing schools and support improvement efforts, and reveals that while some states show promising practices, most have failed to connect school identification processes to robust support systems that would meaningfully improve outcomes for historically underserved students.

entries.title: Jim Crow Debt: How Black Borrowers Experience Student Loans
entries.desc: This report presents findings from the National Black Student Loan Debt Study, based on a survey of nearly 1,300 Black borrowers and interviews with 100 participants. Key findings reveal that Black borrowers view student loans as problematic rather than beneficial, experience income-driven repayment plans as lifetime debt sentences, and overwhelmingly support complete government debt forgiveness as a racial justice solution, with the research connecting these outcomes to systemic racism and labor market stratification.

entries.title: Creating Positive College Campus Racial Climates for Students of Color
entries.desc: EdTrust interviewed 32 Black, Latino, and Asian American students at predominantly White institutions to understand how campus racial climate affects their experiences. The research identified key challenges including underrepresentation of students and faculty of color, limited institutional support, unclear reporting procedures for discrimination incidents, and insufficient mental health resources tailored to students of color.

entries.title: How Student Debt Harms Black Borrowers' Mental Health
entries.desc: Drawing on the National Black Student Debt Study, this brief examines the intersection of student debt and mental health among Black borrowers, finding that 64% of survey participants reported that student debt negatively impacted their mental health. The report documents how Black borrowers face disproportionate challenges with student loans due to structural racism and fewer generational resources, establishing that the psychological toll of debt can be as severe as its financial consequences.

entries.title: How Black Women Experience Student Debt
entries.desc: This brief examines how Black women are disproportionately affected by the student debt crisis due to intersecting discrimination and systemic inequities. Using federal data and the National Black Student Debt Study, the report demonstrates that Black women borrow more for college while earning less, making repayment significantly more challenging than for other groups.

entries.title: Making College Affordable
entries.desc: EdTrust advocates for reducing college costs and student debt through policy initiatives focused on highlighting affordability gaps in higher education, promoting free college programs, doubling Pell Grants, and addressing the disproportionate student debt burden faced by Black borrowers. The organization's goal is enabling students to earn degrees without it turning into a debt sentence, and this hub page links to related research, data tools, and advocacy resources on college affordability.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Reading Partners
entries.desc: Reading Partners is a one-to-one tutoring program delivered by trained community volunteers who work with struggling readers in grades 2–5 for two 45-minute sessions per week. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one MDRC randomized controlled trial (n=1,166) showing students gained significantly more on SAT-10 comprehension and other outcomes (effect size +0.10) compared to peers, two-thirds of whom received alternative literacy services.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Success for All
entries.desc: Success for All is a comprehensive K–5 school reform program combining phonics instruction, cooperative learning, small-group tutoring, and frequent regrouping for high-poverty schools. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on six U.S. studies (n=8,849 students across 207 schools) including two RCTs, with an average effect size of +0.29 and particularly robust effects on foundational reading skills and for lower-achieving students.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Reading Recovery
entries.desc: Reading Recovery targets first-graders in the lowest 20–30% of their cohort, delivering daily 30-minute one-to-one lessons from specially trained teachers covering fluency, decoding, phonemic awareness, and guided reading. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on four qualifying studies (n=7,756 students) with an average effect size of +0.43 on standardized reading assessments including ITBS, Woodcock, and Gates measures.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) — Reading
entries.desc: PALS is a peer-tutoring approach where first-grade student pairs alternate teacher and learner roles, working through a structured sequence of phonemic awareness, phonics, sound blending, and passage reading with error correction. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (n=1,501 students) with an average effect size of +0.23 on Woodcock reading scales.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Enhanced Core Reading Instruction (ECRI)
entries.desc: ECRI enhances Tier 2 supplemental reading instruction for struggling first graders by providing teacher professional development and small-group sessions (3–5 students) targeting phonemic awareness, decoding, fluency, and irregular word reading. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one study (n=811 students in Oregon and Massachusetts) with an average effect size of +0.24 on Woodcock-Johnson and SAT-10 measures.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Reading Corps
entries.desc: Reading Corps is an AmeriCorps program that places trained literacy tutors in K–3 classrooms to deliver 20-minute daily supplemental instruction using research-based protocols. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=798 students in Minnesota and Wisconsin) with an average effect size of +0.36, with particularly strong gains in Letter Sound Fluency (+0.74) and Nonsense Word Fluency (+0.62) for K–1 students.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Targeted Reading Instruction (TRI)
entries.desc: TRI is a one-to-one tutoring intervention that uses webcam technology to train educators to deliver 15-minute daily individualized reading sessions for struggling K–1 readers, covering oral language, decoding, writing, comprehension, vocabulary, and fluency. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two studies (n=471 students) with an average effect size of +0.51 on letter-word identification, word attack, and passage comprehension measures.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Sound Partners — Struggling Readers
entries.desc: Sound Partners is a one-on-one paraprofessional tutoring intervention for kindergarten and first-grade struggling readers, providing 30 minutes of daily instruction four days per week for 18–20 weeks in phonics, phonemic awareness, sight words, and decodable text reading. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two qualifying studies (n=488 students) with an average effect size of +0.58, with sustained gains confirmed at two-year follow-up assessments.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: QuickReads — Struggling Readers
entries.desc: QuickReads is a supplemental tutoring program for struggling readers in grades 2–5 that pairs students for 30-minute daily sessions four days per week over approximately 20 weeks, emphasizing repeated reading, phonics, and comprehension. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (n=483 students) with an average effect size of +0.21 on GORT and Woodcock reading assessments.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Air Reading
entries.desc: Air Reading is a virtual tutoring program for grades 1–6 that delivers explicit, skills-based reading instruction in small groups of up to four students meeting four times weekly for 30 minutes, with consistent tutors using diagnostic assessment to guide individualized instruction. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=740 students in Texas and Louisiana) with a weighted average effect size of +0.20 across NWEA MAP, DIBELS, LEAP, and STAAR assessments.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Building Assets Reducing Risks (BARR) — Reading
entries.desc: BARR is a training and coaching model for K–12 schools built around structured teacher team meetings, data-driven student support, community engagement, and professional development to improve student outcomes. Evidence for ESSA rates the reading component Strong based on three randomized studies (n=9,398 students) with an average effect size of +0.08, with stronger impacts of +0.21 for struggling readers in high school settings.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Intelligent Tutoring for the Structure Strategy (ITSS)
entries.desc: ITSS is a web-based intelligent tutoring system that teaches nonfiction text comprehension through text structure categorization, delivered in 30–45-minute weekly sessions in language arts classes with paraprofessional support. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one RCT (n=2,489 seventh graders across 108 classrooms in 25 rural/suburban schools) with an effect size of +0.18 on the Gray Silent Reading Test after 6–7 months of use.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Zearn Math
entries.desc: Zearn Math is a nonprofit adaptive online supplement for K–8 math instruction featuring guided video lessons using a Concrete-to-Pictorial-to-Abstract progression with mastery-based quizzes. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (including one RCT with 10,000+ students in Texas) with an average effect size of +0.09 on NWEA MAP assessments, with strongest results when schools received dedicated implementation support including professional development and coaching.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: ASSISTments
entries.desc: ASSISTments is an online homework tool for grades 7–8 that provides students with math assistance during practice while giving teachers real-time assessment data on student progress. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (a Maine study and a North Carolina replication with ~6,000 students) with a combined mean effect size of +0.13 on mathematics assessments.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: DreamBox Math
entries.desc: DreamBox Math is an adaptive game-based online platform that delivers personalized, standards-aligned K–8 math instruction as a supplement to classroom teaching. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=13,320 students in California and North Carolina) with an average effect size of +0.10 on the Number Knowledge Test and state assessments, with strongest impacts for K–2 students.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: ST Math (Spatial Temporal Math)
entries.desc: ST Math is a supplemental visual problem-solving program for grades 3–6 built on the science of how the brain learns, using animation and game-based tasks to develop mathematical understanding without language barriers. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on four studies (n=346,248 students) including two cluster-randomized trials in high-poverty schools, with an average effect size of +0.07 on state math assessments.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Math in Focus
entries.desc: Math in Focus adapts Singapore's "My Pals Are Here! Maths" curriculum for grades 2–5 using a concrete-to-pictorial-to-abstract progression in carefully scaffolded lessons, supported by extensive professional development including summer training and four full-day sessions during the school year. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on four qualifying studies (n=3,444 students) with an average effect size of +0.15 on standardized math measures.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: IXL Math
entries.desc: IXL Math is a comprehensive personalized teaching and learning platform featuring adaptive skills practice aligned to state standards, video tutorials, diagnostic assessments, and analytics for monitoring progress at district, school, classroom, and individual levels. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one cluster RCT (n=545 elementary students in Michigan) with an effect size of +0.13 on Renaissance Star Math, with particularly strong impacts for Grade 3, Hispanic, special education, and English learner subgroups.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Saga Math
entries.desc: Saga Math is a high-dosage tutoring intervention for grades 9–10 offering traditional (1:2–1:4) or technology-enhanced formats (1:8 alternating direct tutoring with ALEKS computer-assisted learning), delivered three times weekly for 90 minutes total to supplement regular math instruction. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (n=5,396+ predominantly Black and Latinx students in Chicago) with an average effect size of +0.14, plus increased math GPAs, reduced course failures, and persistent gains one to two years post-intervention.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Math Corps
entries.desc: Math Corps is a tutoring intervention for grades 4–8 where full-time AmeriCorps tutors provide approximately 25 students with 90 minutes of weekly intervention support in small sessions targeting foundational whole and rational number skills and algebraic reasoning. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=1,164 students) with an average effect size of +0.16 on STAR Math and state assessments.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: OGAP Math Professional Learning System
entries.desc: OGAP Math is a professional learning system for grades 3–5 teachers that develops mathematics content knowledge and formative assessment skills using research-based learning progressions, enabling teachers to analyze student work and identify strategies and misconceptions in real time. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one two-year RCT (n=3,488 students across 61 urban schools near Philadelphia) with an effect size of +0.20 on Pennsylvania state math assessments.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Graspable Math — From Here to There (FH2T)
entries.desc: From Here to There (FH2T) is a digital math game for grade 7 in which students strengthen algebraic skills by dragging and transforming expressions using valid mathematical operations, practicing distribution, factoring, inverse operations, and order of operations. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one study (n=1,119 seventh graders across 10 suburban middle schools) with an effect size of +0.14 on an algebraic understanding assessment after nine 30-minute sessions.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: My Math Academy
entries.desc: My Math Academy is a game-based adaptive learning system for pre-K through 2nd grade featuring 30 games with 130+ activities, adaptive scaffolding, embedded assessment, and narrative-driven characters to build foundational number sense and operations. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two studies (n=1,209 students in Texas and Southern California) with an average effect size of +0.27 on CIRCLE and TEMA-3 math assessments.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Whole Number Foundations (Roots)
entries.desc: Whole Number Foundations is a Tier 2 supplemental intervention for kindergarten and first graders delivered by paraprofessionals in small groups of 1–5, with kindergarteners receiving 50 twenty-minute daily lessons and first graders receiving 60 sessions three to five times weekly targeting procedural fluency and conceptual understanding. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on five studies (n=2,140 students across Oregon, Massachusetts, Texas, and Boston) with an average effect size of +0.19 on SESAT and TEMA-3 measures.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Number Rockets
entries.desc: Number Rockets is a scripted small-group tutoring intervention for first graders delivered by retired and substitute teachers in groups of 2–3 students for 40 minutes daily over 17 weeks, progressing from concrete to abstract mathematical tasks to build foundational number sense. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one study (n=994 students below the 38th percentile) with an effect size of +0.34 on TEMA-3 assessments.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Fraction Face-Off!
entries.desc: Fraction Face-Off! is a small-group tutoring intervention for at-risk fourth graders that delivers 35-minute daily sports-themed sessions over 12 weeks (36 days) focused on comparing, ordering, placing on number lines, and understanding equivalencies of fractions. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two studies (n=425 students) with an average effect size of +0.51 on NAEP-derived fraction assessments.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Building Assets Reducing Risks (BARR) — Math
entries.desc: BARR is a training and coaching model for K–12 schools that structures teacher team meetings around student relationship data and progress, supplemented by community engagement sessions and professional development, to improve academic outcomes. Evidence for ESSA rates the math component Strong based on three RCTs (n=9,386 students) with an average effect size of +0.09 on NWEA MAP and PSAT/NMSQT math assessments, with a +0.33 effect in one high school cohort.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Second Step — Social-Emotional Learning
entries.desc: Second Step is a classroom-based SEL curriculum for K–7 that delivers 22–26 weekly lessons per year targeting social-emotional competence and self-regulation, with differentiated content for elementary (K–5) and middle school (6–7) students. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong in academic and emotional well-being categories based on six qualifying studies (n up to 7,213 elementary and 3,705 middle school students) showing effect sizes of +0.10–+0.11 on learning skills, emotional problems, and hyperactivity measures.

entries.title: Evidence for ESSA: Facing History and Ourselves
entries.desc: Facing History and Ourselves provides teacher professional development that uses historical case studies addressing racism, prejudice, and antisemitism to promote students' humane and informed citizenship in grades 7–10. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong in academic and social relationships categories based on three studies including one large RCT across 62 high schools showing effect sizes of +0.14 to +0.23 on historical understanding, tolerance, civic efficacy, civic engagement, and prosocial behaviors.

entries.title: Principled Transformers for Predictive Performance in Knowledge Tracing
entries.desc: Introduces Knowledge Tracing Set Transformers (KTSTs), which apply transformer architecture with learnable ALiBi attention and set-based representations of learner interactions to forecast student capabilities over time. Evaluated on real educational datasets and simulated learning data, KTSTs establish improved benchmarks and are proposed as a simple but effective base model class for future knowledge tracing research.

entries.title: Enhancing the De-identification of Personally Identifiable Information in Educational Data
entries.desc: Investigates GPT-4o-mini fine-tuning versus prompting for detecting personally identifiable information in educational text, benchmarking against Microsoft Presidio and Azure AI Language. The fine-tuned model achieved 0.9589 recall on the CRAPII dataset while reducing cost to roughly one-tenth of competing solutions, with consistent performance across demographic groups.

entries.title: Deep Learning vs. Bayesian Knowledge Tracing: Student Models for Interventions
entries.desc: Compares Bayesian Knowledge Tracing, Intervention-BKT, and LSTM models on two prediction tasks using data from two intelligent tutoring systems. BKT variants outperformed LSTM on post-test score prediction, while LSTM excelled at predicting learning gains; the study also demonstrates that BKT can forecast post-test performance using only the earliest half of a student's training sequence.

entries.title: The Continuous Hint Factory - Providing Hints in Vast and Sparsely Populated Edit Distance Spaces
entries.desc: Proposes the Continuous Hint Factory, an extension of the Hint Factory approach that generates next-step hints in sparse state spaces by averaging weighted reference states from past students rather than requiring exact matches. The framework applies Gaussian processes and edit distance theory, demonstrating improved accuracy in predicting student actions on open-ended programming tasks.

entries.title: dAFM: Fusing Psychometric and Connectionist Modeling for Q-matrix Refinement
entries.desc: Presents dAFM, a model that merges psychometric Additive Factors Modeling with neural network backpropagation to refine Q-matrices—mappings between assessment items and knowledge components. Evaluated on Cognitive Tutor and ASSISTments datasets, models that refined expert-defined Q-matrices outperformed those learning from scratch, showing that combining expert knowledge with data-driven refinement improves generalization.

entries.title: Decision Tree Modeling of Wheel-Spinning and Productive Persistence in Skill Builders
entries.desc: Uses decision tree classification on ASSISTments data to distinguish unproductive wheel-spinning from productive persistence in online math practice. The study identifies two student subgroups engaging in counterproductive help-seeking patterns and finds that spaced practice and limiting bottom-out hint use can reduce unproductive struggle and improve learning outcomes.

entries.title: Who's Learning? Using Demographics in EDM Research
entries.desc: Surveys five years of EDM publications to examine how demographic variables—age, gender, race/ethnicity, SES, and geography—are reported and incorporated into analyses. Only 15% of studies actually used demographic data in models; the authors contextualize this within broader algorithmic bias concerns and call for more equitable and transparent reporting practices in educational data mining.

entries.title: When is Deep Learning the Best Approach to Knowledge Tracing?
entries.desc: Empirically compares logistic regression, deep learning, and Markov-based approaches to knowledge tracing across nine real-world datasets. Logistic regression with well-engineered features leads on moderate-sized datasets, while deep learning excels on large datasets where precise temporal information matters; the study also evaluates model calibration, an important consideration for real deployment.

entries.title: Early Detection of Students at Risk - Predicting Student Dropouts Using Administrative Student Data from German Universities
entries.desc: Develops an early-warning system combining regression, neural networks, and decision trees via AdaBoost to predict student dropout from administrative records at two German universities. The model achieved 79–85% accuracy after one semester and 90–95% by the fourth semester, enabling institutions to target at-risk students with support before withdrawal becomes likely.

entries.title: Using Demographic Data as Predictor Variables: a Questionable Choice
entries.desc: Examines the ethical and practical implications of including demographic variables as direct predictors in educational prediction models. The authors argue that using demographics as predictors can reinforce biases and reduce model utility, concluding that demographic variables yield greater fairness benefit when used to validate model equity rather than as predictive features.

entries.title: Using Auxiliary Data to Boost Precision in the Analysis of A/B Tests on an Online Educational Platform
entries.desc: Demonstrates how incorporating historical user data from machine learning models into A/B test analysis on ASSISTments can substantially improve precision without sacrificing statistical validity. Tested across 250+ randomized comparisons, the method provides precision gains equivalent to increasing sample size by 20% on average—and up to 50–80% in some cases—with additional benefits for subgroup analyses.

entries.title: Tracing Systematic Errors to Personalize Recommendations in Single Digit Multiplication and Beyond
entries.desc: Presents the Systematic Error Tracing (SET) model, which uses a bigraph linking errors to underlying causes—inspired by Ising model physics—to identify persistent mistake patterns in elementary multiplication. Applied to 25,000+ incorrect responses from 335 students on the Math Garden platform, SET outperforms baseline approaches for generating personalized learning recommendations.

entries.title: Using a Latent Class Forest to Identify At-Risk Students in Higher Education
entries.desc: Introduces a latent class forest method—combining latent class analysis with random forest ensembles—to identify subgroups of academically vulnerable students in a large-enrollment introductory psychology course. The approach reveals key demographic and academic characteristics of at-risk students and evaluates how a peer-led Supplemental Instruction program performs across identified subgroups, enabling more targeted recruitment and support.

entries.title: Statistical Consequences of using Multi-armed Bandits to Conduct Adaptive Educational Experiments
entries.desc: Uses simulation studies to investigate how multi-armed bandit (MAB) algorithms affect the statistical properties of adaptive educational experiments. MAB designs substantially improve student outcomes during an experiment but require roughly double the participants to maintain statistical validity and can increase false positive rates when student characteristics vary over time, highlighting a tradeoff between immediate student benefit and rigorous causal inference.

entries.title: Analyzing Student Process Data in Game-Based Assessments with Bayesian Knowledge Tracing and Dynamic Bayesian Networks
entries.desc: Compares Bayesian Knowledge Tracing and Dynamic Bayesian Networks for analyzing student interaction data from Raging Skies, a game-based science assessment aligned to Canadian Grade 5 curriculum. The study evaluates both models on classification consistency and accuracy, contributing evidence on the viability of probabilistic knowledge models for formative game-based assessment.

entries.title: Extending the Hint Factory for the Assistance Dilemma: A Novel, Data-driven HelpNeed Predictor for Proactive Problem-solving Help
entries.desc: Extends the Hint Factory methodology to address the assistance dilemma by creating a data-driven HelpNeed classifier that identifies when students are on unproductive problem-solving paths in logic-based tasks. A controlled experiment showed that students receiving adaptive hints based on HelpNeed predictions achieved superior posttest outcomes, reduced help avoidance, and completed optimal solutions more efficiently than controls.

entries.title: Adjusting the L Statistic when Self-Transitions are Excluded in Affect Dynamics
entries.desc: Identifies a systematic bias in the L statistic—a standard metric for measuring transitions between student affective states—when self-transitions are excluded from data. The authors propose L*, a corrected version that restores the chance value to zero, and validate it on both simulated affective sequences and real student data from digital learning environments.

entries.title: Effects of Scenario-Based Assessment on Students' Writing Processes
entries.desc: Uses keystroke log analysis with a semi-Markov model to examine how scenario-based assessment design conditions—varying number of scenarios and task order—affect students' writing processes. A single-scenario with essay-last format produced fewer but longer editing phases and more fluid text generation, with findings grounded in cognitive writing theory and working memory constraints.

entries.title: Optimizing Financial Aid Allocation to Improve Access and Affordability to Higher Education
entries.desc: Combines optimization algorithms with machine learning to guide merit and need-based financial aid allocation with student-centered affordability goals, moving beyond enrollment-focused institutional strategies. Analysis of admissions data identified seven budget-conscious allocation strategies capable of expanding enrollment access by over 100% while constraining budget growth below 7%.

entries.title: Examining Algorithmic Fairness for First-Term College Grade Prediction Models Relying on Pre-matriculation Data
entries.desc: Builds a GPA forecasting model for first-semester students using only pre-college demographic and academic data, finding the model is less predictive for at-risk students than for majority counterparts. The study concludes that deploying prediction systems too early—before students have accumulated in-college behavioral data—may systematically disadvantage the very students who most need early support.

entries.title: Multi-Dimensional Performance Analysis of Large Language Models for Classroom Discussion Assessment
entries.desc: Evaluates LLM performance on scoring classroom discussion quality, examining how task formulation, context length, and few-shot examples affect accuracy, consistency, and computational efficiency. Using data from a randomized controlled trial, the best-performing LLM methods partially replicated human-scored results and successfully detected improvements in discussion quality over time.

entries.title: Investigating Demographic Features and their Connection to Performance, Predictions, and Fairness in EDM Models
entries.desc: Tests whether including demographic features actually improves predictive accuracy in four at-risk student datasets, finding that removing demographics rarely decreases performance but models still assign weight to them when available. The authors argue that accurate prediction is not always the right goal and advocate for causal understanding and context-specific evaluation of demographic features over blanket inclusion or exclusion.

entries.title: A Scalable, Flexible, and Interpretable Analytic Pipeline for Stealth Assessment in Complex Digital Game-Based Learning Environments
entries.desc: Proposes a stealth assessment pipeline combining machine learning with expert-designed features to evaluate multiple learning outcomes in Mission HydroSci, a 3D narrative educational game. The framework captures complex in-game behaviors, uses unsupervised dimensionality reduction, and applies Accumulated Local Effects visualizations for interpretability; code and sample datasets are publicly released to support generalizability.

entries.title: Automated Feedback Generation for Student Project Reports: A Data-Driven Approach
entries.desc: Introduces Insta-Reviewer, a system that automatically generates instant feedback on student project reports using data-driven methods, filling a gap since prior automated feedback work focused only on simpler student work. A five-dimension evaluation framework shows that Insta-Reviewer achieves near-human quality feedback, though the authors also document obstacles remaining for practical deployment.

entries.title: Optimizing Bayesian Knowledge Tracing with Neural Network Parameter Generation
entries.desc: Proposes OptimNN and BKTransformer—two neural network approaches using hypernetworks and transformers respectively—to generate Bayesian Knowledge Tracing parameters that avoid suboptimal local minima. Both methods outperform existing BKT and deep knowledge tracing baselines while preserving BKT's interpretable skill-level estimates and student mastery probability outputs; implementation and datasets are publicly available.

entries.title: CLST: Cold-Start Mitigation in Knowledge Tracing by Aligning a Generative Language Model as a Students' Knowledge Tracer
entries.desc: Addresses the cold-start problem in knowledge tracing by reformulating it as an NLP task and fine-tuning a generative LLM on formatted problem-solving datasets from math, social studies, and science. The framework achieves significantly enhanced prediction, reliability, and cross-domain generalization with fewer than 100 student records, demonstrating how LLM external knowledge can substitute for sparse interaction histories.

entries.title: Developing a Feedback Taxonomy for Math: A Synergy of Perspectives through Data Mining Methods
entries.desc: Develops an empirically grounded taxonomy for categorizing educator feedback on student math responses, using data mining to reconcile varying interpretations from both teaching professionals and researchers on an online math platform. The resulting shared language across stakeholders is designed to support scalable delivery of pedagogically meaningful feedback at scale.

entries.title: Workload Overload? Late Enrollment Leads to Course Dropout
entries.desc: Uses cross-lagged panel models on enrollment transaction data to establish a causal link between late course registration and subsequent course withdrawal, estimating the effect at approximately 0.8 standard deviations of additional drops per SD of delayed enrollment. About 17% of students enroll late and carry higher course loads, pointing to early planning interventions as a practical dropout-prevention strategy.

entries.title: Using LLMs to Identify Indicators of Persistence from Students' Dialogues with a Pedagogical Agent
entries.desc: Investigates using ChatGPT4o with varying temperature settings to automatically code self-regulation indicators—including help-seeking, goal-setting, and self-efficacy—from over 10,000 chat turns by 107 English learner middle schoolers in a conversation-based math assessment. Self-efficacy showed the strongest human-LLM agreement; the study recommends deterministic settings for well-defined constructs and higher temperatures for theoretically ambiguous ones.

entries.title: Exploring Fairness and Explainability in LLM-Generated Support for Online Learning Discussion Forums
entries.desc: Investigates how LLMs handle fairness and transparency when generating replies in online MOOC discussion forums, fine-tuning GPT-2, Gemma, and LLaMA on both standard and counterfactual datasets. The study introduces absolute distributional sentiment divergence to quantify demographic bias, finding that LLM-generated responses are generally more neutral than human replies but show varying bias patterns by gender. Counterfactual fine-tuning reduced bias and produced more balanced outputs, contributing practical methods for equitable AI support tools in educational environments.

entries.title: Towards More Transparency in Learning Analytics: Sharing Information with University Students Increases Their Awareness of Data Collection Practices
entries.desc: Tests three data disclosure formats — generic text, detailed text, and an icon-based nutrition-label approach — to measure their effect on student understanding of learning analytics practices. Non-generic disclosures improved comprehension, though student acceptance of data collection and beliefs about sharing were unaffected by any disclosure format. The author concludes that institutions should revisit their data disclosure methods to ensure both accuracy and genuine transparency.

entries.title: Balancing Act: Early, Fair, and Accurate Identification of At-Risk Students
entries.desc: Develops fairness-aware machine learning models for identifying struggling students in Advanced Placement statistics, tested at three sequential course stages. By incorporating learning activity data and excluding sensitive demographic features, the models effectively identified at-risk learners at Stage 1 while maintaining equity across demographic groups. The authors evaluate multiple fairness mitigation techniques, offering practical recommendations for equitable early-warning systems in educational settings.

entries.title: Use of Learning Analytics in K–12 Mathematics Education: Systematic Scoping Review of the Impact on Teaching and Learning
entries.desc: Systematic scoping review of 19 studies on learning analytics in K–12 mathematics, finding the field is emerging with applications across diverse curricula. Teaching approaches using analytics-informed data-based decision-making concentrate primarily on supervision and guidance, with generally favorable outcomes for student achievement and notably stronger benefits for higher-performing students. The authors introduce the concept of "guiding analytics for learners" as a framework for leveraging analytics to foster self-directed learning.

entries.title: FATE in MMLA: A Student-Centred Exploration of Fairness, Accountability, Transparency, and Ethics in Multimodal Learning Analytics
entries.desc: Interviews 14 undergraduates who used multimodal learning analytics (MMLA) visualizations to reflect on collaborative learning activities, examining student perspectives on fairness, accountability, transparency, and ethics. Students emphasized that accurate and comprehensive data representation is essential for visualization fairness, and called for varied data access levels and continuous rather than one-time consent mechanisms. The authors conclude that the learning analytics community must systematically prioritize FATE concerns in system design.

entries.title: Words of Wisdom: A Journey through the Realm of NLP for Learning Analytics — A Systematic Literature Review
entries.desc: Analyzes 156 learning analytics papers applying natural language processing to educational contexts, finding that most work focuses on online discussion analysis and essay assessment. Key patterns: developed models are rarely tested in real settings, recent studies increasingly evaluate deep learning approaches such as BERT, datasets tend to be small and English-language, and average model performance reaches 0.54–0.79 on Cohen's Kappa and Accuracy. The review maps how NLP techniques extract insights from student essays, online discussions, and chat interactions.

entries.title: Student Privacy and Learning Analytics: Investigating the Application of Privacy Within a Student Success Information System in Higher Education
entries.desc: Examines how privacy is operationalized in a commercial learning analytics system at a small public higher education institution using inductive coding of interviews, documents, and videos. The findings reveal an institution in the early stages of implementing such a system, with privacy handled reactively rather than proactively. The authors offer recommendations for institutions to adopt more proactive privacy measures when designing learning analytics policies.

entries.title: Effectiveness of a Learning Analytics Dashboard for Increasing Student Engagement Levels
entries.desc: Evaluates SensEnablr, a learning analytics dashboard integrating descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive analytics, finding that student engagement with learning technologies and course resources increased significantly following dashboard interactions. The tool elevated learner motivation and students valued predictive and prescriptive insights, addressing a gap in dashboards that typically provide only basic descriptive data. The study demonstrates that multi-analytic platforms can meaningfully impact student outcomes in tertiary online education.

entries.title: Privacy in LA Research: Understanding the Field to Improve the Practice
entries.desc: Scoping review of 59 learning analytics articles examining how privacy is conceptualized, finding that privacy was not defined at all in 74% of studies. Among the minority that did define it, frameworks included privacy as a right, state, control, or other approaches. The authors argue that poorly implemented privacy frameworks may undermine trust and call for the field to establish clearer privacy definitions to enable responsible practices around student data.

entries.title: Causal Inference and Bias in Learning Analytics: A Primer on Pitfalls Using Directed Acyclic Graphs
entries.desc: Introduces directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) as a practical tool for validating causal reasoning in learning analytics research, identifying three foundational pitfalls: confounding bias, overcontrol bias, and collider bias. The authors demonstrate how these methodological errors appear in published studies and propose DAG-based solutions for researchers working with observational data where randomized experiments are impractical. The paper emphasizes the importance of theoretical development alongside methodological rigor in the field.

entries.title: A Participatory Approach to Designing a Student-Facing Dashboard for Online and Distance Education
entries.desc: Engages 20 distance-learning students through screening questionnaires and focus groups to design student-facing analytics dashboards, finding students prioritize course support and communication with tutors and peers. Students valued descriptive metrics, predictive score forecasting, and prescriptive resource recommendations, with the study emphasizing that effective dashboards should foremost prescribe solutions to overcome performance challenges. The work demonstrates that participatory design approaches are essential for creating analytics tools that address genuine student needs.

entries.title: Unveiling Accuracy-Fairness Trade-Offs: Investigating Machine Learning Models in Student Performance Prediction
entries.desc: Analyzes the Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (OULAD) to evaluate accuracy-fairness trade-offs in machine learning models for student performance prediction. Standard ML models exhibit demographic bias, but applying bias mitigation techniques can reduce these disparities while maintaining acceptable accuracy. The authors conclude that auditing machine learning models for equitable performance across diverse student populations is essential before deployment.

entries.title: The Coming but Uneven Storm: How AI Regulation Will Impact AI and Learning Analytics Research in Different Countries
entries.desc: Analyzes 11 AI regulatory documents across geopolitical regions using the OECD classification system, finding that the EU and China have implemented the most comprehensive and strict AI regulations while the US shifted toward economic competition and reduced regulation. Regulatory priorities differ by region: the EU, UK, and China prioritize safety and bias prevention, while Japan and some African nations focus on economic potential; biometric data in educational contexts faces strict EU restrictions. The authors recommend researchers remain adaptable to evolving international regulations and use regulatory sandboxes to balance learner protection with developer flexibility.

entries.title: Deliberative Interactions for Socially Shared Regulation in Collaborative Learning: An AI-Driven Learning Analytics Study
entries.desc: Analyzes 2,125 utterances from video-recorded collaborative tasks involving 30 Finnish secondary students to examine socially shared regulation patterns. Two distinct deliberation patterns emerged: a Plan and Implementation Approach associated with adaptive group regulation, and a Trials and Failure Approach linked to maladaptive patterns, with many groups failing to recognize or respond to emerging regulatory needs. The study combines epistemic network analysis with multimodal data and AI-assisted coding, advancing understanding of how groups manage cognitive and emotional regulation during collaborative problem-solving.

entries.title: Uncovering Engagement Profiles of Young Learners in K–8 Education through Learning Analytics
entries.desc: Applies k-means cluster analysis to e-learning trace data from grades 1–8 to identify seven distinct student engagement profiles, finding that some profiles were absent at specific grades, indicating shifting engagement behaviors across development. Younger students showed stronger interest in game-based learning components while middle school students prioritized exam preparation, and most students focused on particular platform features rather than using all available resources.
