The authors present five policy and practice recommendations based on their findings.

entries.title: Parsing Coaching Practice: A Systematic Framework for Describing Coaching Discourse
entries.desc: Addresses a significant gap in the coaching literature by developing a taxonomy of coaching "moves" that breaks down specific discourse strategies used by professional learning coaches. The research identifies two key problems: limited comparative evidence about coaching effectiveness, and the field's tendency to focus on abstract concepts like trust-building rather than concrete communication techniques. The proposed taxonomy offers practitioners a shared vocabulary for understanding coach discourse patterns, potentially supporting future investigations into how coaching interactions influence teacher development.

entries.title: Teaching Teachers to Use Computer Assisted Learning Effectively: Experimental and Quasi-Experimental Evidence
entries.desc: Examines a program providing weekly coaching to help educators implement computer-assisted learning for customized student progress in elementary and middle school mathematics. Two field experiments showed significant gains of 0.12-0.22 standard deviations in math performance. The research reveals that meaningful improvements occurred when students received at least 35 minutes of weekly practice, with success factors including strong teacher buy-in, clear implementation timing, and consistent monitoring of student progress with targeted intervention for struggling learners.

entries.title: The Promises and Pitfalls of Using Language Models to Measure Instruction Quality in Education
entries.desc: Examines how NLP techniques can assess instructional practices across K-12 classrooms and pre-service teacher settings. Pretrained language models performed comparably to human raters when evaluating simpler, discrete teaching behaviors but struggled with more complex practices requiring higher-level inference. Notably, analyzing only teacher speech yielded strong results for measuring student-centered approaches, reducing concerns about obtaining quality student speech transcriptions.

entries.title: Using a Multi-Agent System and Evidence-Centered Design to Integrate Educator Expertise Within Generated Feedback
entries.desc: Proposes a framework for using generative AI to facilitate mathematics teacher learning through automated rehearsals where educators practice teaching skills, obtain feedback, and enhance their methods. The approach has three components: leveraging evidence-centered design modeling to create templates capturing mathematics teaching complexity, converting expertise from these templates into GenAI prompts, and employing a multi-agent system that distributes this expertise across agents to maintain sophistication while simulating rehearsals and delivering feedback.

entries.title: UpGrade Resource Center
entries.desc: Resource center for the UpGrade evidence-based testing platform, providing insights, updates, and source code. Offers research papers on A/B testing in educational settings, access to open-source code via GitHub, technical documentation, instructional guides including demo videos, and blog posts covering platform developments and funding opportunities for educational research.

entries.title: New Study Finds Every Minute Spent on Khan Academy Can Lead to Learning Gains
entries.desc: Khan Academy researchers conducted a within-subjects study tracking 100,000 students across two school years and found that increased platform usage correlated with improved MAP Growth test scores. The study design compared each student to themselves over time, eliminating confounding factors like motivation. The findings demonstrate that even modest increases in Khan Academy usage translate to measurable learning gains across different grades and demographics.

entries.title: Three Research-Backed Strategies Teachers Can Implement on Khan Academy to Boost Student Learning Outcomes
entries.desc: Identifies three effective teaching strategies from Khan Academy research: prioritizing Course Mastery Goals to ensure skill development with minimal teacher effort, using supplemental assignments sparingly to address specific gaps rather than duplicating content, and maintaining a regular usage pattern for consistent student success both on the platform and on external assessments.

entries.title: University of Toronto Randomized Controlled Trial of 11K Students Demonstrates a Meaningful Positive Effect of Khan Academy on Student Learning
entries.desc: A University of Toronto randomized controlled trial involving 11,000 students found that elementary students (grades 3-6) who used Khan Academy for approximately 35 minutes weekly showed 0.12 to 0.17 standard deviations higher math assessment scores. The study demonstrated meaningful learning improvements when teachers prioritized consistent platform usage, though middle school students saw fewer gains due to lower engagement levels.

entries.title: Why Khan Academy Will Be Using "Skills to Proficient" to Measure Learning Outcomes
entries.desc: Khan Academy is adopting "skills to proficient" as its primary metric for measuring student learning, as research shows this indicator correlates with standardized math assessments better than time spent alone. The approach aligns with mastery learning principles, emphasizing that students achieve deeper understanding by reaching higher proficiency levels on fewer skills rather than attempting many skills at shallow levels.

entries.title: RPPL Privacy Toolkit Reference Implementation
entries.desc: An integrated web application for CSV file de-identification and redaction review, combining OpenAI-powered PII removal with manual review capabilities. Developed as part of the RPPL partnership's work on privacy-preserving data sharing in educational research.

entries.title: RPPL Visualizer and Converter
entries.desc: A customizable local dashboard and survey visualizer for the RPPL Stronghold environment. Includes an access control system, CSV ingestion, and dynamic chart generation with organization-level comparisons.

entries.title: UpGrade — Open-Source A/B Testing Platform
entries.desc: An open-source platform for A/B testing and feature flagging in educational technology applications, supporting large-scale experiments with group assignment, segmentation, and factorial designs. Developed by Carnegie Learning for use in edtech research contexts.

entries.title: Finding Belonging: The Search for Comprehensive Measurements of Student Belonging in Math
entries.desc: Presents Phase One findings on developing an instrument to measure belonging in mathematics. Encompasses a review and assessment of existing belonging instruments, along with insights from a panel discussion regarding how school-aged students perceive belonging. Key deliverables include a comprehensive definition of belonging, an inventory of current instruments, a multidimensional framework accounting for context and perspective, validated items from existing scales, and a definitionally grounded recommended set of belonging items for future piloting, testing, and validation.

entries.title: Coaching Moves: A Taxonomy and Framework 2.0
entries.desc: A systematic framework by RPPL for describing coaching discourse and strategies used by professional learning coaches. The accompanying framework provides a shared language for reflecting on and improving coaching practice and helps practitioners collect data on coaching patterns and articulate a collective vision of coaching.

entries.title: Analyzing Math Learning Strategies at Scale using AI Representations
entries.desc: AERA 2025 poster presenting ASTRA (AI-based Strategy Analysis), which uses representation learning methods to discover patterns in ITS data for analyzing math strategies. Adapted BERT models to learn representations of math strategies from large-scale 6th and 7th-grade data collected from Carnegie Learning's MATHia platform. By observing sequences of student actions, BERT was pre-trained in an unsupervised manner to understand hidden structure within action sequences, then fine-tuned embeddings enabled identification of correct strategies and analysis of strategy effectiveness.

entries.title: Belonging in Math
entries.desc: Presentation outlining the development of a "belonging in math" measure for elementary students, addressing questions about belonging definition, math-specific belonging, considerations for diverse learners, belonging dimensions, and sample assessment items.

entries.title: Building a Community of Practice to Advance Equity in Mathematics Education Research and Development
entries.desc: A roundtable presentation by the AIMS Collaboratory Facilitation Team at the 2024 NCTM Annual Conference in Chicago that explores strategies for establishing collaborative networks focused on promoting equitable practices in mathematics education research and development.

entries.title: Linguistic Features Predicting Math Word Problem Readability Among Less-Skilled Readers
entries.desc: Investigates which linguistic features predict math word problem readability for struggling readers, using data from 297,072 students in an intelligent tutoring system. A random forest model achieved the best performance (AUC = .75), revealing that traditional readability metrics were not important to the model's performance. Instead, word count emerged as the most predictive feature, followed by custom semantic magnitude and lexical diversity measures, suggesting that specialized readability assessments for math problems differ substantially from conventional approaches.

entries.title: Navigating the Math Mind: The Latest in Math Mind Measures Development
entries.desc: This June 2025 presentation by researchers at Louisiana State University updated psychometric analyses of the Math Mind Measures (MMM) scale, a tool for assessing mathematical thinking. The researchers discussed the long form, short form, brief scales, and parallel versions of the MMM and introduced an accompanying user guide to support practitioners in implementing this measurement instrument.

entries.title: UpGrade: The Evidence Based Learning Platform
entries.desc: UpGrade is an educational research platform that enables researchers to conduct randomized experiments within learning applications. The system communicates with educational software to randomly assign students to experimental conditions monitored in the UpGrade UI, facilitating evidence-based research at scale. It has been integrated into major educational products like MATHia and BrainPOP, reaching hundreds of thousands of learners.

entries.title: Partnering for the Future: Innovative Middle School Math Solutions
entries.desc: This two-part webinar series, hosted by NORC at the University of Chicago as part of the Curriculum & Learning Improvement Project (CLIP), brings together education experts to discuss innovations in middle school mathematics. The sessions cover collaborative data-sharing strategies, emerging education technology solutions, and artificial intelligence applications aimed at enhancing student and educator experiences in math education.

entries.title: Digital Learning Platforms (DLP) Research Catalog
entries.desc: The DLP Catalog is a web-based resource that provides researchers with safe, transparent, and efficient access to information on learning data and experimental platforms to facilitate educational research. Platform providers can contribute their resources to help scholars discover collaboration opportunities. The site currently hosts datasets and platforms across multiple educational technology partners, including Khan Academy, OpenStax, and Curriculum Associates.

entries.title: Assessing ELA and Math Curriculum Shifts: A Practical Guide for Measurement and Progress Monitoring
entries.desc: This toolkit, developed by the Research Partnership for Professional Learning (RPPL), provides shared measurement instruments to help districts and professional learning providers track the implementation of high-quality instructional materials and curriculum-based professional learning in English Language Arts and Mathematics (grades 3-12). The toolkit includes surveys for leaders, teachers, and students, plus classroom observation protocols designed to monitor progress and support continuous improvement efforts across curriculum shifts.

entries.title: A Focus on Motivation: Summary of Focus Group Findings
entries.desc: This 2024 report documents findings from six focus groups with educators in East Baton Rouge Parish schools who discussed strategies for enhancing student motivation in mathematics. The researchers shared survey results from 5th-8th grade students and solicited educator recommendations on fostering internal determination to complete math goals and strengthening students' perceptions of math's importance.

entries.title: Best Practices: Running Focus Groups with Young Children
entries.desc: This practice guide by H. Colleen Sinclair offers recommendations for conducting focus groups with elementary school students. The resource was developed to help researchers gather students' experiences with mathematics instruction, particularly regarding their engagement with digital learning tools in classrooms. It presents practical, accessible guidance for qualitative research with young participants.

entries.title: Educator Perceptions of Working with DreamBox Learning
entries.desc: This research report evaluates how K-12 educators experienced implementing DreamBox Learning, a digital math platform, in their classrooms. The study gathered feedback through focus groups to understand what worked well and what challenges emerged, with particular attention to how the technology affected both student engagement and teaching practices.

entries.title: English Learner Reclassification Policy Structures and Student Characteristics
entries.desc: This research brief examines how English Learner reclassification policies vary across states and their relationship to student demographics. The authors analyze four distinct groups of state policies based on the kind of reclassification requirements and find that states with larger EL populations tend to implement more complex policies, while those experiencing faster EL growth rely more on English language proficiency assessments alone.

entries.title: English Learner Reclassification Policy Structures and Student Outcomes
entries.desc: This research brief examines how policy complexity in English Learner reclassification varies across four states and affects student outcomes. The study finds that students in states with more complex reclassification policies are less likely to be eligible for reclassification, particularly when non-English language proficiency assessments are included as requirements. The analysis suggests that more stringent policies may create barriers prolonging EL student status.

entries.title: Exploring the Impact of School Discipline and Attendance on Academic Achievement
entries.desc: This research brief examines how disciplinary actions and attendance patterns affect academic growth among over 66,000 elementary students in a large U.S. school district. The study found that attendance rate was a strong and consistent predictor of academic growth in both math and reading, while suspensions negatively impacted math achievement, underscoring the importance of equitable discipline practices and attendance support.

entries.title: Geaux Math! EBRPSS & LSU: Differentiating Lessons with AI
entries.desc: This report examines a pilot program created by East Baton Rouge Parish School System and Louisiana State University to explore how generative AI tools can help teachers develop differentiated math instruction for K-8 students. The study, funded by the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, analyzed the impact on teachers' levels of self-efficacy with new technology and their capacity to address diverse student needs while managing burnout.

entries.title: How Digital Platforms Provide Individualized Learning Experiences in Mathematics
entries.desc: This research report examines how a digital learning platform delivers personalized math instruction to approximately 2.7 million students in grades 1-5. The study analyzes assessment feedback mechanisms that adapt learning pathways based on student placement levels, demonstrating that students' experiences with digital learning platforms are individualized and responsive to their math learning needs.

entries.title: Investing Early: The Impact of State Prekindergarten Funding on Kindergarten Academic Outcomes
entries.desc: This research examines how state-level investment in pre-K programs for four-year-olds correlates with kindergarten math achievement across 31 school districts in nine states. Using data from over 200,000 students, the study found that kindergarten math growth was strongest in states with consistently high levels of PreK4 investment, and that overall district performance history proved to be a stronger predictor of success than pre-K enrollment alone.

entries.title: Math Performance in East Baton Rouge Parish Schools: 2023-2024
entries.desc: This research report examines how a digital learning platform and student motivation factors influence math achievement among middle school students in Louisiana. The study analyzes key motivational factors such as confidence and anxiety, digital learning use, and student characteristics to identify intervention strategies that could strengthen student confidence and reduce math anxiety.

entries.title: Measuring Motivation, Improving Engagement
entries.desc: This report addresses the challenge of assessing student motivation in mathematics by integrating existing measurement tools and piloting them across two diverse student populations. The authors developed 10 reliable scales to evaluate different aspects of math motivation, with the goal of constructing reliable scales of pertinent individual differences in math motivation to facilitate discussion about validity and classroom applications.

entries.title: New Normal in Early Elementary Mathematics Learning: Part III -- Learning from Variation in Grade 3
entries.desc: This research examines mathematics learning growth trajectories for third-graders using data from over 6,000 schools. The study found that disparities in student growth persist across school years, with Grade 3 emerging as a potential turning point where initial achievement gaps may begin to widen between students who started at different levels.

entries.title: New Normal in Early Elementary Mathematics Learning: Part II -- School-level Variation in Growth in Grades 1 and 2
entries.desc: This research examines variations in mathematics learning growth across schools for students in first and second grade during the 2021-2022 and 2022-2023 school years. Using data from over 6,000 schools and the i-Ready Diagnostic assessment tool, the study explores how school demographic characteristics relate to student achievement patterns and identifies that learning growth fluctuates year-to-year.

entries.title: New Normal in Early Elementary Mathematics Learning: Part I -- Initial Findings on Growth and Domains in Grade 1 through Grade 2
entries.desc: This research study examines mathematics learning outcomes for over 6,000 schools using adaptive assessment data from students who entered first grade in fall 2021. The analysis reveals that learning growth is greatest in the first half of students' first-grade year and identifies significant disparities in student progression, while highlighting Algebra and Algebraic Thinking as the strongest predictor of overall mathematical growth in early elementary grades.

entries.title: The Math Mind Measures: A User Guide
entries.desc: This user guide presents a comprehensive survey instrument designed to assess math motivation in students. The guide documents the development of the Math Mind Measures (MMM) across multiple forms, including long-forms with 138 items, short-forms with 77 items, brief versions, and parallel forms, created through piloting with diverse student populations across different grade levels and school districts.

entries.title: Toward a Common Research Framework for Professional Learning
entries.desc: This report documents findings from interviews with professional learning organizations within the RPPL network, focusing on how these groups manage and utilize data. The framework aims to advance four key objectives: creating standardized measurement tools across professional learning organizations, broadening access to shared data, establishing secure infrastructure for data storage, and developing innovative, cost-effective research measures.

entries.title: Understanding the Impact of Educators' Social-Emotional Learning (SEL) Skills on Student Academic Growth
entries.desc: This research brief investigates how teachers' social-emotional competencies, specifically emotion regulation and relationship management, correlate with student academic outcomes. The study analyzed survey data from 269 K-8 educators and found associations between teacher demographics and their SEL skills, while also examining how educator well-being relates to their emotional capabilities and perceptions of school climate.

entries.title: Curriculum & Learning Improvement Project (CLIP)
entries.desc: CLIP is an innovative data ecosystem developed by NORC at the University of Chicago for the Gates Foundation that integrates learning management systems and curriculum data to provide educators with real-time insights on middle school math instruction. The project enables teachers, principals, and school district leaders to link curriculum implementation to student outcomes and make more informed instructional decisions, while also supporting researchers in conducting evaluations more efficiently.

entries.title: ALTER-Math: AI-augmented Learning by Teaching to Enhance and Renovate Math Learning
entries.desc: ALTER-Math is a platform that helps middle school students strengthen their math understanding by teaching an AI "student." As learners explain concepts, correct mistakes, and guide their virtual peer, they engage in a research-based educational approach that combines personalization, motivational strategies, and evidence-based feedback to make math learning more accessible and effective for all students.

entries.title: Khanmigo by Khan Academy
entries.desc: The Khan Academy LEVI team develops Khanmigo, an AI-powered tutoring system designed to help students practice math skills with personalized support and feedback. The platform addresses a critical gap in classrooms where students often lack sufficient practice opportunities and immediate guidance when they struggle. By combining AI tutoring with teacher insights and data, the team aims to significantly improve student math performance across early grades and middle school levels.

entries.title: PLUS Tutoring by Carnegie Mellon University
entries.desc: PLUS (Personalized Learning Squared) Tutoring combines human tutors, student math software, and AI analytics to deliver targeted tutoring support during the school day. The platform analyzes student practice data to identify struggling students and allocate tutors strategically, reducing costs while maintaining one-on-one interaction. By focusing scarce human tutoring where it has the biggest impact, the system aims to scale high-quality instruction and improve equity in learning outcomes.

entries.title: Eedi: Misconceptions Map
entries.desc: Eedi develops an AI-powered "Misconceptions Map" that analyzes student math errors to identify root conceptual gaps and predict future learning struggles. The system uses a knowledge graph to track how math concepts interconnect, recommending personalized learning steps for each student. A randomized controlled trial involving 3,000 students across 20 schools demonstrated that using Eedi led to 2-4 months of additional learning gain in an academic year.

entries.title: MATHstream by Carnegie Learning
entries.desc: MATHstream is an interactive, adaptive live-video platform that pairs middle school students with expert instructors for real-time math problem-solving. The system incorporates generative AI supports for audio, video, and text chat to help students recover missed learning and build math proficiency, while also assisting teachers with lesson preparation and professional development. Districts in Massachusetts and Texas demonstrated significant gains, with students completing just 3-5 sessions achieving notable score improvements on state assessments.

entries.title: HAT: Hybrid Human-Agent Tutoring
entries.desc: HAT is an AI-powered platform developed by the University of Colorado Boulder and Saga Education that analyzes small-group tutoring sessions to provide tutors with data-driven feedback on their discourse practices. The system helps scale high-impact human tutoring while maintaining quality by giving tutors specific and directed feedback to improve their instructional techniques and ultimately enhance student math achievement in Title 1 schools.

entries.title: Rori by Rising Academies
entries.desc: Rori is an AI-powered WhatsApp chatbot that delivers personalized math tutoring via conversational interactions. The platform uses natural language processing and machine learning to assess student ability and deliver scaffolded lessons across Grades 1-9, enabling learning gains for under $5 per student annually without requiring high-speed internet or advanced devices. Field testing in Ghana showed the platform more than doubled learning gains among approximately 500 students over six months.

entries.title: TLA Educational AI Leaderboard
entries.desc: Educational AI evaluation platform featuring benchmarks designed around real educational tasks with private test sets to prevent data contamination. Includes ASAP 2.0 (automated scoring of student persuasive essays) and the Eedi Misconception Annotation Project (identifying student math misconceptions), with performance, cost, and latency comparisons across models.

entries.title: FAB-AI Pedagogy Benchmark Leaderboard
entries.desc: Pedagogy benchmark leaderboard evaluating 220+ AI models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, and others on education tasks for low- and middle-income countries. Includes downloadable CSV results, a quality assurance facility, and task-specific AI evaluation capabilities. Maintained by FAB-AI through their AI-for-Education.org initiative.

entries.title: TutorBench
entries.desc: Benchmark evaluating LLM tutoring capabilities across adaptive explanation generation, assessment and feedback, and active learning support in STEM subjects. Features 1,490 expert-curated prompts (56% multimodal) with 15,220 rubric criteria scored by an automated LLM judge achieving 78% agreement with human raters. The best-performing model scores only 55.7%, indicating tutoring remains a challenging frontier for AI.

entries.title: MathTutorBench
entries.desc: Benchmark assessing open-ended pedagogical capabilities of LLM tutors in mathematics through seven tasks organized around three teacher competencies: problem solving, Socratic questioning, and solution correctness evaluation. Published as an EMNLP 2025 Oral paper. Key finding: subject-matter expertise does not translate to effective teaching. Code, data, and a pedagogical reward model are openly available under CC BY 4.0.

entries.title: pyBKT — Python Bayesian Knowledge Tracing
entries.desc: Python library implementing Bayesian Knowledge Tracing and variants for modeling student cognitive mastery from problem-solving sequences. Supports individual student priors, item-specific parameters, and resource-based learning rates with a scikit-learn-style API. Includes optimized C++ backend reducing fitting time from 1.5 hours to 45 seconds for 5,000 students. Published at EDM 2021, 254 stars, MIT license.

entries.title: pyKT — Deep Learning Knowledge Tracing Toolkit
entries.desc: PyTorch-based library providing standardized preprocessing, training, and evaluation for 29+ deep learning knowledge tracing models including DKT, DKVMN, SAKT, SAINT, and AKT across 7+ datasets. Published at NeurIPS 2022 with hyperparameter tuning results. 397 stars, MIT license.

entries.title: py-irt — Bayesian Item Response Theory
entries.desc: Python library implementing Bayesian IRT models using variational inference, supporting 1PL (Rasch), 2PL, and 4PL models with vague or hierarchical priors. Provides CLI and programmatic interfaces for training and evaluation on scored prediction datasets. Published in INFORMS Journal on Computing 2023, 167 stars, MIT license.

entries.title: ADL xAPI Wrapper
entries.desc: Official JavaScript client library from the Advanced Distributed Learning Initiative (U.S. Department of Defense) for communicating with xAPI-compliant Learning Record Stores. Handles statement creation, activity states, agent profiles, attachments, and authentication. 223 stars, Apache 2.0 license.

entries.title: ADL Learning Record Store
entries.desc: Open-source reference implementation of a Learning Record Store for storing xAPI learning data, updated to align with IEEE 9274.1.1 (xAPI 2.0). Built in Python with Docker support, OAuth integration, and Nginx/uWSGI deployment options. 331 stars, Apache 2.0 license.

entries.title: OATutor — Open-Source Adaptive Tutoring System
entries.desc: Open-source adaptive tutoring system using Bayesian Knowledge Tracing for skill mastery estimation, with scaffolded hints and intelligent problem selection. Built in React with optional Firebase logging, deployable to static hosting without a backend. Published at CHI 2023 with learning gains documented in PLOS ONE. 199 stars, MIT license (CC BY 4.0 for content).

entries.title: Learning Commons — AI Developer Tools for Education
entries.desc: Open developer platform providing research-backed resources for building instructionally grounded AI products. Offers a Knowledge Graph API combining curriculum data, academic standards, and learning science research, plus Evaluators for assessing AI-generated educational content quality (literacy, vocabulary, grade-level appropriateness). Includes MCP server integration, playground environments, and alignment with Illustrative Math curriculum.

entries.title: Pre-K Mathematics with DLM Early Childhood Express Math
entries.desc: Pre-K Mathematics with DLM Early Childhood Express Math is a supplemental preschool curriculum using teacher-guided, small-group activities with concrete manipulatives, combined with take-home activities to develop mathematical knowledge in young children. The WWC reviewed 2 studies meeting standards, finding strong positive effects on general mathematics achievement for preschool children in urban settings with racially and ethnically diverse populations. The program showed no discernible effects on oral language, phonological processing, or print knowledge, indicating its primary strength is mathematics readiness.

entries.title: Literacy Express
entries.desc: Literacy Express is a comprehensive preschool curriculum for ages 3–5 that delivers instruction through units covering oral language, emergent literacy, basic math, science, general knowledge, and socioemotional development, accompanied by professional development, teaching materials, and classroom management guidance. The WWC reviewed three studies meeting standards and found the program demonstrated positive effects on oral language, phonological processing, and print knowledge, but no discernible effects on cognition or general mathematics achievement. The evidence base draws primarily from implementations with English learner populations in urban settings in Florida and California.

entries.title: Waterford Early Reading Level One
entries.desc: Waterford Early Reading Level One is a computer-based emergent literacy curriculum for preschool children that targets phonological awareness, letter recognition, and print concepts through computer tutorials, reading placement assessments, and supplementary activities. The WWC identified 2 eligible studies but only 1 met design standards, showing potentially positive effects on print knowledge and no discernible effects on oral language. The limited qualifying evidence constrains the strength of conclusions about the program's overall effectiveness.

entries.title: SpellRead
entries.desc: SpellRead (formerly SpellRead Phonological Auditory Training) is a reading intervention for grade 2 and above that integrates auditory and visual reading processes through systematic, explicit instruction, teaching students to recognize and manipulate English sounds and practice skills using appropriately leveled texts. The WWC reviewed 2 studies meeting standards and found positive effects on alphabetics and potentially positive effects on both fluency and comprehension, with evidence evaluated at grades 5–6 in a predominantly White student sample. The program also serves special education students and English language learners who are significantly below grade level in reading.

entries.title: Cooperative Integrated Reading and Composition (CIRC) — Adolescent Literacy
entries.desc: Cooperative Integrated Reading and Composition (CIRC) is a comprehensive reading and writing program for grades 2–8 that combines cooperative learning with literacy instruction, featuring story-related activities, direct comprehension instruction, and integrated language arts tasks completed in pairs and teams, with a Spanish version available for grades 2–5. The WWC reviewed 6 eligible studies, of which 2 met standards, and found potentially positive effects on comprehension and general literacy achievement at the Tier 3 evidence level. The adolescent literacy report covers grades 2–6, with a separate review protocol applied for this age range.

entries.title: Lindamood Phoneme Sequencing (LiPS) — Students with Learning Disabilities
entries.desc: Lindamood Phoneme Sequencing (LiPS), formerly called Auditory Discrimination in Depth, teaches students to decode words by identifying individual sounds using the oral-motor aspects of sounds to identify and order them within words, along with instruction in letter patterns, sight words, and context clues, targeting kindergarten through grade 3 and struggling or dyslexic readers. In the review focused on students with specific learning disabilities, the WWC found potentially positive effects on alphabetics and reading fluency, and potentially negative effects on writing, based on 1 study meeting standards out of 2 eligible studies. The evidence is classified as Tier 3, reflecting a limited but informative evidence base for this population.

entries.title: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies — English Language Learners (Reading)
entries.desc: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) is a peer-tutoring program for grades K–6 in which students alternate roles as tutor and learner during 30–35 minute sessions, coaching and correcting one another while teachers assign and regularly reassign pairs based on student needs. This intervention report focuses on reading achievement outcomes for English language learners in grades 3–6, finding potentially positive effects based on 1 study meeting standards out of 3 eligible studies reviewed, at the Tier 3 evidence level. The evidence suggests the peer-tutoring model can support reading gains for English learners in elementary settings.

entries.title: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies — Adolescent Literacy
entries.desc: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) is a peer-tutoring program for grades K–6 in which students take turns as tutor and tutee during structured sessions, coaching each other through problems while teachers manage pair assignments based on student abilities. This adolescent literacy review addresses reading comprehension outcomes, finding potentially positive effects based on 1 qualifying study out of 5 eligible studies, with evidence classified at Tier 3. The research examined students from predominantly White, suburban and urban backgrounds across grades 1–6 delivered in small-group settings.

entries.title: Reading Apprenticeship — Grade 9 Literacy
entries.desc: Reading Apprenticeship is a professional development initiative that trains teachers to model reading comprehension strategies and help students develop literacy skills alongside social-emotional learning objectives such as growth mindset and self-efficacy, targeting grade 9 students. The WWC found potentially positive effects on comprehension based on 1 study meeting standards out of 3 reviewed, placing the evidence at Tier 3. The limited evidence base suggests the need for additional high-quality research to strengthen confidence in the intervention's impact on adolescent literacy.

entries.title: Read Naturally — Students with Specific Learning Disabilities
entries.desc: Read Naturally is a supplemental reading program combining books, audiotapes, and computer software that uses repeated reading, teacher modeling, and progress monitoring to develop reading proficiency, with students working independently at appropriate reading levels in grades 2–6. In this review focused on students with specific learning disabilities, the WWC found no discernible effects on reading fluency but potentially positive effects on writing achievement, based on 2 studies meeting standards out of 3 eligible studies. The mixed findings across outcome domains highlight the importance of examining intervention effects separately by outcome type and population.

entries.title: Reading Recovery — Grade 1 Beginning Reading
entries.desc: Reading Recovery provides one-on-one tutoring to grade 1 students with low literacy achievement, delivered by trained teachers in daily 30-minute sessions over 12–20 weeks, incorporating instruction in phonemic awareness, phonics, vocabulary, fluency, and comprehension tailored to individual student needs. The WWC reviewed 79 eligible studies, finding 3 that met standards, and concluded the intervention demonstrated positive effects on general reading achievement and potentially positive effects on alphabetics, reading fluency, and comprehension at the Tier 3 evidence level. Despite the large number of eligible studies, the small number meeting rigorous design standards limits the confidence in the overall evidence base.

entries.title: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies — Students with Specific Learning Disabilities
entries.desc: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) is a peer-tutoring program for grades K–6 in which students alternate as tutor and tutee during 30–35 minute structured sessions, targeting both reading and mathematics outcomes for students with specific learning disabilities. The WWC reviewed 4 studies meeting standards and found potentially positive effects on reading fluency and comprehension, and no discernible effects on mathematics achievement, with reading outcomes rated at Tier 3 evidence. The findings suggest PALS is most effective for literacy skill development within this population, with more limited impact on math.

entries.title: The Incredible Years
entries.desc: The Incredible Years is a multi-component program for children ages 0–12 providing training for children, parents, and teachers, with child lessons covering feelings recognition, friendship skills, anger management, problem solving, and school behavior to build social and emotional competencies. The WWC focused on grades PK–2 and found potentially positive effects on both external behavior and social outcomes, based on 1 qualifying study out of 3 eligible studies reviewed, at the Tier 3 evidence level. The program's multi-tier design targeting children, parents, and teachers simultaneously makes it notable among early childhood social-emotional interventions.

entries.title: Chemistry That Applies
entries.desc: Chemistry That Applies is a 24-lesson science instructional unit organized into four clusters in which students explore four chemical reactions — burning, rusting, the decomposition of water, and the reaction of baking soda and vinegar — through hands-on laboratory investigations to understand the law of conservation of matter, targeting grades 8–10. The WWC identified 1 eligible study that met design standards and found potentially positive effects on general science achievement, representing a limited but directionally favorable evidence base. The hands-on, inquiry-based approach distinguishes this unit curriculum from standard lecture-based chemistry instruction.

entries.title: GEMS: The Real Reasons for Seasons
entries.desc: Great Explorations in Math and Science (GEMS): The Real Reasons for Seasons is a middle school science curriculum unit using models, hands-on investigations, peer-to-peer discussions, reflection, and informational readings to teach scientific concepts about the Sun-Earth connection and seasonal phenomena, targeting grades 6–8. The WWC reviewed 1 eligible study that met standards and found potentially negative effects on general science achievement, making this one of the few WWC-reviewed science programs with an adverse evidence rating. The curriculum's hands-on design contrasts with its negative outcome finding, underscoring the importance of rigorous evaluation.

entries.title: Read Naturally — General Literacy Achievement (Adolescent)
entries.desc: Read Naturally is a supplemental reading program combining books, audiotapes, and computer software that improves reading fluency through repeated reading, teacher modeling, and progress monitoring, serving elementary through middle school students. In this adolescent literacy review covering grades 3–5, the WWC found no discernible effects on general literacy achievement, based on 1 qualifying study out of 4 eligible studies. The reviewed sample was predominantly Hispanic (39%) and Black (35%), with 65% qualifying for free/reduced-price lunch and 35% being English learners in urban Minnesota settings.

entries.title: Developmental Summer Bridge Programs
entries.desc: Developmental Summer Bridge Programs are designed to reduce the need for developmental education in college by providing students with accelerated developmental instruction between high school and college, combining academic content with college preparation training. The WWC reviewed 10 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on academic achievement, postsecondary enrollment, or credit accumulation across all three outcome domains. The absence of positive effects despite the program's intuitive appeal highlights the need for rigorous evidence before broad implementation.

entries.title: TAP: The System for Teacher and Student Advancement
entries.desc: TAP (formerly the Teacher Advancement Program) is a comprehensive educator effectiveness system for pre-K through grade 12 that aims to improve student achievement through leadership opportunities, performance-based salary increases, professional development, teacher evaluations, and performance bonuses to attract, retain, develop, and motivate effective teachers. The WWC reviewed 10 eligible studies, with 2 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on general achievement for students after one year of implementation. The evidence tier was not assigned, indicating the research base was insufficient to establish a tier rating despite the large number of studies reviewed.

entries.title: New Teacher Center Induction Model
entries.desc: The New Teacher Center (NTC) Induction Model is a comprehensive program providing one-on-one mentoring and professional development in a supportive school environment to accelerate the effectiveness of beginning teachers and improve student learning outcomes in kindergarten through grade 6. The WWC reviewed 2 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects across all three teacher retention outcome domains — retention at the school, in the profession, and in the school district. The findings suggest the program's mentoring approach does not have a measurable effect on teacher retention by the metrics studied.

entries.title: Saxon Algebra I
entries.desc: Saxon Algebra I is part of the Saxon Math textbook series covering grades K–12, based on incremental development and continual review of mathematical concepts, emphasizing explicit instruction, continuous review of prior concepts, and frequent cumulative assessments to build fluency in algebra for grades 8–9. The WWC reviewed 12 eligible studies, with 2 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on algebra achievement, with the evidence not meeting the threshold for a tier assignment. The research sample was predominantly Hispanic (43%) and White (46%), with about 46% from economically disadvantaged backgrounds.

entries.title: First Year Experience Courses for Developmental Education Students
entries.desc: First Year Experience Courses for students in developmental education are designed to ease the college transition for students requiring remedial coursework, typically introducing campus resources, providing training in time management and study skills, and addressing student development issues — often linked with or taken concurrently with developmental courses. The WWC reviewed 19 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on academic achievement, credit accumulation and persistence, or progress in developmental education across all three outcome domains. The large gap between eligible and qualifying studies reflects the challenge of rigorous evaluation in this postsecondary context.

entries.title: Saxon Math — High School Multiple Courses
entries.desc: Saxon Math is a core K–12 mathematics curriculum using an incremental approach to instruction and assessment that limits new content per day and reviews concepts cumulatively through ongoing practice rather than discrete chapters, targeting high school students across multiple courses. The WWC found no discernible effects on math achievement for high school students in this review of multiple Saxon courses, based on studies meeting standards. The findings are consistent with the separate Saxon Algebra I review and suggest limited evidence of effectiveness at the secondary level.

entries.title: Cognitive Tutor Geometry
entries.desc: Cognitive Tutor Geometry is a mathematics curriculum combining textbooks and interactive software published by Carnegie Learning, targeting grades 9–12 students in secondary geometry. The WWC reviewed 2 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found potentially negative effects on geometry achievement, delivered as whole-class instruction in urban settings. The potentially negative finding represents an important caution for districts considering this technology-integrated curriculum for high school geometry.

entries.title: Pivotal Response Training
entries.desc: Pivotal Response Training (PRT) is an intervention for children ages 2–18 with Autism Spectrum Disorders that focuses on core affected areas including communication and responsiveness to environmental cues, using instruction, child-selected stimuli, attention-focusing, and reward-based encouragement to develop desired behaviors with increasing complexity. The WWC reviewed 40 eligible studies, with 6 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on communication and language competencies for children and students with autism spectrum disorder. The large number of eligible studies reviewed reflects PRT's prevalence in ASD research, though relatively few met rigorous design standards.

entries.title: Connected Mathematics Project (CMP)
entries.desc: Connected Mathematics Project (CMP) uses interactive problems and everyday situations to explore mathematical ideas with a goal of fostering a problem-centered, inquiry-based learning environment, addressing numbers, algebra, geometry/measurement, probability, and statistics across grades 6–8. The WWC reviewed 24 eligible studies, with 2 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on general mathematics achievement, with no evidence that the intervention had an effect on outcomes. The minimal proportion of studies meeting design standards despite the large body of research underscores the difficulty of conducting rigorous evaluations of widely used curricula.

entries.title: Saxon Math — Primary Courses (Grades 1–8)
entries.desc: Saxon Math is a core K–12 mathematics curriculum in which new concepts are developed, reviewed, and practiced cumulatively rather than in discrete chapters or units, with limited new content introduced per day and ongoing practice of previously learned material, reviewed here for grades 1–8. The WWC reviewed 26 eligible studies, with 5 meeting standards, and found mixed effects — inconsistent evidence of impact — on mathematics test scores in primary courses. Sample characteristics across qualifying studies included 45% White, 16% Black, and 40% Hispanic students, with 45% receiving free/reduced-price lunch.

entries.title: TNTP Teaching Fellows
entries.desc: TNTP Teaching Fellows is a selective alternative teacher certification program that recruits career changers and uncertified recent graduates, providing online coursework and 5–7 weeks of in-person training focused on foundational teaching skills, plus ongoing professional development in the first year, targeting middle and high school instruction in grades 6–12. The WWC reviewed 8 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on mathematics achievement for middle and high school students. The lack of a tier assignment reflects insufficient qualifying evidence to establish a formal evidence tier.

entries.title: I CAN Learn Algebra
entries.desc: I CAN Learn Algebra is a computer-based math curriculum for secondary students — including middle school, high school, and college — that provides mathematics instruction through a series of interactive lessons that students work on individually at their own computers. The WWC reviewed 12 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on general mathematics achievement for secondary students, with a Tier 0 evidence classification indicating insufficient qualifying research. The limited qualifying evidence limits what can be concluded about the program's effectiveness compared to its wide usage.

entries.title: Prentice Hall/Pearson Literature (2007–2015)
entries.desc: Prentice Hall/Pearson Literature (2007–2015) is an English language arts curriculum for grades 6–12 using passages from fiction, nonfiction, poetry, and digital media, with online components for personalized assignments and progress monitoring to build general literacy achievement and comprehension. The WWC reviewed 3 qualifying studies and found no discernible effects on general literacy achievement and comprehension for adolescent readers, with evaluations covering grades 7–10 for comprehension and grades 7–9 for literacy achievement. The no-discernible-effects finding is notable for a widely adopted commercial ELA curriculum at the secondary level.

entries.title: Accelerated Math — Primary Mathematics
entries.desc: Accelerated Math is a software tool by Renaissance Learning for grades K–12 that provides individualized practice problems, scores student work automatically, and generates progress reports to help teachers differentiate mathematics instruction, reviewed here for primary mathematics in grades 2–9. The WWC reviewed 24 eligible studies, with 6 meeting standards, and found mixed effects on mathematics test scores in primary courses, indicating inconsistent evidence of impact. The large research base with mixed results suggests that implementation context and fidelity may play a significant role in whether the intervention produces gains.

entries.title: NBPTS Certification — Student Achievement
entries.desc: National Board for Professional Teaching Standards (NBPTS) Certification awards professional certification to educators whose teaching practices meet established standards for accomplished teaching, requiring a computer-based assessment and three portfolio entries completed over 1 to 5 years, evaluated here for effects on student achievement in grades 3–8. The WWC reviewed 39 eligible studies, with 5 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on English language arts achievement and mixed effects on general mathematics achievement. The large number of eligible studies and varied results point to significant heterogeneity in how board certification relates to student outcomes across contexts.

entries.title: Achieve3000 — Beginning Reading Fluency
entries.desc: Achieve3000 is a supplemental online literacy program providing differentiated nonfiction reading content to students in grades preK–12, building phonemic awareness, phonics, fluency, comprehension, vocabulary, and writing through instruction tailored to each student's reading ability, reviewed here for beginning readers in grades 2–3. The WWC reviewed 2 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on reading fluency for beginning readers. The no-discernible-effects finding in the beginning reading context suggests the program may be better suited to its adolescent literacy applications reviewed under a separate report.

entries.title: Passport Reading Journeys
entries.desc: Passport Reading Journeys is a supplemental curriculum for grades 6–12 combining teacher-led instruction with technology to enhance reading comprehension, vocabulary, word study, and writing skills for struggling readers, organized around themed two-week instructional sequences with whole-class and small-group lessons, independent reading, videos, and computer-based practice. The WWC reviewed 6 eligible studies, with 3 meeting standards, and found mixed effects on reading comprehension (grades 6–9) and no discernible effects on general literacy achievement (grades 6–9). The research sample was predominantly Black (67%) and economically disadvantaged (87% free/reduced-price lunch eligible).

entries.title: Word Generation
entries.desc: Word Generation is a supplemental program for grades 4–8 that builds students' vocabulary, academic language, and perspective-taking skills through classroom discussion and debate, using interdisciplinary units with daily lessons centered on high-interest topics, with variants including WordGen Weekly and subject-specific editions in science and social studies. The WWC reviewed 5 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on either reading comprehension or English language proficiency, with all qualifying studies involving 100% English learner populations. Despite its design rationale for academic language development, the program did not demonstrate measurable gains on the evaluated outcome domains.

entries.title: Literacy Design Collaborative (LDC)
entries.desc: Literacy Design Collaborative (LDC) provides professional development, coaching, and resources to support teachers working collaboratively in their schools to design and implement high-quality, standards-aligned literacy instruction materials focused on reading, research, and writing skills for students in grades 4–8. The WWC reviewed 5 eligible studies, with 3 meeting standards, and found mixed effects on general literacy achievement and no discernible effects on general social studies achievement. The mixed literacy finding across a relatively robust evidence base (3 qualifying studies) suggests LDC may be effective for some implementation contexts but not consistently across all schools.

entries.title: Promoting Alternative Thinking Strategies (PATHS)
entries.desc: Promoting Alternative Thinking Strategies (PATHS) is an elementary curriculum for grades 1–5 that delivers short lessons focused on self-control, emotional literacy, social competence, positive peer relations, and interpersonal problem-solving skills to enhance social-emotional competencies and reduce aggression and behavior problems. The WWC reviewed 13 eligible studies, with 2 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on academic achievement, social interactions, observed individual behavior, or emotional status for elementary students. The null findings across all four outcome domains are notable given the program's widespread adoption in U.S. schools.

entries.title: Xtreme Reading
entries.desc: Xtreme Reading is a supplemental literacy curriculum for struggling readers in grades 6–12 designed to improve vocabulary, decoding, fluency, and reading comprehension through teacher-led whole-group instruction, cooperative group work, paired practice, and independent practice along with social skills training and motivational components. The WWC reviewed 3 eligible studies, with 2 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on comprehension and general literacy achievement for struggling readers in grades 6–12. The consistent null findings across both outcome domains examined suggest limited evidence of the program's effectiveness for this population.

entries.title: Math Expressions
entries.desc: Math Expressions is a mathematics curriculum for pre-K through grade 6 that builds students' conceptual understanding through real-world situations, visual supports including drawings and manipulatives, multiple problem-solving approaches, and opportunities for students to explain their mathematical thinking. The WWC reviewed 3 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on general mathematics achievement for elementary students compared with other mathematics curricula used in the comparison schools. The single qualifying study limits the strength of conclusions that can be drawn about the program's effectiveness.

entries.title: University of Chicago School Mathematics Project (UCSMP) Transitions Math
entries.desc: University of Chicago School Mathematics Project (UCSMP) Transitions/Pre-Transition Math is a core mathematics curriculum for grades 6–9 featuring an inquiry-based approach with active learning, hands-on and small-group activities, covering arithmetic, algebra, geometry, probability, and statistics at progressively advanced levels across the two course variants. The WWC reviewed 4 eligible studies, with 3 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on general mathematics achievement or algebra outcomes for students in grades 6–9 compared with other curricula used in study schools. The relatively strong evidence base (3 qualifying studies) with null findings provides a fairly confident negative conclusion about this curriculum's comparative effectiveness.

entries.title: Social Belonging Intervention
entries.desc: Social Belonging interventions aim to reduce the impacts of negative stereotypes that burden students from underrepresented groups — including racial and ethnic minorities, women in engineering, and first-generation college students — and affect their persistence in postsecondary education. The WWC reviewed 11 eligible studies, with 7 meeting standards, and found mixed effects on academic achievement, no discernible effects on college enrollment, and mixed effects on progression in college. The relatively large number of qualifying studies (7) makes this one of the better-evidenced postsecondary intervention reports, with the mixed findings reflecting heterogeneous outcomes across student populations and institutional contexts.

entries.title: LASER — Science Education Reform (Grades 3–8)
entries.desc: Leadership and Assistance for Science Education Reform (LASER) builds capacity for implementing inquiry-based science curricula through leadership development institutes, professional development for teachers, instructional materials access, assessment support, and partnerships with scientists and community leaders, targeting grades 3–8. The WWC reviewed 2 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on general science achievement compared with standard instruction. The no-discernible-effects finding for this systemic capacity-building approach suggests that regional infrastructure development alone may not translate to measurable student achievement gains.

entries.title: Words and Concepts (Pre-K Oral Language Software)
entries.desc: Words and Concepts is a computer software program for pre-K children designed to build oral language abilities including vocabulary, comprehension, word relationships, and conceptual understanding through six units covering vocabulary, categorization, word identification by function, word association, and conceptual learning, also serving children and adults with language-learning disabilities, developmental disabilities, and other special needs. The WWC reviewed 1 eligible study that met standards and found no discernible effects on oral language outcomes for early childhood populations. The program's broad accessibility design for diverse special needs populations distinguishes it from standard early literacy software.

entries.title: MyTeachingPartner Pre-K
entries.desc: MyTeachingPartner Pre-K is a professional development program incorporating a video library of teacher-student interactions, interactive training, a three-credit college course, and web-mediated coaching focused on improving classroom instruction quality based on the Classroom Assessment Scoring System (CLASS), targeting pre-K teachers and classrooms. As of February 2018, the WWC found no studies meeting their design standards for the Teacher Training, Evaluation, and Compensation review protocol, and therefore cannot draw conclusions about the intervention's effectiveness. The program's video-based coaching approach and CLASS alignment represent a distinctive design rationale among early childhood professional development models.

entries.title: System of Least Prompts
entries.desc: System of Least Prompts (SLP), also known as least-to-most prompting, is an instructional practice for students with intellectual disabilities in which teachers establish a structured hierarchy of prompts starting with the least assistive and increasing support until the student performs the skill correctly. The WWC found no qualifying group design studies, and single-case design studies did not reach the threshold required for an evidence rating in any outcome domain. Despite the absence of group-design evidence, SLP is widely used in special education for skill acquisition across academic and functional tasks.

entries.title: Harlem Children's Zone Promise Academy Charter Schools
entries.desc: Harlem Children's Zone (HCZ) Promise Academy Charter Schools is a non-profit initiative providing community services and operating two public K–12 charter schools featuring extended school days and years, an academic and arts focus, student progress monitoring, teacher quality initiatives, and community support services including counseling and medical care. As of January 2018, the WWC found no studies meeting their design standards and therefore cannot draw conclusions about the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of this intervention. The absence of qualifying evidence reflects the challenges of applying group design standards to unique charter school models with small catchment populations.

entries.title: ACT Aspire — Transition to College
entries.desc: ACT Aspire is a longitudinal, systematic assessment system for grades 3 through early high school that monitors students' preparation for high school studies and readiness for college and career across five subject areas, designed to support ongoing instructional adjustment toward college and career readiness goals. As of May 2017, the WWC found no studies meeting their design standards within the Transition to College review protocol and therefore cannot draw research-based conclusions about ACT Aspire's effectiveness in improving college transition outcomes. The program's assessment-based design distinguishes it from instructional interventions, reflecting the WWC's broader interest in evaluating tools used to support educational decision-making.

entries.title: Prentice Hall Literature (1989–2005)
entries.desc: Prentice Hall Literature (1989–2005) is an English language arts curriculum for grades 6–12 emphasizing reading, writing, listening, viewing, speaking, and language skills through thematic organization, genre-focused units, and supplementary materials for differentiated instruction. As of the WWC review, no studies were found meeting design standards within the Adolescent Literacy review protocol, preventing the WWC from drawing research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness. The curriculum's long publication history and widespread adoption contrast with the complete absence of qualifying rigorous evaluations.

entries.title: Reciprocal Teaching — Students with Learning Disabilities
entries.desc: Reciprocal Teaching is an interactive instructional method for improving reading comprehension in which the teacher and students take turns leading a dialogue regarding segments of the text, teaching four core strategies: generating questions, summarizing, clarifying, and predicting, reviewed here for students with specific learning disabilities. The WWC found no studies meeting their evidence standards for this population within the relevant review protocol and therefore cannot draw conclusions about the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of Reciprocal Teaching for students with learning disabilities. The lack of qualifying evidence is notable given the intervention's prominent theoretical basis and widespread discussion in the reading research literature.

entries.title: UCSMP Advanced Algebra
entries.desc: University of Chicago School Mathematics Project (UCSMP) Advanced Algebra is a core secondary mathematics curriculum emphasizing problem solving, real-world applications, and the use of technology, featuring a student-centered approach with active learning that incorporates reading and flexible lesson structures. As of May 2016, the WWC found no studies meeting their design standards within the Secondary Mathematics review protocol, and therefore cannot draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness. The UCSMP curriculum family includes multiple courses, each reviewed separately, with none of the individual courses having qualifying evaluative evidence in the secondary mathematics protocol.

entries.title: Residential Learning Communities
entries.desc: Residential Learning Communities (also called living-learning programs) are postsecondary programs that integrate students' academic and residential environments by having students live together in dormitories, take classes together, and participate in structured co-curricular and extracurricular activities designed to strengthen academic engagement and social connection. As of November 2014, the WWC found no studies meeting their design standards for this intervention within the Developmental Education review protocol, and therefore cannot draw research-based conclusions about its effectiveness. The absence of qualifying evidence leaves an important gap given the widespread implementation of residential learning communities across U.S. colleges and universities.

entries.title: Lexia Core5 Reading
entries.desc: Lexia Core5 Reading is a blended learning program for PreK–5 that provides explicit, systematic reading instruction through personalized learning paths adapted to individual student skill gaps, combining online instruction with embedded assessments to help educators customize and track student progress. Across four studies involving over 17,000 students, Core5 students showed an average reading achievement effect size of +0.16 compared to matched comparison groups. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: SIPPS (Systematic Instruction in Phonological Awareness, Phonics, and Sight Words)
entries.desc: SIPPS is a structured-literacy reading curriculum using explicit instruction in phonological awareness, phonics, and sight words to support both new and struggling readers across three developmental levels, with flexible grouping based on individual student needs. In a quasi-experimental study of over 500 first and second graders, students receiving SIPPS as a Tier 2 intervention scored significantly higher on DIBELS measures than students who did not (effect size = +0.25). Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Expository Reading and Writing Course (ERWC)
entries.desc: The Expository Reading and Writing Course (ERWC) is a 12th-grade college-readiness program designed to prepare high school seniors to enter California State University and community colleges without needing remediation in English, providing curriculum materials and several days of professional development and coaching. Teachers focus instruction on text analysis, critical thinking, discussions, oral language development, and writing across genres. A statewide evaluation found ERWC students achieved modestly higher English placement test scores than peers in traditional classrooms (effect size +0.13), earning a Moderate rating from Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: The Superkids Reading Program
entries.desc: The Superkids Reading Program is a phonics-based beginning reading curriculum for grades K–2 that emphasizes phonemic awareness, phonics, vocabulary, comprehension, writing, and spelling. Two matched studies demonstrated positive effects on sound and word reading measures, with an average effect size of +0.23, and teachers reported high satisfaction with the program's ease of implementation and motivating qualities. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Amira
entries.desc: Amira is an AI-powered reading tutor for grades K–4 that provides personalized instruction, assesses oral reading fluency, and generates diagnostic reports for teachers and parents. Two research studies—including a randomized trial of 178 students (effect size +0.64) and a matched study of over 15,000 kindergarten and first-grade students (effect sizes +0.26 and +0.06)—demonstrated consistent positive impacts on early reading outcomes. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Imagine Español
entries.desc: Imagine Español is a personalized learning program for Pre-K through grade 6 that accelerates Spanish language and literacy development through engaging, culturally diverse content, with demonstrated gains in both Spanish and English reading proficiency. Two quasi-experimental studies found statistically significant improvements, including effect sizes of +0.25 on Spanish CIRCLE assessments for Pre-K students and +0.16 in English reading gains for grades 2–6. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Heggerty's Bridge to Reading Curriculum
entries.desc: Heggerty's Bridge to Reading Curriculum is a foundational skills program for grades K–2 that pairs explicit phonics instruction with phonemic awareness lessons in a 30-minute daily format, targeting decoding, reading fluency, and phonological awareness. Two studies showed consistently positive effects, including effect sizes of +0.16 to +0.33 on MAP Growth Reading assessments for first and second graders, with a weighted average effect size of +0.23. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Phonological Awareness Deluxe Package (PA Lessons)
entries.desc: The Phonological Awareness Deluxe Package provides explicit, systematic small-group instruction to develop foundational phonological awareness skills in kindergarten students, with teacher-friendly lessons accessible to diverse learners including English language learners. Two studies involving 956 kindergarten students across California schools found significant improvements, with effect sizes of +0.21 and +0.74 on the Acadience Reading assessment, for a combined average of +0.49. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Exact Path Growth — Reading
entries.desc: Exact Path Growth – Reading is a personalized learning platform that accelerates and measures student reading achievement through individualized learning paths and adaptive diagnostic assessments, studied at the 6th-grade level. A quasi-experimental study of 4,367 sixth-grade students in Florida found that students completing at least one lesson significantly outperformed those completing only diagnostics, with an effect size of +0.16 on ELA achievement. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: 95 Phonics Lesson Library (PLL)
entries.desc: The 95 Phonics Lesson Library is a comprehensive Tier 2 small-group phonics program that breaks phonics into 75 distinct subskills across three levels, providing ready-to-use lesson plans for below-benchmark readers in grades 1 and 4–5. Two studies involving 828 students demonstrated positive effects on Acadience Reading assessments, with effect sizes of +0.23 (1st grade) and +0.18 to +0.54 (4th–5th grade), averaging +0.21. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Just Right Reader Take-Everywhere Literacy Packs — Reading
entries.desc: Just Right Reader Take-Everywhere Literacy Packs is a kindergarten decodable book program aligned with the Science of Reading that extends classroom phonics instruction to the home, providing personalized packs with lesson plans and multilingual QR-code video guides for family support. A study of 399 kindergarten students across six Texas schools found users scored significantly higher on end-of-year DIBELS composite scores than comparison students (effect size = +0.39). Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Lexia English Language Development
entries.desc: Lexia English Language Development is a blended program for grades K–6 that combines interactive student-directed online lessons with teacher-led instruction to develop English proficiency for English learners. A quasi-experimental study of 1,570 students across California districts found significant positive effects on both the Overall ELPAC language proficiency assessment (effect size = +0.23) and SBAC English Language Arts assessment (effect size = +0.22), with strongest results for lower-proficiency students. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Coursemojo (Mojo)
entries.desc: Coursemojo (Mojo) is an AI-powered teaching platform for 6th-grade English Language Arts that works alongside classroom teachers to provide individualized academic support to every student. Two quasi-experimental studies in Tennessee and Texas (totaling over 5,000 students) demonstrated positive impacts on reading achievement (effect sizes +0.12 and +0.11), with notable equity gains for students with disabilities and economically disadvantaged learners. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Reading Go! (Formerly Reading Rescue)
entries.desc: Reading Go! (formerly Reading Rescue) trains school staff to provide one-on-one tutoring to struggling first and second graders through 10 full days of professional development over two years, combining fluency building, phonological awareness, word study, and vocabulary instruction in daily 30-minute sessions. A study of language-minority, low-SES students in New York City found significantly positive results on word attack and decoding measures, with an average effect size of +0.39. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Exact Path Proficiency + Exact Path Growth — Reading
entries.desc: Exact Path Proficiency + Exact Path Growth – Reading is a digital learning system for middle grades (6–8) combining personalized, adaptive skill-based instruction with standards-aligned formative assessment tools including practice tests and test-readiness activities. A quasi-experimental study of 3,323 middle school students in Florida found statistically significant improvements in ELA achievement on the FAST assessment (effect size = +0.14) when both components were used together. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Early Steps
entries.desc: Early Steps is a first-grade reading intervention providing one-to-one tutoring in 30-minute sessions five days a week throughout the entire school year, with tutors trained in structured lessons emphasizing re-reading, word study, and writing using leveled books. A study of 86 students in urban Tennessee found substantially positive effects on Woodcock Johnson Word Attack and Passage Comprehension scales, with an average effect size of +0.86. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Magnetic Reading Foundations
entries.desc: Magnetic Reading Foundations is a comprehensive foundational skills program for grades K–2 used 30–45 minutes daily alongside an existing reading curriculum, addressing phonological awareness, phonics, letter formation, fluency, and related competencies through digital and print materials. A quasi-experimental study of 1,615 second-grade students across 41 schools found users achieved significantly higher spring i-Ready Diagnostic scores than comparable peers (effect size = +0.15). Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: CAPIT Reading
entries.desc: CAPIT Reading is a blended supplemental phonics program for Pre-K through grade 3 that uses a Sound-to-Print approach to deliver explicit, systematic instruction designed to develop foundational literacy skills and close reading gaps. A 2023–2024 evaluation of 794 students in a Florida district found CAPIT students scored an average of 17.2 points higher on the FAST Star Early Literacy assessment (effect size = +0.19) and were more than twice as likely to meet kindergarten readiness benchmarks. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Magnetic Reading
entries.desc: Magnetic Reading is a print-based supplemental reading comprehension program for grades 3–5, used 30–45 minutes per day, that supplies culturally relevant and high-interest grade-level texts along with digital educator resources for differentiated instruction. Two studies in rural Iowa schools demonstrated positive effects on spring i-Ready Diagnostic scores and Iowa state ELA tests, with effect sizes of +0.23 and +0.22 respectively across 1,968 combined students. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: i-Ready Personalized Instruction Reading
entries.desc: i-Ready Personalized Instruction Reading uses diagnostic assessments to generate individualized online reading lessons for grades K–8, tailoring instruction to each student's performance level while providing comprehensive monitoring reports for educators. A quasi-experimental study of 2,132 fifth graders in Massachusetts found students using i-Ready had significantly higher ELA MCAS scores than the comparison group (effect size = +0.12), with stronger results for students meeting usage recommendations. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) — Reading — Elementary, Struggling Readers
entries.desc: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) – Reading – Elementary, Struggling Readers is a first-grade intervention in which children work in structured pairs, alternating teacher and learner roles to develop phonemic awareness, phonics, sound blending, passage reading, and story retelling with error-correction strategies. One qualifying study of 79 students found statistically significant positive effects on Woodcock assessment scales, with an average effect size of +0.59. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

entries.title: My Reading Academy
