At the same time, learning sciences research has advanced our understanding of learner variability and the importance of grounding educational practice and policy in the individual, rather than the fiction of an average student. To address this gap, LVP distills existing research on cognitive, social and emotional, content area, and background Learner Factors that affect learning in various domains, such as reading and math. In conjunction with the development process, LPS researchers work

entries.title: Edcamp Design for Learning Series: A New Bridge Between Research and Professional Learning
entries.desc: In the face of a worldwide pandemic, educators rose to the challenge of engaging students and families remotely to not only continue learning but also to provide as much normalcy as possible at a time of chaos and uncertainty. Many educators were looking for quick, meaningful ways to learn new skills to effectively teach remotely and connect with learners from afar. The Edcamp Community by Digital Promise saw this as an opportunity to further demonstrate how the Edcamp model of professional learning paired with learning sciences research could quickly meet the needs of educators and create spaces for shared learning and networking. The Edcamp team created the Edcamp: Design for Learning (DfL

entries.title: Certifying Competencies and Skills with Micro-credentials in a Global Context
entries.desc: In this session, we introduced our platform of competency-based micro-credentials for educators (https://microcredentials.digitalpromise.org/explore), as well as resources and best practices to support using micro-credentials to meet a variety of adult learner needs across K-12 education, higher education, and workforce contexts. We provided examples of how educational institutions can leverage micro-credentials to recognize and incentivize the implementation of quality pedagogical strategies in any learning environment. In addition, we shared an overview of Digital Promise's global education work, including a case study of how micro-credentials will be leveraged in our blended learning pilo

entries.title: Designing Gateway Statistics and Chemistry Courses for Today’s Students: Case Studies of Postsecondary Course Innovations
entries.desc: Scholars of teaching and learning examine the impacts of pedagogical decisions on students’ learning and course success. In this report, we describes findings from case studies of eight innovative postsecondary introductory statistics and general chemistry courses that have evidence of improving student completion rates for minoritized and low-income students. The goal of the case studies was to identify the course design elements and pedagogical practices that were implemented by faculty. To identify courses, Digital Promise sought nominations from experts in statistics and chemistry education and reviewed National Science Foundation project abstracts in the Improving Undergraduate STEM Edu

entries.title: Landscape of Emerging Technologies in Higher Education: A Review
entries.desc: This literature review explores the rapid integration of artificial intelligence in higher education, examining both institutional influences and instructional practices. It highlights how governance frameworks, resource allocation, and faculty attitudes shape access and responsible technology adoption. Pedagogically, the paper emphasizes the necessity of embedding AI literacy, critical evaluation, and ethical reasoning into curricula to prevent student overreliance on AI tools. Ultimately, institutions must balance innovation with accountability by carefully aligning AI tools with educational values to advance authentic learning.

entries.title: Powerful Learning with Emerging Technology
entries.desc: This paper puts forward a research-based, field-informed framework that examines both the opportunities and considerations that come with emerging technologies. The framework offers guidance to design and implement emerging technology tools in ways that are evidence-based, learner-centered, and skill-building. To develop the framework, we interviewed more than 50 experts across the field, collaborated with a working group of 10 distinguished professionals, and conducted a literature review to ground the topics that surfaced in research-based insights. These activities led to the identification and elaboration of key principles, practices and strategies to design and implement technology for

entries.title: Ambitious Mashups: Reflections on a Decade of Cyberlearning Research
entries.desc: This report reflects on progress from over eight years of research projects in the cyberlearning community. The community involved computer scientists and learning scientists who received NSF awards to investigate the design of more equitable learning experiences with emerging technology—focusing on developing the learning theories and technologies that are likely to become important within 5-10 years. In early 2020, the Center for Innovative Research in Cyberlearning's team analyzed the portfolio of past and current projects in this community, and convened a panel of experts to reflect on important trends and issues, including artificial intelligence and learning; learning theories; researc

entries.title: Learning Transition Design Principles for Learning and Employment Records: Co-designing for Equity
entries.desc: Emerging technologies such as learning, and employment records (LER) have been identified as a promising solution for historically and systematically excluded (HSE) learners and workers to share and access their learning- and skills-data from their individual learning journeys and transitions. However, learning journeys are rarely linear; the way in which learners and workers may demonstrate and get recognized for their skills and competencies may evolve over time. Further, systemic barriers and inequities embedded in the learning journeys of HSE communities, disproportionately impact their ability to enter and persist in the education and workforce ecosystem. It is critical for LERs to be d

entries.title: Making Professional Learning Count
entries.desc: This report, by the research firm Grunwald Associates, highlights the importance of effective professional development for educators and summarizes teachers’ attitudes toward competency-based professional learning.

entries.title: Digital Promise COVID-19 Student Survey Topline Data Report
entries.desc: This Digital Promise survey was conducted May 13-June 1, 2020, among a random national sample of 1,008 full- or part-time students enrolled in a two- or four-year college or university who were taking in-person or blended for-credit courses before the coronavirus outbreak began that then transitioned to remote instruction. The sample includes 620 students who took a STEM course that transitioned completely online. Results have a margin of sampling error of 3.6 points for the full sample, 4.6 points among students who took a STEM course and 5.8 points among those who did not take a STEM course. Error margins are larger for subgroups. At a 50/50 division of opinion, a difference of 8 points be

entries.title: Student Empathy Interviews: An Instrument For Considering More Inclusive K-12 Computing Pathways
entries.desc: This presentation shares the development and use of empathy interviews at Iowa City Community School District (ICCSD) which is the fifth largest school district in the state. At this district, equity challenges have arisen as the city has grown rapidly over the past decade, shifting from a rural and predominantly White college town to a burgeoning tech sector with a significant influx of Latinx families. During the first two years of participation in the Developing Inclusive K-12 Computing Pathways Research Practice Partnership (CT Pathways), the district developed a computing pathways document to guide schools and teachers in the integration of computer science (CS) and computational thinki

entries.title: A Summary and Synthesis of Initial OpenSciEd Research
entries.desc: This report summarizes and synthesizes OpenSciEd research published as of August 2022, addressing two questions about OpenSciEd: (1) To what extent do teachers enact OpenSciEd units with integrity to its distinctive principles? and (2) To what extent do OpenSciEd teacher tools and professional learning experiences support teachers to enact OpenSciEd with integrity? This review includes 16 publications (journal articles, peer-reviewed conference proceedings, conference papers, doctoral dissertations, and published reports). Five of the papers focus on the design of OpenSciEd materials and do not have an empirical focus, seven have an empirical focus on classroom enactment, and four have an em

entries.title: Innovation Starts in the Classroom
entries.desc: Part of a series of case studies produced by Digital Promise examining the work of members in our League of Innovative Schools. Click here for more info on the League. To stay up to date on future case studies, sign up for our email newsletter.

entries.title: Rubrics for Examining Historical Thinking Skills in High School World History Activities and Student Work: Construct Validity Evidence from the Literature
entries.desc: Digital Promise sought to create and validate historical thinking skills rubrics for use in its evaluation of Gates Ventures’ World History Project (WHP) curriculum. Adopting a principled assessment development approach called Evidence Centered Design (Mislevy et al., 2003), the Digital Promise team began by conducting an academic literature review. In this paper, we elaborate on how the literature on history education and historical thinking skills informed rubric design, including details of what it says about: how historical thinking skills have been defined in the literature; what dimensions of historical thinking used in national frameworks and standards; convergences of concepts across

entries.title: Tensions and Reflections on Researcher-Educator Co-design of Future-oriented Research Questions that Center Equity
entries.desc: In this presentation, we will identify the tensions and reflect on the process of co-designing future-oriented research questions that center equity with educators and researchers. Participants will learn about the value of this community-building exercise that helps envision research that is highly relevant to educational problems of practice. Three key learnings highlight (1) research question co-design is a valuable community-building exercise as well as an important way to move the work of research closer to practice, (2) the importance of including practitioner voice to acknowledge problems of practice, and (3) although the research questions generated are future-oriented they are thema

entries.title: Changing a Rural Community’s Expectations Through 24/7 Learning
entries.desc: Part of a series of case studies produced by Digital Promise examining the work of members in our League of Innovative Schools. Click here for more info on the League. To stay up to date on future case studies, sign up for our email newsletter.

entries.title: Using Research in Ed-Tech: What Does Research-based Product Development Mean?
entries.desc: This guide for educational technology developers shares examples and promising practices for research-based product development, based on an analysis of 38 submissions to a crowdsource campaign of research-based ed-tech products.

entries.title: Using Research on Homework to Improve Remote and Hybrid Learning
entries.desc: Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, many students are learning remotely or in a hybrid of remote and in-school learning. As a result, most teachers and students are in learning situations where more independent work is assigned to students. There is no research that directly speaks to this unprecedented situation. There is, however, a considerable body of established research to draw on about assigning independent work to students to do at home: research on homework. Further, technology to support homework is becoming more available and research supports its effectiveness. In this article, we review some of the major points of this established research and suggest how schools, teachers, and parent

entries.title: Rubrics for Examining Historical Thinking Skills in High School World History Activities and Student Work
entries.desc: These Historical Thinking Skills rubrics were created and validated for use in the evaluation of Gates Ventures’ World History Project (WHP) curriculum. The set of rubrics for scoring teacher lessons were designed to evaluate the potential of teacher-assigned activities (e.g., an essay prompt) to provide opportunities for students to learn historical thinking skills, while the set of rubrics for scoring student work were designed to assess the extent to which students successfully used historical thinking skills in the work these activities produced (e.g., a written essay). The categories of historical thinking skills identified for measurement are aligned with widely accepted national frame

entries.title: Enabling Analytics for Improvement: Lessons from Year 2 of Fresno’s Personalized Learning Initiative
entries.desc: Now in its second year, the Fresno Unified School District’s Personalized Learning Initiative (PLI) continues to help teachers and students develop the skills, competencies and mindsets essential for “as yet imagined” futures. A unique aspect of Fresno’s PLI is its analytics partnership between Fresno Unified, Microsoft Education, Houghton Mifflin Harcourt and Digital Promise. This report describes the early success of the PLI on students’ learning outcomes, evidence on what elements of the implementation are working, and the process and principles of the analytics partnership. The report aims to share with other education systems the lessons learned from this journey.

entries.title: AI Literacy in the Workforce
entries.desc: (This brief is part of a set sharing specific AI literacy recommendations for various audiences in education.) As artificial intelligence (AI) fundamentally reshapes career trajectories across every sector, workers face a significant transformation in the skills required for future employment. Developing AI literacy will support workers in leveraging AI in their current positions, understanding how AI may influence their tasks, and preparing them to effectively use AI tools to complement their future roles Those who do not develop AI literacy skills and knowledge before entering the workforce face the highest risk of job displacement, as they may struggle to adapt to technological advancemen

entries.title: Putting Learners First: Broadening Access Through Technology Evaluation in California Community Colleges
entries.desc: The California Community Colleges (CCC) Chancellor’s Office (CO) and Digital Promise collaborated to develop a new education technology (edtech) evaluation framework for the CO to identify edtech that supports the full spectrum of community college learners. This project centered authentic instructional needs by leveraging a co-design methodology that used working groups, design sessions, interviews, surveys, and pilots. This report offers a roadmap for higher education leaders ready to build edtech evaluation and procurement systems that center the needs of their learners and prioritize intentional investments in edtech interventions.

entries.title: El Rol de las Micro-credenciales en el Aprendizaje y el Desarrollo a lo Largo de la Vida: Empoderando Estudiantes, Empoderando Organizaciones
entries.desc: El eBook “El Rol de las Micro-credenciales en el Aprendizaje y el Desarrollo a lo Largo de la Vida: Empoderando Estudiantes, Empoderando Organizaciones” es un recurso para los interesados en comprendar micro-credenciales. El eBook se enfoca en varios aspectos importantes que son fundamentales para comprender micro-credenciales, comenzando por la definición de micro-credenciales de Digital Promise, seguida por la posición y el rol de las micro-credenciales en el ecosistema de las credenciales. También, el eBook explora el valor de las micro-credenciales para los estudiantes, las instituciones de educación postsecundaria y la fuerza laboral, las implicaciones de la IA en las micro-credenciales

entries.title: Fulfilling the Maker Promise: Year Two
entries.desc: In this report, in collaboration with Maker Ed, we share what we have learned from June 2017 – June 2018 about the “Maker Champions,” the growth of maker education in US Schools, and how these learnings are shaping the future of the Maker Promise initiative.

entries.title: Emerging Technology Responsible Use Policy Sample Language
entries.desc: This presentation includes sample and exemplar GenAI responsible and acceptable use policies (R/AUPs) that center responsibility, ethics, and equity.

entries.title: Deepening Science Engagement With Challenge Based Learning: Research Report
entries.desc: Learn about the Challenge Based Science Learning Project and its larger implications for the fields of Next Generation Science Learning and Open Educational Resources. The project involved 18 middle school teachers and five administrators from three U.S. school districts partnering with instructional coaches and learning sciences researchers from Digital Promise to address an ambitious educational challenge: How might we deepen engagement and learning of middle school science in our schools and beyond?

entries.title: Nurturing an Innovative District: Inclusive Computing Pathways in Talladega County Schools
entries.desc: This district overview highlights the work Talladega County Schools (Talladega) did over the course of three years to plan, build, and implement computing pathways. Talladega County Schools is a 7,500-student district in rural Alabama. Talladega has eleven STEAM-certified schools and 48% of all educators participate in STEAM leadership professional learning. As a member of Digital Promise’s League of Innovative Schools, Talladega applied to participate in the National Science Foundation-funded Developing Inclusive K-12 Computing Pathways for the League of Innovative Schools project to focus on developing an Inclusive K-12 Computing Pathway aligning the computing courses available within the

entries.title: Socratic Circles: Building OER Tools for Racial Equity Classroom and Community Discourse
entries.desc: This report details how Reynoldsburg City Schools utilized the Inclusive Innovation model to introduce an innovative Open Educational Resource, Socratic Circles, designed to assist teachers in guiding discussions on racial and social justice in the classroom. These OER will be accessible to educators who are inspired by this work and interested in incorporating Socratic Circles into their classrooms. This report also offers insights from members of Reynoldsburg City Schools’ Inclusive Innovation Core Team on how they applied the Core Tenets of Inclusive Innovation throughout their process.

entries.title: The Valuable Role of Edtech Coaches during the COVID-19 Pandemic: A National Survey
entries.desc: This report presents findings from a survey of edtech coaches on how their role changed when schools closed due to the COVID-19 pandemic and provides district and school leaders with insights on the value of edtech coaches in moving instruction online in effective ways.

entries.title: Understanding the Benefits of Participating in the League of Innovative Schools
entries.desc: This paper investigates the effect that participating in the League has had on K-12 public school district leaders in the network. Using a logic model to guide our methodology, our findings from surveys and interviews with district leaders show that membership in the League has provided both immediate and intermediate impacts in their professional development and in how they support the districts and schools they serve.

entries.title: Access to Powerful Technology as a Catalyst for Career Pathway Engagement
entries.desc: This paper explores the opportunities and affordances presented when historically and systemically excluded (HSE) youth gain access to high-powered technology and tools in their school environment through an innovative program. Many research studies have shown the challenges HSE youth face in accessing high-value occupations, particularly in the fields of Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM). Despite the increasing demand for STEM jobs, pathways from schools to careers in these fields often lack effectiveness, especially among HSE students. The Reinvent the Classroom initiative, a collaboration between Digital Promise, HP, Microsoft, and Intel, aims to address this pathwa

entries.title: An Initial Logic Model to Guide OpenSciEd Research: Updated Version
entries.desc: This white paper supports an ongoing effort to define a research agenda and catalyze a research community around the OpenSciEd curriculum materials. Rigorous research on these materials is needed in order to answer questions about the equitable design of instructional materials, impacts on student learning, effective and equitable classroom teaching practices, teacher professional development approaches, and models for school adoption that address the diverse needs of historically marginalized students in STEM. Research findings have the potential to advance the knowledge, skills, and practices that will promote key student, teacher, and system outcomes. The research agenda stands to acceler

entries.title: The Impact of Rhode Island's Charter School Performance Review System
entries.desc: CREDO at Stanford conducted this research at the request of the Rhode Island Department of Education to examine how the state's charter school landscape changed following implementation of the 2017 updated Charter School Performance Review System. The study addresses how the new performance framework was developed and implemented, how it changed the charter school authorization process and quality standards, and what impact it had on the performance and quality of charter schools in Rhode Island.

entries.title: National Charter School Study III
entries.desc: The National Charter School Study III is CREDO's third major national examination comparing the academic progress of students enrolled in charter schools with the progress of students enrolled in traditional public schools. Published in 2023, the study employs CREDO's established empirical evaluation methods to assess education reform initiatives and student performance outcomes at primary and secondary levels, and is presented in an interactive digital format alongside downloadable full report documents.

entries.title: Houston – 2022 City Studies
entries.desc: This CREDO report examines academic performance in public K-12 schools in Houston, Texas by analyzing one-year learning gains students made between school years during the 2018-19 growth period. The study compares Houston students' progress against state averages across charter, magnet, and district schools and disaggregates findings by school sector, race/ethnicity, poverty status, English Language Learner status, special education status, and gender. Houston charter school students—including Black and Hispanic subgroups—generally outperformed state averages in both reading and mathematics.

entries.title: Newark – 2022 City Studies
entries.desc: This CREDO research report examines academic performance in Newark, New Jersey's public K-12 schools during the 2018-19 school year by measuring one-year learning gains and comparing results against state averages across charter, magnet, and district school sectors. Newark students demonstrated stronger math growth than the state average, and Black and Hispanic students in Newark charter schools showed notably stronger gains in both subjects compared to state peers. Findings are disaggregated by school sector, charter management structure, race/ethnicity, and student population including economically disadvantaged, English Language Learners, and special education students.

entries.title: Fort Worth – 2022 City Studies
entries.desc: This CREDO report analyzes public K-12 school performance in Fort Worth, Texas during the 2018-19 growth period by measuring one-year learning gains and benchmarking results against state averages. Students overall demonstrated weaker learning gains in reading and similar gains in math compared to the state average, with charter schools outperforming district schools in reading. Findings are disaggregated by race/ethnicity, poverty status, English Language Learner status, special education status, and gender, with English Language Learners in charter schools outperforming both state and district counterparts in reading.

entries.title: Austin – 2022 City Studies
entries.desc: CREDO at Stanford conducted a comprehensive analysis of public school performance in Austin, Texas during the 2018-19 academic period, examining one-year learning gains across charter and district schools and benchmarking against state averages. Austin students demonstrated stronger academic progress in both reading and mathematics compared to statewide averages, with Charter Management Organization-affiliated charter schools outpacing state performance in both subjects. Findings are disaggregated by race/ethnicity, socioeconomic status, English Language Learner status, disability status, and gender.

entries.title: City Studies: Project Meta-Analysis
entries.desc: CREDO at Stanford's City Studies Project Meta-Analysis provides periodic reports of academic performance for public K-12 schools across selected cities in the United States, offering a foundation for informed evaluation and constructive discussion among stakeholders invested in public education. The project covers two cohorts: Cohort 1 includes ten urban centers (Baton Rouge, Camden, Washington D.C., Denver, Indianapolis, Kansas City, Memphis, New Orleans, San Antonio, and St. Louis) and Cohort 2 covers Austin, Fort Worth, Houston, and Newark. Meta-analysis PDFs are available for both cohorts alongside individual city reports.

entries.title: 2023 Pace of Learning Report
entries.desc: This CREDO report examines solutions to pandemic-related learning losses in K-12 education, presenting a meta-analysis covering sixteen states and offering empirically grounded estimates for student learning recovery. The analysis argues that conventional interventions focused solely on increased instructional time fail to account for individual student learning rates and their variability, and that addressing the pace of learning itself offers greater potential than simply extending teaching time. National Assessment of Educational Progress results showed regression comparable to pre-NCLB performance levels, underscoring the severity of learning loss across many communities.

entries.title: Charter School Performance in Rhode Island – 2023
entries.desc: Published in January 2023, this CREDO analysis examines charter school performance in Rhode Island, serving as a benchmark study during the COVID-19 recovery period. The report includes student demographic descriptions, achievement trajectories for charter school students, and charter school impact assessments covering the full sample, school level breakdowns, school quality curve analysis, and findings disaggregated by student subgroups. The findings are intended to establish a foundation for future studies in charter school evaluation and performance measurement within Rhode Island's education system.

entries.title: Evaluating the Impact of a Mathematics Mastery Learning Platform on Student Achievement
entries.desc: Examines the impact of a mathematics mastery learning platform used by over 500,000 U.S. students in grades 3-8 using multi-year longitudinal panel data. The study improved on prior cross-sectional research by treating each student as their own control to eliminate confounding from time-invariant factors, replicating positive findings on platform usage and test scores. Positive treatment effects did not vary across demographic subgroups, and the authors discuss opportunities for enhanced measurement specificity regarding platform effectiveness. Published in EC-TEL 2024 (Technology Enhanced Learning for Inclusive and Equitable Quality Education); authors include Kodi Weatherholtz, Phillip Grimaldi, Bogdan Yamkovenko, and Kelli Millwood Hill.

entries.title: GADRRS: Guidance and Agreement for Data Harmony, Responsibility, Retention, and Sharing
entries.desc: GADRRS is a universal data sharing agreement developed by the UF Lastinger Center in partnership with the AIMS Collaboratory, offering standardized terms for educational data exchanges among schools, districts, researchers, and education technology providers. The framework prioritizes student privacy by establishing protocols for data security and responsible usage, and aims to increase transparency, establish consistent safety protocols, minimize administrative burden, and expand availability of anonymized datasets for research. The agreement has been reviewed by University of Florida legal counsel and enables organizations to make deliberate choices about data access authorization while maintaining rigorous privacy standards.

entries.title: Motivation and Engagement in the Mathematics Classroom with Kyron Learning
entries.desc: Reports findings from a 2024-2025 school year study evaluating whether AI-powered instruction by Kyron Learning could enhance motivation and engagement among middle school students, with particular focus on English Language Learners. The research involved 22 teachers and nearly 1,000 students across 17 schools, employing a four-cycle rapid iteration design with teacher-created interactive lessons compared against traditional instruction. Quantitatively, student math self-perceptions reached statistical significance favoring Kyron in the final cycle; qualitatively, teachers noted stronger emotional engagement and ELL students benefited from features like pausing, replaying, closed captioning, and vocabulary definitions.

entries.title: What Kids Have to Say About Math: Results from Focus Groups with Middle Schoolers
entries.desc: Examines middle school students' perspectives on mathematics education through focus groups with 39 students at two urban Baton Rouge schools conducted in spring 2025. Key findings show students valued their teachers highly as central to motivation, but test-related anxiety presented a significant obstacle, and many students preferred hands-on, teacher-led approaches over digital tools. Students identified perceiving real-world relevance and career connections as the strongest motivator, and advocated for normalizing mistakes, enabling grade recovery, and clarifying learning objectives. Authors: H. Colleen Sinclair, Kyle Wilson, Avery Ellis, and Norma Martinez (Louisiana State University at Baton Rouge), 2026.

entries.title: Informing Change: A Final Research Report on the Findings from the Longitudinal R&D Partnership for Math Equity Project
entries.desc: Reports findings from a three-year collaborative study between LSU's Social Research and Evaluation Center, East Baton Rouge Parish Schools, and DreamBox Learning examining the effectiveness of DreamBox's technology-based math intervention on student performance. The research addressed three key questions developed with Gates Foundation guidance through longitudinal, multi-method research combining quantitative analysis with qualitative insights from teacher and student focus groups. Published January 2026; authors include H. Colleen Sinclair, Samuel Robison, Youn Kyoung Kim, Stacey R. Terrio, and Judith Lee Falgout Rhodes.

entries.title: Improving School Discipline, Climate, and Culture: Interactive Guidance for Schools and Districts
entries.desc: An interactive guidance resource helping K-12 school administrators and district leaders foster better educational environments by reducing punitive discipline approaches and building preventive systems. The guide encourages users to think critically about school discipline and invest in data-driven, prevention-oriented systems that address problematic student behaviors while improving school climate and culture. Published June 2024 by Gwendolyn Murray at Louisiana State University at Baton Rouge.

entries.title: Longitudinal Analysis of Gender Disparities in Mathematics Achievement among Elementary Students
entries.desc: Investigates how gender differences in mathematics performance develop and change throughout elementary and middle school, analyzing five years of student assessment data across two student cohorts. The research finds that female students often begin elementary school with a modest advantage in mathematics that diminishes as grade levels increase, with gender gaps varying based on district characteristics, student subgroups, and prior achievement rather than following fixed patterns. The author concludes that sustained instructional support and equitable classroom conditions are important for addressing disparities across the elementary through middle school years. Published February 18, 2026 by Kadir Bahar via the Northwestern E4 research brief series.

entries.title: School-Based Law Enforcement Strategies to Reduce Crime, Increase Perceptions of Safety, and Improve Learning Outcomes in Primary and Secondary Schools
entries.desc: This Campbell Collaboration systematic review examines the relationship between school-based law enforcement (SBLE) presence and school outcomes. Analysis of quantitative studies found that schools with SBLE had higher rates of crime and behavior problems than schools without SBLE, driven primarily by increased exclusionary discipline rather than improvements in actual safety. The review concludes that SBLE appears ineffective for maintaining school safety and may produce harmful consequences for students.

entries.title: The Effects of Small Class Sizes on Students' Academic Achievement, Socioemotional Development and Well-Being in Special Education
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review examined the impact of small class sizes in special education on student outcomes, identifying only seven studies across quantitative, qualitative, and mixed-methods designs. Smaller class sizes are the most preferred option among students with special educational needs, their teachers, and school principals due to opportunities for individualized instruction and increased teacher attention. The authors conclude that the impact of small class sizes in special education is under-researched and call for additional research to inform practitioner guidance.

entries.title: The Effects of Flipped Classrooms to Improve Learning Outcomes in Undergraduate Health Professional Education
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review examined 45 studies (11 RCTs) involving 8,426 undergraduate health professional students to assess flipped classroom effectiveness. Findings indicate low certainty of evidence suggesting flipped classrooms, which combine pre-recorded lectures with interactive on-campus sessions, may enhance academic performance and student satisfaction compared to traditional lecture-based instruction. Well-designed studies with larger sample sizes are needed for more rigorous evaluation.

entries.title: The Effects of Inclusion on Academic Achievement, Socioemotional Development and Wellbeing of Children with Special Educational Needs
entries.desc: This Campbell Collaboration systematic review and meta-analysis examined 15 non-randomized studies to evaluate whether placing children with special needs in inclusive educational settings versus segregated settings affects their academic achievement and psychosocial adjustment. Key findings indicate that inclusion neither increases nor decreases learning and psychosocial adjustment on average, with effect sizes varying considerably across studies. The authors conclude that placement decisions should be individualized rather than following a one-size-fits-all approach.

entries.title: Evidence-Informed vs Evidence-Based Practice Educational Interventions for Improving Knowledge, Attitudes, Understanding and Behaviour Among Undergraduate Students
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review examined the effectiveness of evidence-informed versus evidence-based practice educational interventions for undergraduate health and social care students. The review screened 46 full-text articles but identified no studies examining the relative effectiveness of the two approaches, making it impossible to determine which better facilitates evidence application in healthcare practice. The search covered publications from 1996 through June 2019 across all languages.

entries.title: Single-Track Year-Round Education for Improving Academic Achievement in US K–12 Schools
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review synthesized 30 studies comparing student performance in single-track year-round education schools versus traditional-calendar schools. Average student achievement was higher in both reading and math at year-round schools, with gains roughly equivalent to one month of learning, similar to documented summer learning loss. Schools with shorter summer breaks of four to six weeks demonstrated larger achievement improvements than those with seven to eight week breaks.

entries.title: Recovery Schools for Improving Behavioral and Academic Outcomes Among Students in Recovery from Substance Use Disorders
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review examined the effects of recovery schools on student behavioral and academic outcomes, including one quasi-experimental study of recovery high schools with 194 participants. Findings suggest recovery high schools may reduce students' school absenteeism, marijuana use, and other drug use, though they showed no clear advantage for grades, truancy, or alcohol use. The authors concluded there is insufficient evidence to determine effectiveness and recommend additional rigorous evaluations prior to widespread implementation.

entries.title: What Are the Effects of Teach For America on Math, English Language Arts, and Science Outcomes of K–12 Students in the USA?
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review examined how Teach For America-prepared educators impact student learning across mathematics, English language arts, and science in U.S. K-12 schools. From 919 initial citations, only four studies met rigorous methodological standards; these found no significant reading improvements from first- or second-year TFA elementary teachers versus comparable non-TFA novices, though a modest positive effect emerged for early elementary reading. The authors conclude that insufficient high-quality evidence exists to draw firm policy conclusions about TFA's effectiveness.

entries.title: E-Learning of Evidence-Based Healthcare to Increase Competencies in Healthcare Professionals
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review examined the effectiveness of e-learning in improving evidence-based healthcare knowledge and practice among healthcare professionals, including 24 trials with 3,825 participants across various disciplines. Pure e-learning improves knowledge and skills but not attitudes and behavior compared to no learning, while showing no advantage over face-to-face approaches; however, blended learning outperforms no learning across all outcomes and surpasses face-to-face learning in improving attitudes and behavior. The authors recommend future research focus on specific e-learning components and comprehensive intervention reporting.

entries.title: Parental, Community and Familial Support Interventions to Improve Children's Literacy in Developing Countries
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review examined 13 studies assessing interventions involving parents, families, or community members to improve literacy for children aged 3-12 in developing countries. Parent training and child-to-child tutoring are not effective, with eight of nine outcomes showing no significant results; however, educational television appears to improve literacy with frequent viewing at three to five times weekly over several months. The review identifies substantial gaps in empirical evidence for most literacy models implemented by governments and NGOs globally.

entries.title: Early Intensive Behavioral Intervention (EIBI) for Young Children with Autism Spectrum Disorders
entries.desc: This systematic review examined early intensive behavioral intervention involving 20-40 hours weekly of applied behavior analysis for young children with autism, analyzing one RCT and four clinical control trials totaling 203 participants. Results showed positive effects across adaptive behavior, IQ, expressive and receptive language, socialization, and daily living skills, with effect sizes ranging from g=0.42 to g=0.76. However, the current state of evidence is limited due to reliance on non-randomized studies and was rated as low quality, indicating additional randomized trials are needed.

entries.title: School-Based Interventions to Reduce Dating and Sexual Violence
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review examined 23 studies on school-based programs (grades 6-12) designed to prevent teen dating and sexual violence. While interventions showed moderate improvements in knowledge about dating violence and more accepting attitudes toward conflict resolution, they have little impact on actual behavior change. The review concludes that existing programs require redesign to better support behavioral change and recommends future research emphasize measuring behaviors alongside attitudes and expand beyond North America.

entries.title: Relative Effectiveness of Conditional and Unconditional Cash Transfers for Schooling Outcomes in Developing Countries
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review evaluated 35 studies examining conditional and unconditional cash transfer programmes' effects on education outcomes in low- and middle-income countries. Both program types increase school enrollment compared to no intervention, though effects on learning outcomes remain limited. The analysis reveals that conditions that are strictly monitored and enforced produce substantially larger enrollment gains, increasing odds by 60% versus under 20% for programmes without conditions.

entries.title: Pre-Graduation Transition Services for Persons with Autism Spectrum Disorders: Effects on Employment Outcomes
entries.desc: This systematic review examined pre-graduation interventions for secondary school-age individuals (ages 14-22) with autism spectrum disorder designed to facilitate competitive employment. After searching 30 databases covering 1943-2011 and narrowing 5,665 citations to 85 full-text reviews, no studies were identified that met all inclusion criteria. The authors concluded that despite regulations supporting transition programming, rigorous experimental research on employment intervention effectiveness remains scarce and urgently needed.

entries.title: Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) Interventions to Improve the Employability and Employment of Young People in Low- and Middle-Income Countries
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review examined 26 studies across multiple regions to assess whether TVET interventions enhanced employment outcomes for youth aged 15-24 in developing nations. TVET interventions have a small but positive effect on all but one of the employment outcomes measured, though considerable variation existed between studies, with lower-quality research showing inflated effects. The review emphasizes the need for more rigorous future program evaluations and notes that selecting affordable and culturally suitable models may be prudent absent stronger comparative evidence.

entries.title: Interventions in Developing Nations for Improving Primary and Secondary School Enrollments
entries.desc: This systematic review examined 73 randomized controlled trials and quasi-experiments published through December 2009 to assess interventions in low- and middle-income countries on student enrollment, attendance, graduation, and progression. Results showed positive effects across all outcomes, with enrollment showing the largest average effect size (d=.18) and infrastructure interventions for new schools demonstrating the strongest results (d=.44). Effects were modest, representing three to nine percent increases in positive outcomes, and policymakers should evaluate whether such gains justify implementation costs.

entries.title: Social Skills Groups for People Aged 6 to 21 with Autism Spectrum Disorders
entries.desc: This systematic review examined five randomized controlled trials involving 196 participants to evaluate whether social skills groups help individuals aged 6-21 with autism spectrum disorder. Findings indicate some evidence that social skills groups improve overall social competence and friendship quality, though no significant improvements were found in emotion recognition, understanding of idioms, or most quality-of-life outcomes. The authors conclude that while social skills groups show promise for enhancing social competence, additional rigorous research is needed to establish more definitive conclusions.

entries.title: School Feeding for Improving the Physical and Psychosocial Health of Disadvantaged Students
entries.desc: This systematic review evaluated whether school feeding programs enhance health outcomes for economically disadvantaged pupils, analyzing 18 studies examining weight gain, height, school attendance, and academic achievement. Results indicated modest positive effects, particularly in lower-income countries where children gained approximately 0.39-0.71 kg over study periods and showed improved attendance and math performance. The reviewers concluded that school meals may provide small benefits and recommended additional well-designed research with standardized outcome reporting.

entries.title: The Effects of School-Based Social Information Processing Interventions on Aggressive Behavior, Part I: Universal Programs
entries.desc: This systematic review analyzed 73 studies of universal classroom programs aimed at improving children's ability to interpret social situations to reduce aggressive and disruptive behaviour. Findings indicate that brief, intensive interventions of 8-16 weeks prove more effective than extended year-long programs, with social information processing interventions training children through six cognitive stages from encoding cues to behavioral enactment. The research demonstrates that emphasizing thought patterns rather than direct behavior modification strengthens children's general social competencies and reduces aggression across diverse populations.

entries.title: The Effects of School-Based Social Information Processing Interventions on Aggressive Behavior, Part II: Selected/Indicated Pull-Out Programs
entries.desc: This Campbell systematic review examined school-based education programmes targeting children's ability to interpret social situations to reduce aggressive behaviour, covering 47 studies of interventions delivered to selected children with behavioural problems or at-risk populations. Students who participated exhibited notably reduced aggressive and disruptive conduct compared to non-participants, with particularly strong effects for pull-out programmes serving at-risk groups outside regular classrooms. This companion review to Part I focuses specifically on targeted interventions rather than universal programs and demonstrates stronger effects for higher-risk populations.

entries.title: Understanding 5Essentials School Averages and School Climate Malleability
entries.desc: Published May 2026. Examines the extent to which school climate is responsive to improvement efforts, the interrelationships among all five essentials, and how likely the essentials are to improve over time. Also investigates how well school averages represent the range of student experiences within Chicago Public Schools.

entries.title: Research Agenda 2026–30
entries.desc: Published April 2026. Sets forth a five-year research agenda continuing some Consortium studies and outlining new investigations, reflecting stakeholder input and emphasizing research that is collaborative, responsive, and rooted in the communities served. The document outlines five priority focus areas for investigations into Chicago's educational system and commits to specific methodological approaches.

entries.title: Combining Early Grade Assessments to Study Literacy Skills
entries.desc: Published April 2026. Addresses considerable variability in literacy assessments taken in Kindergarten through second grade across schools, between multilingual learners and other students, and within students over time. Researchers examined several popular early grade assessments, developed latent scores of literacy development, and found that each assessment provides predictive information toward third-grade literacy outcomes with considerable overlap, enabling the production of metrics for studying school and district policies around literacy development.

entries.title: Supports for Centering Student Experience
entries.desc: Published March 2026. A field scan examining how educators utilize student experience data, identifying three key topics: shifting adult mindsets in shaping the use of student experience data, engaging students in data analysis, and fostering alignment across systems. Participants acknowledged technical and cultural challenges while expressing optimism about improvement potential through this work.

entries.title: Co-Designing New Approaches to School Safety
entries.desc: Published March 2026. A field scan capturing experiences of Chicago Public School officials and Community-Based Organization leaders who served on the district's Whole School Safety Steering Committee and co-developed the Whole School Safety Framework between 2020 and 2024. Community leaders viewed the co-design process as healing and restorative, finding that the framework helped ground safety plans in individual communities' perspectives, facilitated power-sharing, and supported the reimagining of safety with impact measured across student and family engagement and outcomes.

entries.title: Connection, Trust, and Learning: Student Attendance in the Middle and High School Grades Following the COVID-19 Pandemic
entries.desc: Published January 2026. Examines absenteeism in Chicago among students in grades 6–11, comparing post-pandemic to pre-pandemic years, finding that absenteeism increased significantly following COVID-19 while students' academic achievement remained strongly related to their attendance. Notably, school climate is strongly associated with attendance, and the connection is even stronger today than before the COVID-19 pandemic.

entries.title: Illinois High School to Career Series: Precarious Prospects and Promising Pathways
entries.desc: Published December 2025. Tracks Illinois high school seniors from 2008–2012 through post-secondary education and into the workforce, examining demographic characteristics, degree attainment, and career earnings variation by household income level. Finds that while higher education correlates with higher earnings across all backgrounds, students from lower-income families earned less than peers with identical credentials, and that some sub-baccalaureate pathways led to good jobs — particularly benefiting men.

entries.title: The Educational Attainment of Chicago Public Schools Students (2024)
entries.desc: Published December 2025. Annual report examining how CPS students progress toward and through high school and college, tracking three key milestones: high school graduation, college enrollment, and college completion. Calculates an updated Post-secondary Attainment Index representing the proportion of current CPS ninth-graders who would complete any degree or certificate from a two-year or four-year college within ten years if current attainment rates held constant.

entries.title: Parent Advisory Councils
entries.desc: Published November 2025. Reviews what is currently known about parent councils in the United States — both their implementation and impact — in order to provide parents, educators, and family engagement practitioners with guidance on building better parent councils. Synthesizes existing research to inform how councils can be structured and supported for greater effectiveness.

entries.title: Using Data to Improve Schools
entries.desc: Published November 2025. Addresses common myths and assumptions that divert attention from the data that matter most, and provides guidance on using systems for continuous improvement such as Early Warning Indicator (EWI) and Multi-Tiered Systems of Support (MTSS). Explains how schools can improve key performance areas — including attendance, test scores, and graduation rates — by identifying high-impact metrics and fostering a collaborative culture of improvement.

entries.title: Organizing Schools for Improvement: Lessons from Chicago
entries.desc: Published January 2010. Provides a detailed analysis of why students in 100 public elementary schools in Chicago substantially improved in reading and math over a seven-year period while students in another 100 schools did not. Identifies five essential supports for improvement — school leadership, professional capacity, parent-community ties, student-centered learning climate, and instructional guidance — and examines how community context shapes school improvement efforts.

entries.title: Foundations for Young Adult Success: A Developmental Framework
entries.desc: Published June 2015. Draws on wide-ranging evidence to show what young people need to develop from preschool to young adulthood to succeed in college and career, have healthy relationships, be engaged citizens, and make wise choices. Finds that rich experiences combining action and reflection help children develop critical skills, attitudes, and behaviors, while emphasizing that policies should ensure all children have consistent, supportive relationships — particularly for disadvantaged youth who face significant opportunity gaps.

entries.title: Supporting Social, Emotional, and Academic Development: Research Implications for Educators
entries.desc: Published October 2018. Designed to help teachers and principals support equitable outcomes for all students by synthesizing UChicago Consortium research on school climate's influence on student achievement alongside other education research. Emphasizes the critical role of engagement and mindsets in student success and examines how educators can create strong school climates that support students, engage families as partners, and enable all students to have strong academic engagement.

entries.title: Predictive Power of Grades and Other Indicators for English Learners
entries.desc: Published July 2025. Examines how various indicators predict high school graduation and college outcomes for different groups of English Learners who entered ninth grade between 2008–2017 in Chicago Public Schools, challenging the field's reliance on standardized test scores. Finds that GPAs were the most predictive of high school graduation, college enrollment, and degree completion for all English Learner groups, consistent with findings for non-English Learners.

entries.title: Variability in the Grades Teachers Give: Teacher Grade Value-Add, Mismatch, and Long-Term Effects
entries.desc: Published June 2025. Examines systematic differences in ninth-grade teachers' effects on students' course grades and their lasting influence on academic outcomes, finding that teacher effects are stable across years and predictive of students' long-term outcomes including eleventh-grade ACT scores, graduating GPA, and high school graduation. The analysis reveals that persistent effects from ninth-grade teachers correlate with long-term academic benefits, though the relationship between grading mismatches and outcomes shows more complex patterns.

entries.title: Disparate Teacher Effects, Comparative Advantage, and Match Quality
entries.desc: Published June 2025. Addresses whether teacher impacts vary across student subgroups and how this knowledge could enhance policy effectiveness, developing a new analytical approach introducing "revealed comparative advantage" as a measure capturing how teachers affect outcome gaps between groups. The methodological framework has implications for both policy efficiency and equity in education, showing that a teacher's comparative advantage for one subgroup can yield substantial test score improvements for that group with no effect on others.

entries.title: Using the Freshman On-Track Indicator to Improve High Schools in Chicago
entries.desc: Published April 2025. Explains how Chicago Public Schools improved graduation rates through an iterative research-policy-practice process over fifteen years, centering on the Freshman OnTrack indicator that signals a student's likelihood of graduating and is used for goal-setting and accountability. The system includes real-time data reports, diagnostic tools, research findings, and staff support structures designed to keep ninth-grade students on track for high school completion.

entries.title: How Did Chicago Connected Broadband Access Affect Remote Learning During COVID-19?
entries.desc: Published March 2025. Examined the educational effects of Chicago Connected, a broadband internet expansion initiative, analyzing whether eligibility for and participation in the program affected engagement in remote learning during fall 2020 and GPAs in fall 2020 and spring 2021 among grades 5–8 students. Provides evidence on how home internet access shaped student academic outcomes during the COVID-19 pandemic's shift to remote instruction.

entries.title: Student Experiences through the COVID-19 Pandemic: Attendance, Online Learning Platform Use, and Annual Survey Reports
entries.desc: Published February 2025. Examines student experiences in Chicago Public Schools across grades K–12 during the remote/hybrid school year (2020–21) and two subsequent recovery years through 2022–23, addressing attendance patterns, online learning platform engagement, and 5Essentials Survey results measuring school relationships and academic engagement. Documents changes in instructional time, student-teacher relationships, social skills, perseverance, and perceived support systems during the pandemic disruption.

entries.title: Early Insights from the Revalidation of the Cultivate Survey
entries.desc: Published January 2025. Examines whether the Cultivate Survey accurately and reliably measures students' experiences of learning conditions in Chicago Public Schools and whether improvements in those conditions predict increases in students' sense of feeling valued. Finds the survey reliably captures learning conditions across all student groups without bias, and that improving students' experiences of their learning conditions from fall to spring predicts substantial and positive changes in how valued students feel.

entries.title: English Learners in Chicago Public Schools: A Spotlight on High School Students
entries.desc: Published November 2024. Examines high school outcomes, college enrollment, and two-year persistence rates for English Learners in Chicago Public Schools, distinguishing between students who remained English Learners in high school and those who previously held that classification. Finds that formerly classified English Learners demonstrated higher-than-average outcomes in GPA, SAT scores, and college persistence, while long-term English Learners faced greater academic challenges and late-arriving English Learners showed mixed results.

entries.title: The Educational Attainment of Chicago Public Schools Students (2023)
entries.desc: Published October 2024. Annual report tracking how CPS students progress on the path to and through high school and college, examining high school graduation, college enrollment, and college completion milestones and tracking how rates have changed across time. Calculates a Post-secondary Attainment Index representing the proportion of current CPS ninth-graders projected to complete any degree or certificate from a two-year or four-year college within ten years.

entries.title: Measuring Patterns of Immediate and Continued College Enrollment among CPS Graduates
entries.desc: Published October 2024. Examines the To&Through Project's definitions and measurement approaches for college enrollment and persistence metrics for Chicago Public Schools graduates, clarifying how immediate college enrollment is defined and how college persistence rates are calculated. Reports rates for both associate and bachelor's degree institutions and explains methodological updates to ensure accurate tracking of student pathways through post-secondary education.

entries.title: Removing Police Officers from Chicago Schools: Trends and Outcomes
entries.desc: Published June 2024. Examines what happened when Chicago Public Schools began removing School Resource Officers from its high schools during the 2020–21 school year, finding that schools removed SROs without detrimental changes in student disciplinary outcomes or perceptions of school climate. Research was conducted by investigators from the UChicago Consortium, University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, and Lurie Children's Hospital's Center for Childhood Resilience.

entries.title: Mitigating the Academic Impacts of Proximity to Homicide: The Role of Schools
entries.desc: Published June 2024. Examines how community violence affects Chicago Public Schools students and whether schools can protect them from these impacts, demonstrating that schools can and do mitigate the negative impacts of adversity that young people experience. Key findings reveal that proximity to homicide negatively affected academic performance, but schools with strong climates featuring engaging instruction and trusting relationships helped counteract these effects.

entries.title: Who Benefits From Attending Effective Schools? Examining Heterogeneity in High School Impacts
entries.desc: Published May 2024. Examines how students with different educational backgrounds benefit from attending effective high schools, finding that all students benefit but the least advantaged students experience the largest improvements in high school graduation, college-going, and school-based arrests. Highlights that less advantaged students gain the most from schools' non-test-score dimensions of quality, though more advantaged students are disproportionately likely to attend the most effective schools.

entries.title: The Four Years Fallacy: Four-Year vs. Six-Year Bachelor's Degree Completion Rates
entries.desc: Published December 2023. Joint study by the UChicago Consortium and the To&Through Project examining important differences between four-year and six-year completion rates for Chicago Public Schools graduates who received bachelor's degrees, investigating completion trends for 2004–14 CPS graduates across different colleges, high schools, student GPAs, ACT scores, and demographic groups. Finds that while only 30% of CPS graduates completed degrees within four years, this rate increased to 51% when measured over six years — with significant implications for how institutions and policymakers measure college success.

entries.title: Family Playbook: Effective Principal Leadership
entries.desc: Published June 2025. Helps parents and community leaders better understand what great principals do and how to work together to build strong, supportive school communities, addressing what research says effective principals do, how parents can work with principals, and how to use data to learn more about their school. Includes supplementary materials for Local School Council members on principal hiring, evaluation, and support.

entries.title: Family Playbook: How Can We Understand Students' Progress in School?
entries.desc: Published March 2025. Provides families with key information and research to support informed decision-making, emphasizing that grades and attendance from pre-K through 12th grade are the most important ingredients for students' success in and after high school. Addresses what research indicates about grades, attendance, standardized tests, and on-track metrics, and how families can collaborate with Chicago Public Schools educators to access and act on this information.

entries.title: Family Playbook: School Suspensions, Discipline and Restorative Justice
entries.desc: Published June 2024. Helps parents understand discipline data and research, covering how suspensions impact all students, disparities in suspension rates, and the benefits of restorative justice approaches in schools. Provides concrete actions parents can take to learn more about their child's school's approach to school discipline and school climate.

entries.title: Accelerated Reader
entries.desc: Accelerated Reader is a computerized supplementary reading tool for grades 1–8 in which students select books based on interest and reading level and then take computerized comprehension quizzes. The WWC reviewed 2 studies that met standards out of 11 eligible studies. The program was found to have mixed effects on comprehension and no discernible effects on reading fluency for beginning readers.

entries.title: Accelerated Reader (English Language Learners)
entries.desc: This WWC intervention report examines Accelerated Reader, a computerized supplementary reading program for grades K–12, within the scope of the English Language Learners review protocol. No studies of the program met WWC evidence standards for this population. Consequently, the WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness for English learners.

entries.title: AEGIS (Acquiring Ethical Guidelines for Individual Self Governance)
entries.desc: AEGIS is a K–6 character education program that teaches ethical behavior through seven core concepts—including worth and potential, fairness and justice, and personal integrity—delivered via 35 integrated curriculum lessons using a five-step instructional process. As of June 2007, the WWC found no studies meeting its design standards and was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness.

entries.title: Active Early Learning Kit for Pre-K by Steck-Vaughn
entries.desc: This WWC report covers the Active Early Learning Kit for Pre-K by Steck-Vaughn, an early childhood education intervention. As of December 2005, no studies of this program fell within the scope of the Early Childhood Education review protocol and met WWC design standards. The WWC therefore could not draw research-based conclusions regarding the intervention's effectiveness or ineffectiveness.

entries.title: Active Learning (Early Childhood)
entries.desc: This WWC report examines Active Learning as an early childhood education intervention. No studies meeting WWC design standards were found as of December 2005. The organization could not draw any research-based conclusions about the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of Active Learning for improving early childhood outcomes.

entries.title: Addison-Wesley Mathematics Basal Program
entries.desc: The Addison-Wesley Mathematics basal program is a middle school mathematics curriculum reviewed by the WWC. As of July 2007, no studies of this program fell within the scope of the Middle School Math review protocol and met WWC design standards. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness for improving middle school mathematics outcomes.

entries.title: AVID (Advancement via Individual Determination)
entries.desc: AVID (Advancement via Individual Determination) is a college-readiness initiative for grades 9–12 that places middle-achieving students in advanced courses with academic support, including daily elective tutoring. The WWC reviewed 1 qualifying study out of 8 eligible studies under the Adolescent Literacy protocol. The program was found to have no discernible effects on reading comprehension for adolescent learners.

entries.title: Adventures of Jasper Woodbury Series
entries.desc: The Adventures of Jasper Woodbury Series is a videodisc-based mathematics curriculum for grade 5 and above, consisting of 12 problem-based adventures aligned with NCTM standards and connecting to science, social studies, and literature. The WWC reviewed this program under the Elementary School Mathematics protocol and found no qualifying studies as of July 2007. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness.

entries.title: ALAS (Achievement for Latinos through Academic Success)
entries.desc: ALAS (Achievement for Latinos through Academic Success) is a dropout prevention program for grades 7–9 that assigns each student a counselor/mentor who monitors attendance, behavior, and grades while providing student training in problem-solving and parent training in school engagement. One qualifying study was reviewed, showing potentially positive effects on both staying in school and progressing in school through ninth grade. The sample was 96% Hispanic, 91% receiving free/reduced-price lunch, and 23% English learners in an urban California setting.

entries.title: Algebra Project
entries.desc: The Algebra Project is a middle school mathematics curriculum reviewed by the WWC under the Middle School Math protocol. As of July 2007, no studies of the program were found that met WWC design standards within the scope of the review. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness for improving middle school mathematics outcomes.

entries.title: Algebraic Thinking
entries.desc: Algebraic Thinking is a middle school mathematics intervention reviewed under the WWC Middle School Math protocol. As of July 2007, no qualifying studies were found meeting WWC design standards. The WWC stated it was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of this intervention for improving middle school math outcomes.

entries.title: Alpha-Time
entries.desc: Alpha-Time is a beginning reading intervention reviewed under the WWC Beginning Reading protocol. As of July 2007, no studies of this program were found that met WWC design standards. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about Alpha-Time's effectiveness or ineffectiveness for improving beginning reading outcomes.

entries.title: Alphabetic Phonics
entries.desc: Alphabetic Phonics is a multisensory reading curriculum targeting students with specific learning disabilities, using simultaneous visual, auditory, and kinesthetic instruction across ten daily activities per one-hour session. The program covers reading, handwriting, spelling, verbal and written expression, and comprehension for elementary and secondary students. As of July 2010, no studies met WWC standards under the Students with a Specific Learning Disability review protocol, preventing any conclusions about effectiveness.

entries.title: AlphabiTunes
entries.desc: AlphabiTunes is an early elementary beginning reading intervention reviewed under the WWC Beginning Reading protocol. No qualifying studies were found as of July 2007. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness for improving beginning reading outcomes.

entries.title: Als speciale kleuter tel je ook me! (Young Children with Special Needs Count, Too!)
entries.desc: Als speciale kleuter tel je ook me! (Young Children with Special Needs Count, Too!) is an elementary school mathematics intervention reviewed by the WWC. No qualifying studies were found as of July 2007 within the Elementary School Mathematics review protocol. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness.

entries.title: America's Choice (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: America's Choice is a school reform program reviewed under the WWC Beginning Reading protocol. As of July 2007, no studies met WWC design standards within the scope of that review protocol. The WWC was therefore unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness for improving beginning reading outcomes.

entries.title: Appalachia Model Mathematics Program
entries.desc: The Appalachia Model Mathematics Program is an elementary school mathematics intervention reviewed by the WWC. As of July 2007, no studies of this program were found within the scope of the Elementary School Mathematics review protocol that met WWC design standards. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness.

entries.title: Arthur (English Language Development)
entries.desc: Arthur is a book-based educational television program for ages 4–8 featuring an 8-year-old aardvark character in 30-minute episodes addressing moral issues, used as a listening comprehension and language development tool for English language learners. One qualifying study was reviewed for kindergarten ELL students (100% English learners, 80% free/reduced-price lunch, urban setting), finding potentially positive effects on English language development. The program received a Tier 3 (Promising) evidence rating under the WWC English Language Learners protocol.

entries.title: Athens Tutorial Program
entries.desc: The Athens Tutorial Program is a beginning reading intervention reviewed by the WWC. As of July 2007, no studies of this program were found within the scope of the Beginning Reading review protocol meeting WWC design standards. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness for improving beginning reading outcomes.

entries.title: ATTLAS (Augmenting Thinking Through Language Acquisition Skills)
entries.desc: ATTLAS (Augmenting Thinking Through Language Acquisition Skills) is an English language learner intervention reviewed under the WWC English Language Learners protocol. As of July 2007, no studies of this program met WWC design standards within the scope of the review. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness for improving outcomes for English learners.

entries.title: Concept-Oriented Reading Instruction (CORI) — Adolescent Literacy
entries.desc: Concept-Oriented Reading Instruction (CORI) is a grades 3–9 program that integrates reading and science instruction through activities and science books, targeting reading comprehension and reading engagement. This WWC report covers CORI under the Adolescent Literacy review protocol; as of August 2010, no qualifying studies were found that met WWC design standards. A separate WWC review of CORI under the Beginning Reading protocol also found no qualifying studies.

entries.title: Connect with Kids
entries.desc: Connect with Kids is a character education program for grades 3–12 that uses videos, discussion questions, activities, and a website to promote prosocial attitudes and positive behavior through core character values, with implementation flexibility ranging from one semester to a full year. One qualifying study was reviewed, finding potentially positive effects on middle and high school students' behavior. The program received a Tier 3 (Promising) evidence rating under the WWC Character Education protocol.

entries.title: Connecting Math Concepts (CMC)
entries.desc: Connecting Math Concepts (CMC) is an elementary school mathematics program reviewed by the WWC. As of July 2007, no studies of this program were found that met WWC design standards within the scope of the Elementary School Mathematics review protocol. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness for improving elementary mathematics outcomes.

entries.title: Consistency Management
entries.desc: Consistency Management is an elementary school intervention reviewed under the WWC Elementary School Mathematics protocol. As of July 2007, no qualifying studies were found that met WWC design standards. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness for improving elementary mathematics outcomes.

entries.title: CORD Applied Math
entries.desc: CORD Applied Math is a middle school mathematics program reviewed under the WWC Middle School Math protocol. As of July 2007, no studies of this program were found that met WWC design standards. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness for improving middle school mathematics outcomes.

entries.title: Corrective Reading
entries.desc: Corrective Reading is a scripted reading intervention using 45-minute lessons delivered 4–5 times weekly in small groups or whole-class settings for grades 3–5, targeting adolescent literacy across alphabetics, reading fluency, and comprehension domains. The WWC reviewed 1 qualifying study out of 13 eligible studies under the Adolescent Literacy protocol. The program was found to have no discernible effects on alphabetics, reading fluency, or comprehension for adolescent learners.

entries.title: DaisyQuest
entries.desc: DaisyQuest is a software bundle providing computer-assisted instruction in phonological awareness for children ages 3–7 (preschool through grade 2), using activities that teach rhyming, sound recognition, and phoneme blending within a fairy tale narrative framework. The WWC reviewed 4 qualifying studies out of 4 eligible studies, finding positive effects on alphabetics under the Beginning Reading protocol. The program received a Tier 3 (Promising) evidence rating.

entries.title: Heartwood Ethics Curriculum (An Ethics Curriculum for Children)
entries.desc: The Heartwood Ethics Curriculum (An Ethics Curriculum for Children) is a read-aloud literature-based character education program for grades 1–6 that uses multicultural stories with optional cross-curricular integration and parental involvement components. One qualifying study was reviewed, finding no discernible effects on either behavior or knowledge, attitudes, and values. The program received no evidence tier designation under the WWC Character Education protocol.

entries.title: Heath Mathematics Connections
entries.desc: Heath Mathematics Connections is a middle school mathematics textbook series featuring teacher-led instruction supplemented with practice problems, cooperative learning activities, and multiple assessment methods including warm-up problems, chapter reviews, and standardized format tests, with materials available in Spanish. As of July 2007, no studies met WWC design standards within the Middle School Math review protocol. The WWC was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness.

entries.title: Herman Method
entries.desc: The Herman Method is a reading intervention program for students with specific learning disabilities, delivered through direct instruction and drill-and-practice exercises using software, flashcards, and workbooks, targeting struggling readers, English language learners, and students with learning disabilities through fifth grade. The program covers phonemic awareness, phonics, fluency, vocabulary, reading comprehension, spelling, and writing.
