As of the review date, no studies met WWC design standards under the Students with a Specific Learning Disability protocol, and no conclusions about effectiveness could be drawn.

entries.title: Leveraging Interview-Informed LLMs to Model Survey Responses: Comparative Insights from AI-Generated and Human Data
entries.desc: This study examines whether large language models guided by personal interviews can reliably predict human survey responses. Using exercise behavior questionnaires and staff interviews as a case study, the researchers found that while LLMs capture general response patterns, they exhibit reduced variability compared to humans. Key findings indicate that interview content matters more than demographic data, negatively-worded questions prove challenging for models, and "LLMs faltered in reconstructing the test's psychometric structure." Published January 14, 2026, by Zhang et al. (University of Arkansas, University of Wyoming, University of South Carolina).

entries.title: Data Plus Theory Equals Codebook: Leveraging LLMs for Human-AI Codebook Development
entries.desc: The researchers examined how GPT-4o can support theory-grounded codebook creation in educational contexts. Across two studies, they tested a method combining established learning theories, think-aloud protocols, and human refinement. Results demonstrated that the AI successfully identified self-regulated learning concepts in student work. When comparing prompting strategies in a game-based learning study, naming theories without full references yielded the most practical codebooks, while supplying complete papers enhanced theoretical fidelity but reduced usability. Published January 24, 2026, by Zambrano, Wei, Zhang, Baker et al.

entries.title: Automating Self-Affirmation Essay Coding: Fine-Tuned BERT Performance Comparable to Human Coders and Comparison with GPT-4
entries.desc: The researchers developed a machine learning approach to automate the labor-intensive process of evaluating student self-affirmation writing exercises. Using a fine-tuned BERT model, they achieved performance matching human raters with a Cohen's Kappa of 0.85 (compared to 0.83 between human coders). While GPT-4 demonstrated reasonable zero-shot accuracy, it underperformed the specialized model, and few-shot prompting provided minimal improvement. The fine-tuned model maintained consistent accuracy across diverse student groups and demonstrated strong generalizability when tested on external datasets. Published January 30, 2026, by Ye, Borman, and Borman (AIR and Arizona State University).

entries.title: Human-AI Collaboration for Qualitative Analysis in Participatory Design: Refining the Writing Analytics Tool
entries.desc: This research presents a hybrid methodology combining human and artificial intelligence for examining qualitative data during the development of the Writing Analytics Toolkit (WAT). The study involved nine college writing instructors participating in focus groups to provide feedback on an early WAT prototype. Researchers used GPT-4o within a secure system to "generate inductive codes and preliminary themes from transcripts," which were then refined through human review. Four design priorities emerged: clearer feedback, greater instructor customization, reduced administrative load, and an improved interface. Published February 20, 2026, by Potter, Serhan, Patne et al. (Arizona State University team).

entries.title: Integrating Topic Modeling and LLM Prompt Engineering into a Human-driven Approach to Analyze Interview Transcripts
entries.desc: This research presents a hybrid methodology combining topic modeling, large language model prompting, and human coding to analyze qualitative data. The study applied this framework to focus group interviews with 13 U.S. teachers examining how communication and digital literacy skills are conceptualized and assessed in competency-based education settings. The authors demonstrate that this computational approach supports "construct alignment, thematic stability, and methodological transparency" while maintaining data richness. A key finding emphasizes the importance of human guidance in directing AI outputs and preserving theoretical coherence. Published February 22, 2026, by Ober, Courey, and Flor (ETS and Rice University).

entries.title: A Framework for Considering Exploration, Interpretation, and Confirmation During Data Analysis: Computationally Assisted Analysis of Teacher-Group Interactions
entries.desc: The researchers introduce a framework distinguishing three analytical stages — exploration, interpretation, and confirmation — and classify each as primarily human- or computer-led. They demonstrate this approach through analyzing teacher-student group interactions in high school mathematics classrooms using 15 videos from three instructors. Their computational pipeline combined pose detection, audio analysis, and large-language-model indicators to flag 317 interaction events across 21 student groups. The study revealed that computational tools showed preferences for detecting spatial proximity rather than discourse changes, illustrating how "leadership in exploration shaped what became detectable." Published March 3, 2026, by Hur, Palaguachi, Machaka et al.

entries.title: Comparing Zero-Shot Large Language Model Prompting with Human Coding of Theory Concepts in Student Essays
entries.desc: This research investigates how well large language models perform when coding content errors in college essays about criminological theories, comparing their accuracy to human coders. The study evaluated four different LLMs across multiple prompt variations and theory dimensions. Key findings reveal that Claude Sonnet 4 delivered superior performance while GPT-4.1 Mini underperformed. "Error rates were lowest when identifying whether students listed a concept, and highest when assessing whether definitions were correct," and the models struggled more with intricate theories than simpler ones. Published April 6, 2026, by Keith, Pavlik, Stives, and Kerr (University of Memphis, Seton Hall, Grand Valley State).

entries.title: Seeing Is Solving: MLLMs, Reasoning, and Refusal in Visual Math
entries.desc: The research examines how six contemporary multimodal language models handle math problems requiring visual information. The study tested 376 middle-school items from Illustrative Mathematics where diagrams contained essential task-critical information. With images, "the top-performing reasoning models achieve accuracy in the mid-50%, while non-reasoning models fall in the mid-30s to low-40s." When images were unavailable, models predominantly declined to answer rather than guess incorrectly. The authors recommend that tutoring systems gate automated scoring based on visual evidence availability and suggest incorporating structured visual descriptions to improve accessibility. Published April 5, 2026, by Croteau and Heffernan (Worcester Polytechnic Institute).

entries.title: Large Language Models Generalize SRL Prediction to New Languages Within But Not Between Domains
entries.desc: The researchers examined whether LLM-based classifiers trained in one language could effectively identify self-regulated learning (SRL) behaviors when applied to another language. Using OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small model, they analyzed think-aloud protocols from 26 students across German and American universities working with chemistry and formal logic tutoring systems. They predicted four SRL categories based on the Winne-Hadwin model. The key finding: models demonstrated strong cross-linguistic transfer "within but not between domains," with performance declining due to "linguistic differences in subject-specific terminology and unawareness of instructional context." Published September 8, 2025, by Borchers, Zhang, Fleischer, Schanze, Aleven, and Baker (CMU and University of Pennsylvania).

entries.title: Evaluating 21st-Century Competencies in Postsecondary Curricula with Large Language Models: Performance Benchmarking and Reasoning-Based Prompting Strategies
entries.desc: This research examines how effectively postsecondary programs embed 21st-century competencies using AI-powered curricular analytics. The team analyzed 7,600 manually annotated alignment scores across 38 competencies and 200 courses spanning five document types. Key findings indicate that "detailed instructional activity descriptions are the most informative type of curriculum document" for this analysis. While open-weight language models performed comparably to proprietary ones on broader assessments, none achieved human-level accuracy in nuanced pedagogical evaluation. The researchers introduced a "curricular chain-of-thought" strategy that yielded modest improvements in detecting complex instructional evidence. Published February 25, 2026, by Xu, Guan, Shi, Chen, and Yu (Columbia University).

entries.title: Profiling Pre-service Teachers' Computational Thinking: The Role of Metacognition and Coding Experience
entries.desc: This research examines how trainee educators develop computational thinking skills. The study surveyed 128 participants and found that "metacognitive strategies and prior coding experience significantly predict profile membership," revealing three distinct skill levels among the group. The researchers identified important connections between cognitive awareness, hands-on programming background, and problem-solving capability. The work suggests that understanding these patterns could help design better training programs for educators preparing to teach computational skills in elementary and secondary schools. Published March 14, 2026, by Chichekian, Cutumisu, Savard, and Zhang (Université de Sherbrooke and McGill University).

entries.title: Assessing Patterns of Students' Attainment of Professional Standards in Higher Education
entries.desc: The research addresses a gap in higher education assessment by proposing a novel curriculum analytics method. Rather than relying on traditional approaches, the authors present "an approach that uses psychometric modelling and learning analytics to identify distinct patterns in learners' acquisition of professional standards." This method enables automated inference of student competency without requiring alterations to current grading systems, offering institutions a scalable way to evaluate professional readiness alongside traditional GPA measures. Published March 15, 2026, by Barthakur, Jovanović, Baker, Kovanović, Deneen, and Dawson (Adelaide University and University of Belgrade).

entries.title: Assessing 21st Century Competencies: A Two-Case Study on Curriculum Analytics to Inform Continuous Improvement in Higher Education
entries.desc: This research examines how curriculum analytics tools support learning outcomes assessment aligned with modern competencies. The authors conducted a two-case study involving interviews, cognitive walkthroughs, and document reviews at two universities. Their analysis identified 18 themes with nine reaching full researcher consensus. The findings indicate that these analytics tools effectively produce "actionable insights that guide faculty toward more authentic and reflective teaching practices," bridging assessment and pedagogical improvement for higher education institutions seeking to implement competency-based education frameworks. Published March 15, 2026, by Hernández-Campos, Hilliger, and García-Peñalvo (Instituto Tecnológico de Costa Rica, UC Chile, Universidad de Salamanca).

entries.title: A GAI-Based Chatbot for Scaffolding Self-Regulated Learning: Insights from a Design-Based Research Approach
entries.desc: The research explores how generative AI chatbots can support student learning while addressing concerns about overreliance and diminished critical thinking. Through three design cycles with 276 students across 10 courses, the tool evolved into "a dynamic, course-integrated tool capable of supporting personalized, Socratic-style dialogue." Key findings emphasize that students need structured guidance to engage meaningfully with AI, and that effective implementation requires collaboration between educators, learners, and AI systems to ensure "GAI enhances deep learning and independent thought" rather than replacing human instruction. Published March 15, 2026, by Hilliger, Pérez-Sanagustín, González, Villalobos, and Celis.

entries.title: Modelability as a Strategy for Improving the Generalizability and Scalability of Predictive Models
entries.desc: The researchers introduce the concept of a "modelable world" — an educational environment designed to support development of predictive models that work across different contexts. They identify three core design principles: implementing valid and interpretable measurements, ensuring scalable and stable implementation, and creating a collaborative ecosystem connecting research, practice, and technology. Through a case study of CourseKata, an online textbook platform used across multiple institutions, the team developed early prediction models for student performance demonstrating "effective generalizability across diverse educational contexts." Published March 14, 2026, by Xu, Zhang, Blake, and Stigler (UCLA and CourseKata).

entries.title: Beyond Time on Task: A Novel Analytical Framework for Assessing Student Workload and Its Relationship with Learning
entries.desc: This research examines how workload dynamics correlate with student learning outcomes, moving beyond simple time-on-task averages. The researchers analyzed data from 14 engineering courses using three sources: self-reported weekly timesheets, learning management system interactions, and course evaluations. Key findings indicate that "workload dynamics metrics, such as the presence of workload peaks, were highly correlated with perceived learning and perceived difficulty." The framework proposes practical tools for educators to optimize course design and improve student learning outcomes. Published March 18, 2026, by Sargent, Hilliger, and Baier (Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile and Instituto Milenio Fundamentos de los Datos).

entries.title: 12 Heuristics for Learning Analytics in Simulation-Based Professional Learning
entries.desc: The research develops evaluation frameworks for learning analytics in simulation-based professional training. The study addresses how to create appropriate assessment heuristics by combining theoretical frameworks with empirical findings, then refining them through expert collaboration. The resulting framework supports assessment across "technological, pedagogical, and social dimensions of professional learning" in simulation environments, offering researchers and practitioners a structured approach to evaluating analytics interventions in high-fidelity training contexts. Published March 20, 2026, by Harrington and Sellberg (Linköping University and University of Gothenburg).

entries.title: Learning Analytics to Uncover Ethnic Bias in Educational Texts: An Ensemble Learning Approach
entries.desc: This research explores using machine learning to automatically detect ethnic bias in text-based educational materials. The study examined 345 labeled text samples assessed by 193 students from diverse ethnic backgrounds. Researchers tested multiple classification models, finding that support vector machines and random forests performed best for bias identification. The authors note that "biased content, particularly in text-based learning materials such as textbooks, lesson plans, and course excerpts" can harm student outcomes, and the work aims to support human reviewers through automated detection while contributing to more equitable educational technologies. Published February 25, 2026, by Albuquerque, Rienties, Hlosta, and Holmes.

entries.title: Human-Centred Development of Indicators for Self-Service Learning Analytics: A Transparency through Exploration Approach
entries.desc: The researchers investigated how to achieve transparent learning analytics by empowering end-users. They developed and evaluated the Indicator Editor through human-centered design methods with 15 participants. Their findings demonstrate that "supporting user interaction and providing user control in the indicator implementation process can have positive effects on different crucial aspects of LA, namely transparency, trust, satisfaction, and acceptance." The study focuses on self-service learning analytics as a means to address trust and acceptance challenges when educational insights and recommendations lack transparent reasoning. Published March 22, 2026, by Joarder and Chatti (University of Duisburg-Essen).

entries.title: Temporal Network Analysis of Collaborative Prototyping from a Knowledge Creation Perspective
entries.desc: The research examines prototyping sessions across three design disciplines to understand collaborative problem-solving through a knowledge creation lens. The study analyzed "teams' shared epistemic agency, segmenting activities into three phases based on idea improvement patterns." The findings revealed that engineers prioritized generative actions, product designers focused on mutual understanding, and service designers addressed knowledge gaps. The authors propose design principles including attention to disciplinary differences, timely educator support, and shorter task durations to facilitate idea sharing. Published March 22, 2026, by Ohsaki, Tan, Eagan, Shaffer, and Swiecki.

entries.title: Function Art: Understanding the Artistic Transformation of Mathematical Functions through Learning Analytics
entries.desc: This research examines how students in grades 8–12 engage with mathematical functions creatively using function art, a STEAM-based method. The study involved 400 Philippine students creating digital artworks in GeoGebra. Through hierarchical clustering analysis, researchers identified three learner profiles: "Repetitivists" (quantity-focused), "Simplists" (limited use), and "Multifunctionists" (diversity-focused). Using TIMSS cognitive frameworks, findings revealed that students' strategic approaches and precision in transformations varied independently from how many or which function types they employed, suggesting function art offers valuable insights for interdisciplinary STEAM instruction. Published March 30, 2026, by Bautista Jr., Cacuyong, Lavicza, Sabitzer, and Emara.

entries.title: Exploring Fairness and Explainability in LLM-Generated Support for Online Learning Discussion Forums
entries.desc: The research examines potential bias concerns when using large language models to generate responses in educational discussion forums. The team fine-tuned three models (GPT-2, Gemma, and LLaMA) and analyzed sentiment patterns in their outputs compared to human-written replies. Key findings indicate that "LLM-generated responses are generally more neutral than human replies but exhibit varying degrees of sentiment bias across gender." The researchers introduced a measurement tool called absolute distributional sentiment divergence (ADSD) to quantify bias disparities and tested counterfactual fine-tuning as a mitigation strategy. Published September 3, 2025, by Liu, Xing, Jiao, and Li (University of Florida, NYU, University of Utah).

entries.title: Towards More Transparency in Learning Analytics: Sharing Information with University Students Increases their Awareness of Data Collection Practices
entries.desc: The research examines how disclosure statements about data collection affect student understanding. Sepp tested three formats — generic text, detailed text, and icon-based "nutrition labels" — with university participants. Key findings indicate that "non-generic disclosures significantly improved comprehension of data practices," though student acceptance of data collection remained unchanged regardless of disclosure format. The study concludes that institutions should reconsider their data disclosure methods to enhance transparency and student understanding of campus data collection practices. Published November 24, 2025, by Stoo Sepp (University of New England).

entries.title: Balancing Act: Early, Fair, and Accurate Identification of At-Risk Students
entries.desc: The researchers developed fairness-aware machine learning models to identify struggling high school Advanced Placement statistics students while addressing demographic bias. They analyzed predictive performance across three course stages, examining outcomes across racial, gender, and socioeconomic groups. Key finding: "at Stage 1 (i.e., up to the first unit review assignment), the models effectively identified at-risk students while maintaining fairness across demographic groups." The study demonstrates that incorporating learning activity data reduces reliance on demographic information, thereby mitigating potential algorithmic bias in educational prediction systems. Published November 26, 2025, by Cheng, Pei, and Liu (University of Notre Dame and University of South Florida).

entries.title: Interpreting Predictive Learning Sequences in a College Math Course through a Self-Regulated Learning Framework
entries.desc: The researchers collected multimodal data from 49 undergraduates completing mathematics tasks to validate inferences about self-regulated learning sequences. Using sequence pattern mining on digital traces and corroborating findings with "think aloud verbalizations," they identified behavioral patterns predicting performance. Key findings included that "sequences representing learner conscientiousness predicted better performance" while those involving video pausing and rewinding predicted worse outcomes. The study revealed alignment between verbal reports and digital evidence for some SRL processes, though "heterogeneity in verbal-to-digital trace alignment" highlighted need for methodological refinement. Published November 26, 2025, by Yu, Halpin, Bernacki, Ren, Plumley, and Greene (UNC Chapel Hill).

entries.title: Learning Analytics in Schools: Is Digital Data Use Influenced by Teacher-Level or School-Level Factors?
entries.desc: This survey examined 2,247 teachers across 112 Swiss upper secondary schools to understand what drives educators' use of digital data in their teaching. The research found that "teacher characteristics including their positive beliefs about technologies, competency with digital data, and availability of data technologies significantly predict their digital data use." Additionally, school environment factors like colleague collaboration and principal support indirectly influence usage through their effect on teacher attributes. The study suggests customized professional development can better support teachers' adoption of learning analytics based on individual and institutional contexts. Published October 4, 2025, by Michos, Schmitz, and Petko (EPFL and University of Zurich).

entries.title: Learning Analytics for Early Identification of At-Risk Students and Feedback Intervention
entries.desc: The study addresses supporting academically vulnerable students by combining predictive machine learning models with theoretically-grounded intervention feedback. Researchers developed models using prior course data to identify at-risk students, then delivered email interventions designed around relational feedback principles. Results demonstrated strong model accuracy (AUC scores exceeding 0.8), with over 30% of flagged students engaging previously unvisited learning materials within two weeks. Survey respondents indicated satisfaction with the intervention, though they preferred "receiving the intervention more frequently than the twice-per-semester frequency." Published November 30, 2025, by Dai, Lin, Jin, Tsai, Srivastava, Le Bodic, Gašević, and Chen.

entries.title: Augmented Reality Enhanced Analytics for Education: A Systematic Review
entries.desc: This systematic literature review examines augmented reality (AR) applications that utilize enhanced analytics in educational settings, covering publications from 2000 to 2025 across primary, secondary, and higher education sectors. The authors note that "AR enhanced analytics expand measures of engagement and effectiveness" while identifying both advantages and challenges in implementation. The research highlights how combining AR with learning analytics enables "richer, unobtrusive, real-time insights into student learning processes," potentially advancing understanding of student engagement, motivation, behavior, and skill acquisition beyond traditional assessment methods. Published December 15, 2025, by Singh, Bangay, and Sajjanhar (Deakin University).

entries.title: Universal Preschool in California: Promising Practices for Mixed Delivery Systems
entries.desc: Through a $4 billion California investment, this report examines how local leaders are expanding early childhood education access for 4-year-olds and eligible 3-year-olds. Drawing on case studies from two school districts and two cities, the research identifies strategies for improving quality, equity, and coordination across mixed delivery systems. Key findings emphasize the importance of dedicated leadership, simplified enrollment processes, shared quality standards, competitive educator compensation, and sustained support for community-based providers serving younger children as transitional kindergarten expands. Published February 27, 2026.

entries.title: Community Schools in Rural California: Leveraging Shared Resources in West Kern County
entries.desc: Rural schools frequently struggle with restricted availability of academic and mental health resources. This report examines how six districts in California's West Kern County addressed these constraints through a collaborative approach. By establishing a cross-district community schools consortium, participating districts were able to "pool and coordinate otherwise out-of-reach resources, strengthening their capacity to expand student access to academic and social supports." The initiative achieved measurable improvements in student math and literacy achievement along with significant decreases in chronic absenteeism. Published February 6, 2026.

entries.title: Investing in Our Nation's Future: Advancing Educational Opportunity for Underserved Students
entries.desc: This research examines evidence-based educational interventions and their economic returns, synthesizing findings on programs spanning early childhood through higher education, school integration, and community-based initiatives. Key findings show these interventions generate substantial taxpayer returns "ranging from approximately $2 to over $10 for every dollar invested." The report highlights persistent educational disparities and explores two potential futures, arguing that pursuing equity-centered improvements could yield "$20.2 to $72.6 billion per year" in net public benefits, framing educational investments as both a moral imperative and strategic economic necessity. Published December 8, 2025.

entries.title: Preparing Early Childhood Teachers: Credentialing and Preparation Programs in Four States
entries.desc: This Learning Policy Institute report examines early childhood teaching credentials across the United States, with focused case studies in Louisiana, Massachusetts, New Jersey, and New York. The research reveals that while 46 states offer stand-alone early childhood credentials, requirements vary significantly. Key findings include barriers candidates face — such as tuition costs and navigation challenges — and effective strategies that preparation programs use to increase access and diversity. The report provides policymakers and practitioners with recommendations for strengthening credential programs through improved coursework design, expanded pathways, financial support, and comprehensive clinical experiences. Published December 4, 2025.

entries.title: Investing in a High-Quality Teacher Workforce: Lessons From Texas Teacher Preparation Programs
entries.desc: This research examines three high-quality educator preparation programs at Texas universities that employ full-year clinical teaching pathways. The study documents how these institutions address Texas's persistent teacher shortage and high attrition rates by redesigning coursework around practice-based learning and establishing intensive mentorship models. The researchers identify common success factors, including "scaffolded and cohesive preparation coursework aligned with intensive full-year clinical experiences," strong partnerships with local school districts, and robust financial support for candidates. Published November 17, 2025.

entries.title: The Importance of Family Engagement
entries.desc: Authentic family and community engagement serves as a key strategy for enhancing students' social-emotional and academic outcomes. This EdTrust resource presents three reports addressing parental perceptions of school engagement post-pandemic, policy changes for better family-community involvement, and improvements in how assessment results are communicated to families. However, schools still need to improve their equitable engagement with all families, particularly those from historically underserved communities. Published April 11, 2024.

entries.title: CCSR: A New Model for the Role of Research in Supporting Urban School Reform
entries.desc: The report examines how the UChicago Consortium established itself as a distinctive organization by conducting research that is both technically rigorous and accessible to practitioners and policymakers. The authors argue that "the UChicago Consortium's focus on building capacity for school reform both sets the UChicago Consortium's role apart from traditional approaches" and represents an innovative model for policy-relevant research in educational reform. The document provides historical context for the Consortium's distinctive approach to applied urban education research. Published February 2009.

entries.title: Legislating Literacy at the State Level: Six Considerations for Supporting Students With Disabilities
entries.desc: This WestEd Perspectives brief examines how states can better support students with disabilities through literacy policymaking. It outlines recent legislation affecting both general and special education systems and offers six key considerations for implementation. The brief addresses the intersection of structured literacy mandates with special education law, providing a resource list for policy development that helps states ensure literacy reforms reach all learners, including those with learning disabilities. Published 2025.

entries.title: From AI Risk to AI Readiness
entries.desc: This recorded webinar explores how educational leaders can assess their organization's readiness for AI implementation. The session covers "AI Readiness Assessment," fine-tuning approaches for large language models, privacy and governance considerations, and cost analysis for various implementation strategies. Experts Sarah Quesen and Baron Rodriguez provide frameworks for responsible AI integration while protecting student data and ensuring equitable access for students and staff. Published January 15, 2026.

entries.title: Selecting GenAI Tools That Build Healthy Classroom Culture
entries.desc: This webinar explores how to evaluate generative AI tools for classroom use through a cultural fit lens. The session covers edtech history, AI risks and benefits, procurement strategies, and human-centered design principles to ensure tools support healthy learning environments and student outcomes. The resource helps educators and administrators move beyond feature checklists toward evaluating how AI tools align with school culture and equity goals. Published January 13, 2026.

entries.title: The Impact of NBA Math Hoops on Students' Math Achievement: A Randomized Controlled Trial
entries.desc: This study evaluated how NBA Math Hoops affects student math performance through a rigorous experimental design. The research found that "students in NBA Math Hoops classrooms scored an average of 0.19 standard deviations higher than peers in STEM enrichment classrooms" — roughly equivalent to advancing from the 50th to 58th percentile. The program demonstrated practical benefits for educators, requiring minimal training and costing under $20 per student while remaining straightforward to implement in out-of-school settings. Published 2026.

entries.title: Advancing Achievement Through Biliteracy: Statewide Trends in Bilingual Teacher Supply, Demand, and Assignment in California
entries.desc: This brief examines bilingual authorized (BLA) teacher workforce supply and demand trends in California using statewide data. The authors find that while the state has progressed toward its goal of 2,000 new bilingual authorizations annually, significant data gaps exist. The brief emphasizes the need for "improved data systems, strategic planning, and pathway investment" to support California's vision of universal bilingualism and biliteracy and ensure adequate bilingual teacher supply to serve the state's large English learner population. Published 2026.

entries.title: Friction by Design: A Framework for Centering Learning in the Age of AI
entries.desc: This framework helps educators integrate AI intentionally while preserving meaningful learning. It distinguishes between productive friction — the effort and struggle that enables growth — and unproductive friction that obstructs progress. The guide employs five design lenses (Cognitive Ownership, Productive Struggle, Social Sense-Making, Activation Energy, and Access Barriers) to show how AI can reduce barriers while maintaining the challenge essential to learning. Published 2025.

entries.title: Bridging Science and Engineering for Every Learner
entries.desc: A recorded webinar introducing practical, research-backed strategies for creating inclusive STEM experiences in informal settings. The session focuses on serving multilingual learners, those with physical and sensory disabilities, and Indigenous learners using Universal Design for Learning principles, with examples drawn from the NASA-funded PLANETS curriculum. The resource supports educators and informal science educators seeking to expand access to rigorous STEM learning for historically underserved student populations. Published February 25, 2026.

entries.title: DISC AI Resource Collection
entries.desc: This collection comprises practical guidance developed by WestEd's Data Integration Support Center for secure AI implementation in public agencies. The materials address the complete lifecycle of AI deployment, including "infrastructure requirements, technical methodologies, policy frameworks, and practical decision-making tools" to help organizations build trustworthy AI capabilities while protecting sensitive data. Designed for education agencies navigating AI adoption decisions, the collection covers responsible data governance, vendor evaluation, and privacy safeguards. Published 2024–2025.

entries.title: Improving Learning Opportunities for Multilingual Learners: CABE 2026 Related Resources
entries.desc: This curated collection of research, tools, and strategies is designed to support educators working with multilingual learners. The collection covers strengthening professional learning communities, expanding dual language programs, engaging multilingual students in ambitious mathematics, and supporting long-term English Learners. Assembled for WestEd's participation in the California Association for Bilingual Education 2026 Annual Conference, the resources are drawn from ongoing WestEd research and practitioner-facing tools. Published early 2026.

entries.title: Youth Mental Health Corps Implementation Reporting: Resource Collection
entries.desc: This collection documents WestEd's evaluation of the Youth Mental Health Corps, a multisector partnership launched in 2024. The materials include a Year 1 report showing how the initiative "engaged 317 members across four states, reached thousands of students and families," plus four state case studies from Colorado, Michigan, Minnesota, and Texas. The resources examine how near-peer navigators expand youth mental health support while building the behavioral health workforce, offering a model for scaling mental health services in school-linked contexts. Published September 2025.

entries.title: What Is The Statewide BHSSA Evaluation?
entries.desc: This fact sheet explains California's Behavioral Health Student Services Act (BHSSA) evaluation, which funds partnerships between county behavioral health departments and schools. WestEd and the Commission for Behavioral Health are leading the assessment "from July 2025 through June 2027" using five evaluation components to measure how BHSSA funds have been deployed and their impact on school behavioral health systems. The evaluation will track implementation quality, reach, and outcomes across California school districts. Published 2026.

entries.title: Youth Reimagining School Behavioral Health Systems
entries.desc: This resource outlines the Youth Engagement Cohort (YEC) model, "a five-session, student-centered process" where students analyze school data, reflect on available supports, and present recommendations to school leaders. The initiative ensures young people shape California's behavioral health transformation efforts while developing leadership and evaluation competencies. The model positions students as active researchers and advocates within their school communities, contributing to California's broader BHSSA implementation goals. Published 2026.

entries.title: The California State University's AANHPI Student Achievement Program: An Evaluation of Progress From Spring 2024 Through Fall 2025
entries.desc: This WestEd evaluation examined how legislative funding was distributed across all 23 CSU campuses and its initial effects on AANHPI students. The report found that "ASAP-supported initiatives strengthened students' academic engagement, personal well-being, and connection to AANHPI cultures and histories." Survey results indicated participating students reported notably more positive views regarding campus climate, belonging, and self-efficacy compared to non-participants. However, implementation showed considerable variation among campuses due to differences in institutional support history, staffing, student demographics, and data systems. Published March 2026.

entries.title: Advancing Education Research: AERA and NCME 2026 Related Resources
entries.desc: A curated collection of research papers, blog articles, and websites assembled for AERA Annual Meeting and NCME concurrent sessions (April 2026), covering topics such as responsible AI integration in education, mathematics curricular resources, K–12 disciplinary literacy, technology in education, and support for multilingual learners. The collection surfaces WestEd research relevant to current debates in educational measurement, assessment, and equity-focused instruction. Published early 2026.

entries.title: Academically Diverse Classrooms, Deeper Needs: What Teachers Face After the Pandemic
entries.desc: The research examines post-COVID classroom composition, analyzing the spread of academic achievement levels. Key findings reveal that "more students are off-track and further behind grade-level proficiency," presenting significant challenges for educators managing increasingly diverse student populations. The study documents how the widened distribution of achievement within classrooms makes differentiated instruction more complex and resource-intensive, with implications for teacher support, professional development, and assessment design. Published April 2026, by Kuhfeld, Morton, Lewis, and Peters (NWEA).

entries.title: Should Kindergartners Be Redshirted? Costs Likely Outweigh Academic Benefits
entries.desc: The research examines data on delaying kindergarten entry — known as redshirting. Using assessment results from over three million kindergartners, the study analyzed trends between 2017–2025 and followed one cohort through third grade to determine whether delayed entry provided academic advantages. The findings indicate that the academic costs of delayed entry likely outweigh any benefits, providing quantitative evidence to inform family and district decisions about kindergarten enrollment timing. Published March 2026, by Kuhfeld et al. (NWEA).

entries.title: Kindergarten Redshirting: What Families Should Know
entries.desc: This family-facing fact sheet summarizes the data on the practice of delaying kindergarten enrollment, known as redshirting. Designed for schools to use with families considering or seeking information about delayed entry, the resource translates research findings from the companion NWEA study into accessible guidance. The publication highlights key considerations about the academic and social impacts of kindergarten timing decisions. Published March 2026, by Kuhfeld and Postell (NWEA).

entries.title: A Practical Guide for Considering and Interpreting Test Score Differences Among Student Subgroups
entries.desc: The guide provides recommendations for understanding differences in student group data, offering leaders "structure for how they might consider and interpret those differences" across various demographic subgroups. The resource addresses how to contextualize score gaps without misinterpreting them as fixed or deterministic, supporting more equitable use of assessment data in school and district decision-making. Published April 2026, by NWEA researchers.

entries.title: The Middle School Science Course Landscape: Course Offerings, Enrollment Patterns, and Implications for Assessment
entries.desc: This research examines middle school science curriculum structures across 2,200 schools. The study finds that "multi-disciplinary course models now dominate versus single-course offerings," while noting disparities in access tied to student demographics. Notably, the research indicates that "students in different course models performed similarly on assessments," suggesting structural course design may not significantly impact achievement outcomes, with implications for science assessment design and equitable course access. Published April 2026, by NWEA researchers.

entries.title: Evaluation of the Networks for School Improvement Initiative: Impacts on Students and Implementation Factors
entries.desc: This evaluation examined how network-based continuous improvement initiatives affected student outcomes through schools' fourth year of participation. The Gates Foundation funded 34 Networks for School Improvement across the country from 2018 to 2025, investing over $300 million in intermediary organizations. The study compared results for students in participating schools against similar students in non-participating schools, finding mixed results — some grade-level interventions improved math scores and course outcomes, while others showed limited impact on graduation rates and college enrollment. Published April 22, 2026, by Mathematica.

entries.title: Trusting Your LLM: Evaluating Statistical Analysis Performance
entries.desc: A framework evaluating how large language models perform statistical analysis on complex survey data. The research tested 35 questions based on American Community Survey data, comparing reasoning-only versus code-execution workflows. Findings show that "data access and tool invocation substantially improve accuracy, particularly for descriptive tasks, while causal inference performance varies meaningfully across models." The results have direct implications for researchers and policy analysts considering LLM use in education data analysis. Published March 6, 2026, by Mathematica.

entries.title: Early Childhood Educator Pay Equity Fund: Do Economic Returns Change Over Time?
entries.desc: This research brief analyzes the economic returns of Washington, DC's Pay Equity Fund in fiscal year 2024. The study found that the program's benefits exceeded costs by $16 million, yielding a 21 percent social return on investment. The analysis examines how the program's shift to a facility payroll model affected its economic efficiency while delivering wage payments to over 4,000 educators and subsidized health insurance benefits, offering a model for other jurisdictions seeking to raise early childhood educator compensation. Published April 21, 2026, by Mathematica.

entries.title: Jobs in the Balance: The Three-Year Labor Market Impacts of Washington, DC's Early Childhood Educator Pay Equity Fund
entries.desc: This working paper examines how DC's Pay Equity Fund — the nation's first dedicated public funding initiative to narrow compensation gaps between early childhood educators and public school teachers — affected the labor market over three years. Using federal labor market data and synthetic control methods, researchers found the program increased the educator workforce by approximately 341 workers (11% growth) and raised wages following the transition to a facility payment model, though it did not significantly increase the number of child care establishments. Published April 17, 2026, by Mathematica.

entries.title: The Impact of Registered and Unregistered Apprenticeship
entries.desc: This research examines employment and earnings outcomes from two federally funded apprenticeship grant programs. The study found that "registered and unregistered apprenticeships had large and significant impacts on employment and earnings in the ninth quarter post enrollment." The research covers IT, healthcare, and advanced manufacturing sectors, providing rigorous causal evidence on the effectiveness of employer-based training models that combine classroom instruction with on-the-job mentorship for workforce development. Published April 1, 2026, by Mathematica.

entries.title: I CAN Learn Geometry
entries.desc: I CAN Learn Geometry is a computer-based math curriculum for students in middle school, high school, and college that delivers math instruction through interactive lessons students complete individually at their own pace. Students must demonstrate mastery of each concept before advancing, and teachers can provide supplemental instruction based on student performance data from the software. As of August 2017, the WWC found no studies meeting its design standards for this intervention, so it could not determine effectiveness or ineffectiveness for improving secondary mathematics outcomes.

entries.title: Really Great Reading's Foundational Literacy Programs
entries.desc: RGR provides teachers with professional development and structured curriculum resources for explicit instruction in phonological awareness, phonics, word recognition, decoding, and fluency. The program includes digital lesson delivery tools designed to standardize instruction and support diverse learners in grades 1–3. A study of over 1,000 students across 13 urban charter schools found positive results: "Grade 1 and grade 2 students receiving RGR instruction scored higher on the NWEA MAP Reading Growth assessment in winter 2022 (effect size = +0.30, +0.24)," earning a Promising ESSA evidence tier.

entries.title: mCLASS Intervention (formerly BURST Reading)
entries.desc: This reading intervention delivers 10-lesson units targeting specific skills identified through formative assessments conducted three times yearly. Small groups of 3–5 students in grades K–3 receive instruction from teachers or paraprofessionals using "a direct instruction approach, followed by progress monitoring and assessment." One study across 57 schools evaluated the program with 4,022 students, showing positive effects on DIBELS Next and STAR Early Literacy measures (effect size = +0.10), earning a Promising ESSA evidence tier.

entries.title: Reading Apprenticeship Academic Literacy (RAAL)
entries.desc: Reading Apprenticeship Academic Literacy (RAAL) is a high school literacy program (grades 9–12) representing an adaptation of Reading Apprenticeship designed for diverse student populations. Implementation requires a dedicated year-long course taught by at least one teacher, with WestEd providing comprehensive professional development including a 5-day pre-course institute and ongoing coaching. One study examining RAAL against Xtreme Reading and control conditions found participants demonstrated significantly improved state assessment performance, with an average effect size of +0.10 across 2,255 students, earning a Strong ESSA evidence tier.

entries.title: The COVID-19 Slide: What Summer Learning Loss Can Tell Us About the Potential Impact of School Closures on Student Academic Achievement
entries.desc: This research brief examines how summer learning loss patterns can illuminate potential academic impacts from COVID-19 school closures. The brief helps educators and policymakers understand and prepare for achievement gaps when students return to in-person instruction. The analysis draws on established seasonal learning research to project consequences of the extended instructional pause.

entries.title: Rethinking Summer Slide: The More You Gain, the More You Lose
entries.desc: This research draws on data from 3.4 million students assessed with NWEA MAP Growth to examine summer learning loss patterns. The study reveals that students experiencing the greatest academic decline during summer were those who had made the most substantial gains during the preceding school year. The findings challenge conventional explanations for summer learning loss such as program access and school year length.

entries.title: The Development of Racial/Ethnic and Socioeconomic Achievement Gaps During the School Years
entries.desc: This study examines how achievement gaps between racial/ethnic and socioeconomic groups develop from school entry through middle school. The researchers found that poor White students consistently outperformed poor Black and poor Hispanic students, with gaps widening around ages 7–8. The analysis highlights the importance of examining race and poverty together rather than separately when studying educational disparities.

entries.title: Seven Successful Strategies for Literate Assessment
entries.desc: This article presents evidence suggesting that principals' knowledge and skills related to assessment leadership — including professional development, data use in classroom planning, and professional collaboration — are fundamental to building assessment literacy among teachers. The research emphasizes that balanced assessment systems encompass diverse evaluation methods beyond traditional testing. Seven evidence-based practices are identified that enhance classroom learning outcomes.

entries.title: How to Promote Equity in Advanced Coursework Participation: Three Strategies for Closing the Gaps
entries.desc: This article examines how district leaders can address opportunity gaps in advanced course enrollment through data-driven approaches, documenting the experience of Gresham-Barlow School District in Oregon. The research focuses on identifying and closing disparities in course-taking patterns while supporting student retention in advanced programs.

entries.title: Mind the Kinder-Gap: New Data on Children's Math and Reading Skills as They Enter Kindergarten
entries.desc: This research examines academic preparation at kindergarten entry, presenting data revealing both declining achievement and shrinking achievement gaps between student subgroups. The study analyzes trends in children's math and reading proficiency as they begin formal schooling, providing insights into early learning disparities and achievement patterns across student populations.

entries.title: When Does Inequality Grow? A Seasonal Analysis of Racial/Ethnic Disparities in Learning in Kindergarten Through Eighth Grade
entries.desc: This research examines seasonal patterns of racial/ethnic achievement gaps across K-8 using data from over two million students. The study finds that Black-White achievement gaps widen during school years and shrink during summers, while Asian students advance faster relative to White students during summer break. The findings suggest schools play a significant role in either reproducing or exacerbating educational inequalities across grade levels.

entries.title: It Matters How You Start: Early Numeracy Mastery Predicts High School Math Course-Taking and College Attendance
entries.desc: This study examines how early numeracy skills at age 54 months predict later educational outcomes using data from 1,364 children. The researchers measured mastery of counting, concrete representational arithmetic, and abstract arithmetic operations, finding that foundational math competencies in early childhood have significant long-term implications for high school course enrollment and college attendance rates.

entries.title: What Do Teachers Know About Dyslexia? It's Complicated!
entries.desc: This 2022 study examines teachers' understanding of dyslexia concepts, finding that teachers hold both scientifically-based conceptions and misconceptions about dyslexia. The research surveyed 524 Midwestern teachers using the Dyslexia Knowledge Questionnaire-2, discovering that prior training and confidence predicted dyslexia knowledge, though years of teaching experience did not.

entries.title: COVID-19 in the Early Elementary Years: A Comparison of Achievement in Spring 2019 and Spring 2022
entries.desc: This research brief examines how pandemic-era schooling affected students in early elementary grades by analyzing MAP Growth data from 1.6 million first and second graders tested in spring 2022 and comparing results to a spring 2019 cohort. Reading and math achievement in first and second grade remained substantially lower in 2022 relative to historical data, with particularly pronounced impacts for economically disadvantaged and historically marginalized populations.

entries.title: Longitudinal Models of Reading and Mathematics Achievement in Deaf and Hard of Hearing Students
entries.desc: This study analyzes longitudinal NWEA MAP Growth data from grades 2–8 across reading and mathematics domains over five years to understand how academic achievement progresses for deaf and hard of hearing (DHH) students. The research reveals that both DHH and hearing students continue building skills throughout this period, challenging prior assumptions that DHH students plateau in the elementary grades. The findings demonstrate the importance of longitudinal data analysis for identifying where DHH students may need additional academic support.

entries.title: Disparities and Discrimination in Student Discipline by Race and Family Income
entries.desc: This study examines racial and socioeconomic disparities in school discipline by comparing punishments given to Black and White students and poor versus nonpoor students involved in the same incidents. The researchers find that Black and poor students are punished more harshly than the students with whom they fight, revealing evidence of discrimination in discipline practices. The research addresses a critical methodological challenge that had previously prevented scholars from identifying discriminatory patterns in student discipline outcomes.

entries.title: Learning During COVID-19: Initial Findings on Students' Reading and Math Achievement and Growth
entries.desc: This study analyzed data from approximately 4.4 million students in grades 3–8 who took MAP Growth assessments in fall 2020, finding that math achievement was 5 to 10 percentile points lower than pre-COVID performance and that students demonstrated reduced growth rates compared to the prior year. Demographic shifts in the tested student population suggest the actual impact on vulnerable populations may be underestimated.

entries.title: Understanding the Initial Educational Impacts of COVID-19 on Communities of Color
entries.desc: This investigation examines how the pandemic affected academic outcomes for Black, Indigenous, and students of color by analyzing math and reading assessments from 2.1 million BIPOC students in grades 3–8. Math achievement was significantly impacted in fall 2020 while reading remained relatively stable, and learning rates during the pandemic fell below pre-pandemic trajectories.

entries.title: Trends in Children's Academic Skills at School Entry: 2010 to 2017
entries.desc: This research examines kindergarten entry skills across approximately 2 million students comparing data from 2010 and 2017. The study found that kindergarteners in 2017 had moderately lower math and reading skills than in 2010, while simultaneously revealing that achievement gaps between racial/ethnic groups and schools with different poverty levels narrowed during this period.

entries.title: Examining the State-Trait Composition of Social-Emotional Learning Constructs: Implications for Practice, Policy, and Evaluation
entries.desc: This 2019 study investigates how stable social-emotional learning (SEL) competencies are compared to academic achievement. The researchers found that the four SEL domains seem to be more strongly influenced by contextual factors, while math and reading skills remain highly stable over time. The findings have implications for school accountability systems and intervention design.

entries.title: The Widening Achievement Divide During COVID-19
entries.desc: This study analyzed MAP Growth assessment data from 8 million students in grades 3–8 across 24,000 public schools to examine changes in test score variability during the pandemic. Test scores for lower-achieving students dropped more than declines observed for higher-achieving students, with increased score dispersion concentrated at the bottom of the achievement distribution. The findings emphasize the urgent need for targeted support to help struggling learners close the significantly widened achievement gap.

entries.title: Student Achievement in 2021-22: Cause for Hope and Continued Urgency
entries.desc: This analysis examined 8.3 million students' MAP Growth data from grades 3–8 across the 2021–22 school year, revealing encouraging early signs of academic rebounding with stronger gains in math and among younger students, though overall achievement remained below pre-pandemic levels. Achievement disparities remained wider in spring 2022 than prior to the pandemic, with recovery timelines extending beyond federal funding deadlines.

entries.title: Learning During COVID-19: An Update on Student Achievement and Growth at the Start of the 2021-22 School Year
entries.desc: This research examined how the COVID-19 pandemic affected student achievement and growth in reading and math using data from 6 million students in grades 3–8. Achievement was lower for all student groups in fall 2021, but with disproportionately high impacts for historically marginalized students and students in high-poverty schools. Both achievement levels and learning gains lagged behind pre-pandemic trends, particularly in mathematics.

entries.title: The Forgotten 20 Percent: Achievement and Growth in Rural Schools Across the Nation
entries.desc: This research examines how achievement and growth patterns differ between rural and nonrural schools using data from 840,000 students across 8,800 public schools. The study reveals that rural students start kindergarten slightly ahead of nonrural students but fall behind by middle school, with larger summer learning losses driving this divergence. The findings underscore the significance of seasonal learning patterns when evaluating rural school performance.

entries.title: Does Four Equal Five? Implementation and Outcomes of the Four-Day School Week
entries.desc: This large-scale study examined the implementation and outcomes of four-day school weeks compared to traditional five-day schedules across Idaho, New Mexico, and Oklahoma. Cost savings related to the four-day model were relatively small, and students on the four-day schedule had lower scores over time when compared with peers on a five-day schedule. Results overall were mixed, with stakeholders reporting high satisfaction despite modest academic performance differences.

entries.title: Learning During COVID-19: Reading and Math Achievement in the 2020-2021 School Year
entries.desc: This study analyzed data from 5.5 million students in grades 3–8 to examine how pandemic disruptions affected reading and math achievement during the 2020–2021 school year. Students across most grades made reading and math gains in 2020–21 but their outcomes remained lower across multiple dimensions compared to pre-pandemic trends. American Indian and Alaska Native, Black, and Latinx students, along with those in high-poverty schools, experienced disproportionately larger achievement declines.

entries.title: Academic Growth for Students with Disabilities: Lessons from School-Year Learning Gains and Summer Learning Loss
entries.desc: This brief examines how schools can support academic progress for students with disabilities who faced disproportionate pandemic-related learning challenges. The resource offers guidance on leveraging federal ESSER and IDEA funding to provide early intervention, extended school year support in the summer, and other evidence-based supports for sustainable academic advancement.

entries.title: Education's Long COVID: 2022-23 Achievement Data Reveal Stalled Progress Toward Pandemic Recovery
entries.desc: This study analyzed data from 6.7 million U.S. public school students in grades 3–8 to examine academic progress in the 2022–23 school year, finding that progress toward academic recovery stalled. Students require approximately 4.1 additional months of instruction in reading and 4.5 months in mathematics to catch up to pre-pandemic performance levels, with persistent achievement gaps remaining.

entries.title: Progress Towards Pandemic Recovery: Continued Signs of Rebounding Achievement at the Start of the 2022-23 School Year
entries.desc: This research analyzed data from 7 million students in grades 3–8 across 25,000 schools who completed MAP Growth assessments between 2020–21 and fall 2022. The study found that students are rebounding from the impacts of the pandemic with achievement gaps narrowing from their widest point in spring 2021, though student achievement remains lower than in a typical year and full recovery is likely still several years away.

entries.title: Effects of 4-Day School Weeks on Older Adolescents: Examining Impacts on Academic Achievement, Attendance, and Behavior in High School
entries.desc: This research examined how 4-day school week schedules affect high school students using Oklahoma district data. The study found that 4-day school weeks decrease per-pupil bullying incidents by approximately 39% and fighting incidents by roughly 31%, though no meaningful changes were detected in test scores or attendance rates.

entries.title: Achievement and Growth for English Learners
entries.desc: This working paper examines academic achievement and growth patterns for English Learners from kindergarten through 4th grade across three distinct groups. All three EL groups had lower test scores than never-ELs throughout K-4, with notably different patterns in math versus reading development. The research indicates that summer academic support is essential, as English Learners experience greater skill loss during break periods compared to non-English learners.

entries.title: Within-Year Achievement Gains for English Learners
entries.desc: This working paper examines fall-to-spring achievement gains across grades K–8 for three groups of English Learners, analyzing data from 186,139 ever-ELs and their never-EL peers in 1,520 schools. The study reveals that ever-ELs and current-ELs make larger gains in the early grades but smaller gains in the middle grades, with findings indicating that better academic support is urgently needed for all multilingual students.

entries.title: Dual Language Education and Academic Growth
entries.desc: This 2020 working paper examines math and reading academic achievement and growth in grades 2 to 8 for Hispanic participants and nonparticipants of a Spanish-English dual language program. While dual language students demonstrated faster math growth in early grades, they did not learn more than their peers in the long run. The research reveals that programs aimed at addressing achievement gaps need to consider summer as well as school-year learning.

entries.title: Dual Identification? The Effects of English Learner Status on Special Education Placement
entries.desc: This study examines the effects of English Learner (EL) status on subsequent special education placement through analysis of seven student cohorts tracked over seven years. The research reveals that EL classification resulted in either null effects or slight under-identification for special education placement, with variation by language group.

entries.title: Absenteeism and Achievement
entries.desc: This study, conducted in partnership with Santa Ana Unified School District, examines the impact of chronic absenteeism on student achievement over two years. The research investigates whether reaching a 10% absence threshold represents a point where intervention efforts become increasingly ineffective, providing evidence-based guidance for school and district leaders addressing attendance challenges.

entries.title: Measuring Middle School Achievement Trajectories for College Readiness
entries.desc: This study analyzes academic trajectories for over 360,000 students using college readiness benchmarks in math and reading. The research reveals that higher proportions of Black and Hispanic students are consistently off-track throughout middle school compared to White and Asian peers, and demographic factors at both student and school levels significantly influence whether middle schoolers remain on track for college readiness.

entries.title: Looking Back: How Prior-Year Attendance Impacts Starting Achievement
entries.desc: This working paper examines how the impact of absences carries from one year to the next, focusing on a gap in the chronic absenteeism literature that has largely looked only at end-of-year achievement. The study finds that students with moderate to chronic absenteeism start the following year significantly behind their peers academically, underscoring the compounding nature of attendance problems.

entries.title: Do High Flyers Maintain Their Altitude? Performance Trends of Top Students
entries.desc: This 2013 study from the Thomas B. Fordham Institute tracked students who scored at or above the 90th percentile to examine whether high-achieving students maintained their performance levels. Analyzing data from over 91,000 middle and high school students, researchers found that approximately 60% of initially high-achieving students remained in that category, while those who dropped typically stayed at the 70th percentile or above.

entries.title: Reconciling Recent Evidence on Academic Recovery in the US from 2022 to 2023
entries.desc: This analysis compares NWEA and Education Recovery Scorecard results to examine pandemic recovery progress across 2022–2023. The study explores differences and similarities across the two data sources, authored by researchers from NWEA, Harvard, and Stanford, to provide a comprehensive picture of where and how students are recovering academically.

entries.title: Recovery Still Elusive: 2023-24 Student Achievement Highlights Persistent Achievement Gaps and a Long Road Ahead
entries.desc: This July 2024 report reveals that growth during 2023–24 fell short of pre-pandemic trends in nearly all grades. Marginalized students remain furthest from academic recovery, with students requiring approximately 4.8 additional months of schooling in reading and 4.3 months in math to catch up to pre-COVID performance levels.

entries.title: Effective Summer Programs: Practical Guidance for District Leaders
entries.desc: This June 2024 research brief examines research on summer programs, including their implementation, design, and effectiveness for literacy, math, and social-emotional learning outcomes. The publication offers recommendations for district leaders to use as a framework for planning and implementing effective summer programming, synthesizing evidence on program design choices that maximize student impact.

entries.title: High Dosage Tutoring for Academically At-Risk Students
entries.desc: This research brief examines high dosage tutoring as a strategy to address increased academic risk post-pandemic, noting that if implemented effectively and at proper scale, accelerated academic growth is possible. The brief examines implementation factors including frequency and scheduling, group size, qualified personnel, measurement of outcomes, curriculum, tutor-to-student relationships, and strategies for removing barriers to access.

entries.title: School's in for Summer: A Scalable and Effective Post-Pandemic Academic Intervention
entries.desc: This December 2025 research report examines the effects of post-pandemic summer school on student achievement and district recovery, how program design and implementation aligned with recommended best practices, and how the scale and impact of summer school compared to other interventions such as tutoring. The study by Morton, Goldhaber, McEachin, and Kane provides large-scale evidence on summer programs as a cost-effective recovery strategy.

entries.title: Unequal Access to 8th-Grade Algebra: How School Offerings and Placement Practices Limit Opportunity
entries.desc: This study examines inequitable access to early Algebra across the United States using data from 162,000 eighth-grade students across 22 states. The research analyzes both whether schools offer Algebra in 8th grade and how students are placed when courses are available, finding notable disparities based on school poverty levels, geographic location, and student race/ethnicity.

entries.title: Post-Pandemic School Closure Trends: Schools Serving Black Students Disproportionately Closed
entries.desc: This NWEA research examines permanent school closures and their disparate impacts on communities. The analysis reveals that while most districts maintained open schools, closures disproportionately affected institutions serving high-poverty populations and predominantly Black student bodies, highlighting how pandemic-era closure decisions created unequal consequences across different communities.

entries.title: Boys Regain the Advantage in Middle School STEM Skills: Post-COVID Trends in Gender Achievement Gaps
entries.desc: This study investigates how boys' and girls' achievement in STEM skills were affected during the pandemic by analyzing five years of data from three national assessments. The research examines whether gender gaps widened among low- and high-achieving students, and explores variations in 8th grade algebra enrollment by gender during and after the pandemic.

entries.title: The COVID-19 School Year: Learning and Recovery Across 2020-2021
entries.desc: This study examined student achievement trends before and during the pandemic using test scores from 4.9 million U.S. students in grades 3–8. Students were still behind typical pre-pandemic averages by spring 2021, and while students demonstrated positive gains during 2020–2021, the pandemic simultaneously widened existing educational inequities with much of the academic progress concentrated among higher-performing students.

entries.title: Test Score Patterns Across Three COVID-19-Impacted School Years
entries.desc: This research analyzed data from 5.4 million U.S. students in grades 3–8 to track achievement changes across the pandemic's first two years. Average math test scores in fall 2021 were 0.20–0.27 standard deviations lower compared to 2019, while reading scores declined by 0.09–0.18 standard deviations, with achievement gaps between students in low- and high-poverty schools growing by 0.10–0.20 SD.

entries.title: Family Engagement as a Long-Term Strategy for Continued COVID Recovery
entries.desc: This June 2025 collaborative report examines family engagement as an underutilized approach to supporting student academic growth during COVID recovery. The research provides a broader definition of family engagement, shares insights from recent surveys on family experiences with schools, identifies barriers to engagement, and explains how improved communication strategies can drive more involvement and partnerships with families.

entries.title: Hot Test Days, Lower Math Scores: How Heat Affects Student Achievement
entries.desc: This study examines how rising temperatures impact student learning by analyzing roughly three million test events from fall 2022, 2023, and 2024. Higher temperatures most strongly affect math scores, with students in high-poverty schools facing the greatest impact, addressing a critical infrastructure challenge as many U.S. schools lack adequate cooling systems during increasingly intense heat waves.

entries.title: Integrating Literacy and Science: A Powerful Partnership for Student Success
entries.desc: This research brief explores the connection between science and literacy instruction, documenting the benefits of integrated science and literacy instruction at the elementary level. The publication highlights advantages of integrating instruction while offering research-based guidance on effective implementation methods for elementary educators.

entries.title: Practitioner's Guide to Integrating Literacy and Science
entries.desc: This practitioner's guide accompanies a research brief on integrating literacy and science instruction at the elementary level, providing sample lessons to demonstrate each component in action in an elementary classroom. The guide addresses four key components of effective literacy and science integration, making research-based practices accessible and actionable for classroom teachers.

entries.title: Trend Snapshots: Math Recovery Continues, Reading Remains Stalled in Spring 2025
entries.desc: This brief analyzes recent data from the NWEA MAP Growth National Dashboard, highlighting a modest, incremental recovery post-COVID but stalled progress in reading achievement as of Spring 2025. The analysis examines divergent patterns between mathematics and reading subjects in the ongoing post-pandemic educational landscape across student populations.

entries.title: Summer Credit Recovery: Impact on Newcomer English Learners
entries.desc: This research examines a summer credit recovery program for high school newcomer English learners in California. The study finds that credit recovery increased the number of math, English Language Arts, science, and social science classes taken by newcomer EL students, and the findings suggest the program may positively affect English language development.

entries.title: The Impact of English Learner Reclassification on High School Reading and Academic Progress
entries.desc: This research examines how reclassifying English learner students in 8th grade affects their subsequent academic performance using data from a California district. The study found statistically insignificant effects on standardized ELA test scores in grades 9–11, though results on SAT reading showed potentially negative outcomes, suggesting that reclassification timing may not substantially improve high school reading achievement.

entries.title: Pathways of Mathematics Achievement in Preschool: Examining Executive Function and Task Orientation
entries.desc: This longitudinal study examines how executive function skills in preschoolers predict mathematics achievement growth, exploring whether growth in children's task orientation mediates the connection between executive function and math performance. The study investigates potential sex differences in these associations, finding that task orientation partially explains how executive function relates to math achievement across diverse student populations.

entries.title: COVID's Impact on Science Achievement: Trends from 2019 Through 2024
entries.desc: Building on NWEA's previous research series focused on math and reading, this report represents the first comprehensive large-scale examination of how COVID-19 affected science achievement across U.S. schools. The analysis examines science test scores spanning seven school years to document pandemic impacts on student performance in this subject area.

entries.title: From Loss to Recovery: Diverging Paths and Uneven Gains Across Schools
entries.desc: This analysis examines school recovery patterns following the COVID-19 pandemic by studying initial achievement declines and subsequent gains across a diverse set of schools. By understanding different recovery patterns, the brief offers insights to policymakers and leaders on where to invest for long-term improvement and how to target support where it is needed most.

entries.title: MAP Reading Fluency with Coach: Evidence Base
entries.desc: This July 2024 brief examines the research foundation underlying MAP Reading Fluency with Coach's AI-powered intelligent reading tutor and the research on key elements of early literacy instruction. The document outlines the components of the MAP Reading Fluency with Coach pedagogy and the research base supporting each component, providing transparency about the evidence behind the tool's instructional design.

entries.title: Identifying Students Who Are Off-Track Academically in 9th Grade: The Role of Social-Emotional Learning Trajectories
entries.desc: This working paper examines whether a student's initial level of SEL skills at the start of middle school or change in SEL skills over time is a better indicator of academic struggles in high school. Using longitudinal data to analyze three constructs related to successful transitions, the study finds that while baseline social-emotional skills matter, growth in self-management also predicts high school performance independently.

entries.title: Persistence and Convergence: The End-of-Kindergarten Outcomes of Pre-K Graduates and Their Nonattending Peers
entries.desc: This study examined how pre-K benefits persist through kindergarten for 2,581 low-income children, finding that pre-K graduates outperformed nonattenders in achievement and executive functioning skills at the end of kindergarten, though initial advantages diminished by approximately half during the kindergarten year. The convergence between groups was attributed to nonattenders making greater gains in kindergarten rather than differences in classroom experiences.

entries.title: Leveled Literacy Intervention for Secondary Students: Results from a Randomized Controlled Trial in Oakland Schools
entries.desc: This RCT — the nation's first randomized controlled trial of Leveled Literacy Intervention (LLI) in secondary grades — examined implementation and outcomes across Oakland Unified School District secondary schools, where roughly half of students read multiple grade levels below level. Students did not receive the recommended minimum number of LLI sessions due to delayed starts, scheduling conflicts, and varied fidelity. The intervention showed no positive impact on reading comprehension and produced negative outcomes on ELA/literacy standards mastery, with students pulled from core classes particularly affected.

entries.title: Effectiveness of Reading and Mathematics Software Products: Findings from Two Student Cohorts
entries.desc: This congressionally mandated study evaluated ten computer-based reading and math software products across two student cohorts (2004–05 and 2005–06 school years), using an experimental design.
