Teacher experience with the software showed no systematic relationship to changes in outcomes between years. Only one product had statistically significant positive effects overall, raising questions about the educational value of educational software as deployed in these conditions.

entries.title: Achievement Effects of Four Early Elementary School Math Curricula: Findings from First Graders in 39 Schools
entries.desc: The largest experimental study of its kind, this federal report compared the effectiveness of four early math curricula (Math Expressions, Saxon Math, Investigations in Number/Data/Space, and Scott Foresman-Addison Wesley Mathematics) across 39 schools. Math Expressions and Saxon Math produced significantly higher first-grade achievement than the other two programs, with benefits particularly pronounced for students in low-performing, high-poverty schools.

entries.title: Menu of Measures: High-Quality Measures of Middle Years Math Student Outcomes
entries.desc: Developed with a research panel for Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation grantees, this curated guide provides educators, district leaders, researchers, and funders with high-quality ways to measure middle-grades students' math skills, perceptions and beliefs, and math use beyond the classroom. It includes validity and reliability criteria and context-specific selection guidance, with a focus on improving math outcomes for Black, Latino, and economically disadvantaged middle-grade students.

entries.title: Measuring Children's Progress from Preschool Through Third Grade
entries.desc: This paper examines how to assess child outcomes when evaluating preschool program effectiveness, arguing that traditional standardized tests alone are insufficient for young children. The author advocates for observational measures spanning preschool through elementary grades and a multimethod assessment strategy that avoids high-stakes applications, highlighting the need for classroom quality measurement in program evaluations.

entries.title: Testing New Approaches to Math Tutoring: Lessons from Eight Evaluations
entries.desc: This brief synthesizes findings from evaluations of eight tutoring programs piloting alternative approaches — including virtual and group tutoring — for students in grades 4–10 during the 2021–22 school year. Programs with high attendance, adequate staffing, and strong student-tutor relationships produced the best learning outcomes. The brief finds that group and virtual tutoring can boost math knowledge and may improve students' math confidence and sense of belonging.

entries.title: Examining Student and Teacher Math Learning: Lessons from Three Summer Programs
entries.desc: This brief evaluates three Gates Foundation-funded summer programs for grades 6–8 that used research-based curricula and instructional practices to support diverse learners during 2019–20. Students showed substantial grade improvements in the following semester, and teachers successfully incorporated new instructional approaches from training into their regular practice. The brief identifies actionable lessons around teacher training, program monitoring, and student recruitment.

entries.title: Math Corps Successfully Replicated
entries.desc: Mathematica independently evaluated the replication of Math Corps — a summer camp for middle school students that originated at Wayne State University — across three new cities (Cleveland, Philadelphia, and Utica) funded by the National Science Foundation (2017–19). All three replication sites successfully served their priority communities, maintained strong attendance, and produced statistically significant improvements on Math Corps assessments from camp start to completion.

entries.title: Sustaining Effective Teacher Practice: The Impact of the EL Education Language Arts Curriculum and Professional Development on Teachers' Instruction
entries.desc: This i3-funded study examined how the Teacher Potential Project (TPP) — combining a Common Core-aligned EL Education Language Arts curriculum with intensive professional development — affected teachers' instructional practices compared to district-provided curricula. Significantly more TPP teachers encouraged higher-order thinking, had students cite textual evidence in writing, and engaged students in reading, writing, and speaking about texts, especially in the second year of implementation.

entries.title: Teacher Factors That Drive Improvement in Secondary Writing Outcomes: A Landscape Analysis
entries.desc: This Gates Foundation-funded brief synthesizes an academic literature review identifying instructional skills and practices that support writing improvements for grades 6–12 students. Teachers influence writing outcomes both through explicit instructional practices and through subtler means — how they engage with students, provide support, and offer feedback — making teacher development a critical lever for secondary writing improvement.

entries.title: What Matters for Student Achievement? Exploring Teacher Instructional Practices and the Role of School-Level and Student-Level Characteristics
entries.desc: This exploratory study analyzed data from 10,716 students taught by 214 ELA teachers across 63 schools to identify which instructional practices relate to higher student achievement. General instruction and classroom management practices — fostering engagement, facilitating discussion, minimizing disruptions, and maintaining a positive climate — showed significant associations with achievement, more so than specific reading/writing strategies. School characteristics like racial diversity, student-teacher ratios, and enrollment moderated these relationships.

entries.title: Strengthening Principal Preparation Through On-The-Job Training: Insights from KIPP's Strategies for Developing Assistant Principals as School Leaders
entries.desc: This publication examines how KIPP, the nation's largest charter school network, developed a structured internal program to cultivate school leaders by turning the assistant principal experience into a deliberate leadership development pathway. Rather than relying solely on external recruitment, KIPP's strategies address the ongoing need for qualified principals through on-the-job training and competency-building for assistant principals.

entries.title: Implementation and Impacts of Pay-for-Performance: The 2010 Teacher Incentive Fund (TIF) Grantees After Three Years
entries.desc: This NCEE study snapshot examines how districts implemented performance-based compensation systems funded through 2010 federal Teacher Incentive Fund grants. After three years, pay-for-performance had small positive impacts on students' math and reading achievement in the ten evaluated districts. However, few districts structured bonuses to align well with TIF guidance, and many teachers misunderstood their eligibility or potential earnings.

entries.title: Districts and Data: Developing Capacity for Effective Data Use
entries.desc: This issue brief examines the challenges school districts face in using data strategically, noting that while large interconnected datasets have raised educator awareness of data's value, many districts struggle to identify the right questions, datasets, or interpretive methods. The brief offers four recommendations: build a culture of data use, ask the right questions, correctly interpret findings, and dedicate adequate resources to analysis.

entries.title: How Community Colleges Address Basic Needs and Financial Stability of Low-Income Students to Boost College Completion
entries.desc: This report examines the Working Students Success Network (WSSN), a comprehensive support strategy for low-income students and students of color across 19 community colleges, integrating financial literacy, budgeting, and career planning with credential attainment. The research found that receiving intensive one-on-one service mattered more than multi-pillar group services, though colleges could reach more students through group-based delivery when individualized approaches were adapted to individual needs.

entries.title: From Outreach to Enrollment: Strategies to Engage Adults in Education Beyond High School
entries.desc: This Lumina Foundation-funded brief examines marketing, outreach, and enrollment strategies employed by states receiving Adult Promise grants to engage adult learners (ages 25–64) in higher education. Key findings emphasize that appropriate messaging is critical because adult learners' priorities differ from traditional enrollees, high-touch personalized guidance through enrollment and financial aid is essential, and digital tools can make the enrollment process more convenient for adults balancing work and family demands.

entries.title: Infusing Academics into Career and Technical Education
entries.desc: This issue brief examines the integration of academic content — particularly mathematics — into career and technical education (CTE) curricula, based on feedback from CTE educators. The research finds that incorporating academic learning into CTE requires substantial time, effort, and resources, and highlights the real implementation challenges educators and curriculum developers must navigate when blending vocational training with academic content.

entries.title: The NIH Undergraduate Scholarship Program: Career Outcomes of Scholars and Non-Awarded Finalists
entries.desc: This evaluation compared career outcomes between NIH Undergraduate Scholarship Program recipients and finalist applicants who did not receive awards, examining whether the program effectively supports disadvantaged undergraduate science students in pursuing biomedical research careers. Scholars were achieving career milestones at high rates and making steady progress toward becoming biomedical researchers, substantially outpacing non-awarded finalists, suggesting the program is operating as intended.

entries.title: Disproportionality in School Discipline: An Assessment in Maryland through 2018
entries.desc: This Regional Educational Laboratory Mid-Atlantic study analyzed disciplinary removal trends in Maryland schools from 2009/10 to 2017/18 using administrative data. Although overall exclusionary discipline rates declined, Black students and students with disabilities continued to be suspended and expelled at more than twice the rates of other students. Even for identical infractions, these groups were significantly more likely to receive out-of-school suspensions, suggesting implicit bias as a contributing factor.

entries.title: Preparing for Life After High School: The Characteristics and Experiences of Youth in Special Education
entries.desc: This NCEE evaluation brief summarizes findings from the National Longitudinal Transition Study 2012, which surveyed secondary school youth with disabilities and their parents to assess IDEA implementation. Youth with disabilities were found to be more socioeconomically disadvantaged, to demonstrate lower academic performance, and to be less likely to take steps toward postsecondary education and employment, with youth with intellectual disabilities, autism, and multiple disabilities facing the highest risks.

entries.title: Building Teacher Capacity to Support English Language Learners in Schools Receiving School Improvement Grants
entries.desc: This NCEE evaluation brief examined staffing and professional development strategies for serving English Language Learners in 11 high-ELL schools receiving federal School Improvement Grant funding (2010–13). Few schools leveraged staffing strategies or hired with ELL expertise in mind, while teachers widely participated in ELL-related PD but received limited hours. Schools that devoted a greater share of PD to ELL topics had teachers more likely to report improved effectiveness.

entries.title: Not Just Numeracy and Literacy: Theory of Mind Development and School Readiness among Low-Income Children
entries.desc: This longitudinal study of 120 low-income preschool and kindergarten children examined how theory of mind development contributes to school readiness, exploring links among theory of mind, understanding of teaching, learning behaviors, and literacy and numeracy skills at school entry. Children's understanding of teaching was particularly important — it predicted changes in literacy scores over time — underscoring that sociocognitive development matters for school readiness beyond traditional academic skills.

entries.title: Student Characteristics and Achievement in 22 KIPP Middle Schools
entries.desc: This multi-year evaluation of the Knowledge Is Power Program (KIPP) employed a matched comparison group design to examine impacts on disadvantaged students across 22 KIPP middle schools. For the vast majority of KIPP schools in the evaluation, impacts on state assessment scores in math and reading were positive, statistically significant, and educationally substantial, providing rigorous evidence for the model's effectiveness.

entries.title: Pre-Kindergarten Impacts Over Time: An Analysis of KIPP Charter Schools
entries.desc: This study tracked outcomes over five years to determine whether benefits from KIPP Pre-K enrollment persisted as students progressed through elementary school. After five years, KIPP Pre-K combined with KIPP early elementary school showed positive and statistically significant impacts on reading and math achievement, with potential advantages for executive function development. Reading skill improvements were sustained over time, though reading comprehension impacts largely dissipated by grade 2.

entries.title: Scaling Programs with Research Evidence and Effectiveness (SPREE)
entries.desc: Published in The Foundation Review, this article describes the SPREE process — Scaling Programs with Research Evidence and Effectiveness — a framework used by the Corporation for National and Community Service since 2016 to help foundations and grantees identify and scale effective interventions. SPREE uses evaluation and implementation science to determine which programs produce desired outcomes and which organizations are ready for expansion, and can help organizations develop evaluation cultures even when none previously existed.

entries.title: Challenges in Adolescent Reading Intervention: Evidence from a Randomized Control Trial
entries.desc: Reporting on the nation's first RCT of Leveled Literacy Intervention (LLI) in secondary grades in Oakland schools, this study found no measurable improvement in reading comprehension and adverse impacts on ELA/literacy standards mastery. Students pulled from regular classes for LLI were particularly negatively affected, possibly due to missing grade-level content. The research underscores the difficulty of adapting elementary interventions for adolescents without accounting for curriculum continuity.

entries.title: Promise Neighborhoods Case Studies
entries.desc: This case study analysis examined how five Promise Neighborhoods grantees — part of a $100 million federal initiative across 48 lead agencies in 23 states — designed their programs, selected services, and navigated early implementation challenges. Promise Neighborhoods aim to build integrated support systems from early childhood through career preparation in high-poverty communities, offsetting the effects of poverty on children's educational and life outcomes.

entries.title: Missouri's Teacher Career Ladder Program
entries.desc: This working paper analyzed nine years of longitudinal data from 524 Missouri school districts, comparing achievement in districts participating in the state's Career Ladder teacher compensation program against matched non-participating districts. The study found that any positive effect on test scores was likely very small, ranging from negligible to no measurable impact, contributing evidence to debates about differentiated teacher pay structures.

entries.title: Landscape Analysis of Non-Cognitive Measures
entries.desc: This landscape analysis collected and analyzed information about instruments designed to measure learning strategies and student mindsets in middle childhood, examining tools for assessing non-cognitive skills relevant to academic outcomes. The project was part of a broader effort to understand what measures are available, valid, and reliable for assessing the non-academic factors that influence student success.

entries.title: Measuring Charter School Effectiveness Across States
entries.desc: This report outlines methodology developed with New Leaders for identifying effective schools and teachers across a consortium of 99 charter schools in 18 states and Washington, DC. The approach was customized based on each partner's priorities and available performance data, with plans for future refinement and incorporation of additional metrics, offering a model for multi-state charter effectiveness measurement.

entries.title: Word Generation
entries.desc: Word Generation is an approach to vocabulary building in which students are encouraged to discuss and read about topics containing target words, targeting grades 6–8. The Evidence for ESSA review rated it Promising based on positive effects on GMRC reading comprehension outcomes. Key studies showed modest gains in comprehension but limited transfer to vocabulary growth.

entries.title: The Project Approach
entries.desc: The Project Approach is a set of teaching strategies that helps teachers guide Pre-K children through in-depth investigations of real-world topics, balancing play, direct instruction, and extended project work including fieldwork and investigation. Rated Promising by Evidence for ESSA, the program demonstrated positive effects on early literacy outcomes in a key study from 2008. The curriculum model supports teacher-facilitated inquiry projects that can extend over weeks.

entries.title: PAX Good Behavior Game — Reading
entries.desc: The PAX Good Behavior Game organizes students into teams where teachers monitor classroom behavior using a checkmark system; the team receiving the fewest checkmarks earns a small privilege, encouraging peer accountability and positive behavioral support in grades 1–3. Rated Promising by Evidence for ESSA based on a 2015 study showing positive effects on reading outcomes. The behavior management framework is embedded within daily reading instruction.

entries.title: REDI (Research-Based, Developmentally Informed)
entries.desc: REDI is a preschool program designed primarily to enhance executive functions and school readiness among disadvantaged students, incorporating the Preschool PATHS® Curriculum for social-emotional learning alongside phonological awareness training and alphabet activities. The program provides in-person and online teacher training with ongoing coaching support. Evidence for ESSA rated it Promising based on studies from 2008 and 2014 showing improvements in reading-readiness outcomes.

entries.title: IMSE's Orton-Gillingham Plus (OG+)
entries.desc: IMSE's OG+ is a Structured Literacy core foundational skills program based on Orton-Gillingham principles, designed for whole-class, small-group, and one-on-one reading and spelling instruction in grades K–5. The program incorporates multimodal techniques and is supported by professional development and coaching services. Evidence for ESSA rated it Promising based on a 2025 study showing improved literacy outcomes for K–3 students.

entries.title: Corrective Reading — Elementary
entries.desc: Corrective Reading is a program for upper-elementary struggling readers that uses scripted, structured lessons focused on phonics, fluency, and comprehension, employing explicit and systematic methods with rapid-paced activities. The intervention, studied in grades 3 and 5, was delivered with substantial teacher professional development support. Evidence for ESSA rated it Promising based on a 2007 study.

entries.title: Every Classroom, Every Day (ECED) — Reading
entries.desc: Every Classroom, Every Day (ECED) provides a structured literacy curriculum based on authentic expository texts as a supplement to regular English instruction, doubling student exposure to language arts in grades 9–10. The two-year program spans Reading Matters (year one, focusing on comprehension) and Writing Matters (year two, emphasizing communication skills), both employing instructional strategies called the Power 10. Evidence for ESSA rated it Promising based on a 2015 study.

entries.title: Spell Read
entries.desc: Spell Read emphasizes systematic and explicit fluency-oriented instruction in phonics for struggling readers in grades 3 and 5, incorporating shared reading with leveled books, discussion, and writing activities alongside extensive teacher professional development. Evidence for ESSA rated it Promising based on a 2007 study showing improvements in reading fluency and decoding. The program targets students significantly below grade level in reading.

entries.title: National Geographic Reach
entries.desc: National Geographic Reach is a comprehensive, content-based core ESL program for students in grades K–5 designed to provide scaffolded instruction and flexible learning opportunities, helping English Language Learners achieve language proficiency and reading independence. Evidence for ESSA rated it Promising based on a 2011 study focused on 3rd grade that found improved reading comprehension outcomes. The program uses authentic National Geographic content to build academic language and literacy.

entries.title: Responsive Classroom — Reading
entries.desc: Responsive Classroom is an approach focused on building social-emotional skills in elementary students (grades 1–5) through extensive teacher professional development including summer training and in-school coaching. The program embeds modeling of prosocial behavior, collaboration, and self-control into daily reading instruction. Evidence for ESSA rated it Promising based on a 2007 study showing positive effects on reading outcomes.

entries.title: Teacher Certification in Texas: District Strategies to Recruit and Retain a Credentialed Workforce
entries.desc: Texas faces substantial teacher staffing challenges, with over two-thirds of newly hired teachers entering through emergency permits or without full certification, creating equity disparities across districts. This report examines how districts with higher certification rates maintain credentialed workforces through partnerships, recruitment strategies, Grow Your Own programs, and teacher residencies. The report includes policy recommendations for districts, preparation programs, and the legislature in light of House Bill 2 and the PREP Program Allotment.

entries.title: Why Expert Advising Matters for College Access
entries.source: Brookings Institution (Brown Center on Education Policy)
entries.desc: This Brown Center on Education Policy article evaluates Tennessee's Advise TN program, a statewide college advising initiative that increased college enrollment by approximately 4 percentage points overall, with particularly strong results among Hispanic students and those in rural schools. The effectiveness stems from specialized advisors providing intensive, hands-on support with concrete tasks like FAFSA completion and college applications rather than general counseling. Published May 11, 2026, the research highlights how expert, task-specific college advising can drive meaningful improvements in post-secondary access.

entries.title: Positionality in Teaching: Implications for Advancing Social Justice
entries.desc: This article examines how educators teaching about justice and equity must model these values through vulnerability and self-reflection, openly discussing their mistakes and struggles regarding power, privilege, and oppression. The authors argue that this teacher positionality creates an environment where students feel safe engaging in critical reflection about their own relationships to issues of justice. Published 2019, the piece addresses professional development implications for educators working in equity-focused contexts.

entries.title: Three Measures of Test Adaptation Based on Optimal Test Information
entries.desc: This study introduces three new measures designed to evaluate test adaptation by analyzing the information provided to individual test-takers, offering theoretical, engineering, and operational perspectives on adaptation effectiveness. The measures were applied to adaptive, multistage, and fixed-form test simulations to compare their performance. Published 2020, the research contributes to the design and evaluation of computerized adaptive testing systems used in K–12 assessment.

entries.title: Validating the SEDA Measures of District Educational Opportunities via a Common Assessment
entries.desc: This convergent validity study compares growth estimates from the Stanford Educational Data Archive (SEDA) against NWEA MAP Growth assessments across grades 3–8, finding strong precision-adjusted correlations of .90 in math and .82 in ELA. The research demonstrates substantial agreement between the two measurement approaches for tracking district-level educational opportunities. Published 2019, the study provides evidence supporting the validity of using SEDA as a large-scale proxy for assessment-based growth measures.

entries.title: A Posterior Predictive Model Checking Method Assuming Posterior Normality for Item Response Theory
entries.desc: This research investigates violations of local independence assumptions in unidimensional item response theory models and proposes a Bayesian posterior predictive model checking method (PPMC-N) that accounts for parameter uncertainty when evaluating local dependence. The method was estimated through full-information maximum likelihood and addresses a key challenge in ensuring the psychometric integrity of educational assessments. Published 2019, the study supports the technical validity of large-scale adaptive tests used in K–12 education.

entries.title: The Influence of Rapidly Guessed Item Responses on Teacher Value-Added Estimates: Implications for Policy and Practice
entries.desc: This research examines whether students' low-effort test behaviors (rapid guessing) affect teacher evaluations through value-added estimates, finding that while rapid guessing varies across grades, subjects, and teachers, it did not substantially impact teacher performance assessments. Published 2018, the study provides reassurance to policymakers that teacher value-added models remain robust despite test disengagement. The authors recommend ongoing educator awareness of test effort issues when interpreting student achievement data.

entries.title: Are Test and Academic Disengagement Related? Implications for Measurement and Practice
entries.desc: This study examines the connection between academic disengagement behaviors (chronic absenteeism, course failures) and test disengagement behaviors (rapid guessing), exploring whether social-emotional factors including low self-efficacy and poor self-management serve as common underlying causes. Published 2018, the research suggests that schools should consider both behavioral and motivational dimensions when interpreting student assessment data. The findings have implications for integrating SEL supports with academic achievement monitoring.

entries.title: Concurrent Validity of the Independent Reading Level Assessment Framework and a State Assessment
entries.desc: This study examines the validity of the Independent Reading Level Assessment (IRLA) framework as a screening tool within Response to Intervention models, analyzing how effectively IRLA functions across different grade levels, programs, and demographic categories. Published in the Journal of At-Risk Issues (2016), the findings suggest IRLA may help bridge the gap between initial screening and providing intensive, systematic reading instruction. The research supports the use of IRLA as a diagnostic complement to state accountability assessments.

entries.title: MAP Growth National Dashboard
entries.desc: NWEA's MAP Growth National Dashboard provides timely trends and data on U.S. student academic achievement and growth for grades K–8, enabling district leaders, policymakers, and education stakeholders to contextualize local data within broader national and state frameworks. The complimentary public resource reveals areas of strength and highlights disparities across student populations, connecting real-time data to NWEA's 2025 MAP Growth Norms. Published 2025, the dashboard supports data-driven decision-making for pandemic recovery and ongoing accountability monitoring.

entries.title: Lessons From Implementing Courseware in Gateway Statistics Courses
entries.desc: Digital Promise studied implementation factors and student outcomes associated with a digital courseware product for undergraduate statistics, a must-pass course for many degree programs. Collecting data from 63 classes, the team measured the frequency with which teaching practices backed by research evidence were employed and students' cognitive and affective outcomes.

entries.title: Educator Perspectives on Research Using Digital Learning Platforms
entries.desc: As digital learning platforms increasingly serve as educational research infrastructure, a persistent divide remains between researchers and frontline practitioners. This descriptive study, conducted with SEERNet, surveyed 43 educators to explore perceptions of the benefits, challenges, and ethical dimensions of DLP-based research across K-12 and higher education contexts.

entries.title: Change Management and AI Tools in Literacy Instruction
entries.desc: This report examines a research-practice-industry partnership between Digital Promise, three school districts, and Coursemojo — an AI-powered English language arts instructional assistant. Grounded in John Kotter's 8-Step Process for Leading Change and the Digital Promise AI Literacy Framework, the study explores how change management and AI literacy support high-fidelity technology implementation in fifth through eighth grades.

entries.title: ATS Hub Field Note — Breakthrough Innovation: A Working Definition
entries.desc: Breakthrough innovation in education requires more than incremental improvement — it demands fundamental shifts in how problems are understood, addressed, and measured. This field note presents a working definition of breakthrough innovation in education as a highly novel advance that redefines entrenched problems, enables significant improvements in learning outcomes, expands participation and access, and drives system-level change.

entries.title: ClassWide Peer Tutoring (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: ClassWide Peer Tutoring (CWPT) is a peer-assisted instructional strategy designed to be integrated with most existing reading curricula. This approach provides students with increased opportunities to practice reading skills by asking questions and receiving immediate feedback from a peer tutor. Pairs of students take turns tutoring each other to reinforce concepts and skills initially taught by the teacher. The teacher creates age-appropriate peer teaching materials for the peer tutors; these materials take into account tutees’ language skills and disabilities.

entries.title: Peer Tutoring and Response Groups (English Language Learners)
entries.desc: Peer Tutoring and Response Groupsaims to improve the language and achievement of English learners by pairing or grouping students to work on a task. The students may be grouped by age or ability, or the groups may be mixed. Peer tutoring typically consists of two students assuming the roles of tutor and tutee, or “coach and player” roles. Peer response groups give four or five students shared responsibility for a task, such as editing a passage or reading and answering comprehension questions. Both peer tutoring pairs and peer response groups emphasize peer interaction and discussion to complete a task.

entries.title: Start Making a Reader Today® (SMART®) (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: Start Making a Reader Today® (SMART®)is a volunteer tutoring program for students in grades K–2 who are at risk of reading failure. The program is designed to be a low-cost, easy-to-implement intervention. Volunteer tutors go into schools where at least 40% of students are eligible for free or reduced-price lunch and read one-on-one with students twice a week for 30 minutes. Typically, one volunteer works with two students on four types of activities: reading to the student, reading with the student, re-reading with the student, and asking the student questions about what has been read. The program also gives each student two new books a month to encourage families to read together.

entries.title: Fluency Formula (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: Fluency Formula™is a supplemental curriculum designed to promote reading fluency in grades 1–6. The program emphasizes automatic recognition of words, decoding accuracy, and oral expressiveness as the foundations for building reading fluency. A daily 10- to 15-minute lesson is delivered in the classroom. Students participate in whole-class, small-group, and individual practice activities using workbooks, read-aloud anthologies, library books, fluency activity cards, and audio CDs. The curriculum encourages at-home practice and includes aFluency Formula™ Assessment System, which allows teachers to assess student fluency using 1-minute grade-level passages and a timer.

entries.title: Stepping Stones to Literacy (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: Stepping Stones to Literacy (SSL)is a supplemental curriculum designed to promote listening, print conventions, phonological awareness, phonemic awareness, and serial processing/rapid naming (quickly naming familiar visual symbols and stimuli, such as letters or colors). The program targets older preschool and kindergarten students who are considered to be underachieving readers, based on teacher’s recommendations, assessments, and systematic screening. Students participate in 10- to 20-minute daily lessons in a small group or individually. The curriculum consists of 25 lessons, for a total of 9–15 hours of instructional time.

entries.title: Read, Write & Type! (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: Read, Write & Type!™ Learning Systemis a software program with supporting materials designed to teach beginning reading skills by emphasizing writing as a way to learn to read. The program was developed for students ages 6–9 years who are just beginning to read, and for students who are struggling readers and writers. The main goal ofRead, Write & Type!™is to help students develop an awareness of the 40 English phonemes and the ability to associate each phoneme with a letter or a combination of letters and a finger stroke on the keyboard. Other goals of the program include identifying phonemes in words; and fluency in sounding out, typing, and reading regularly spelled words.

entries.title: Building Decision Skills (Character Education)
entries.desc: Building Decision Skillsaims to raise middle and high school students’ awareness of ethics, help them gain experience developing core values, and give them strategies for dealing with ethical dilemmas.Building Decision Skillsconsists of 10 lessons that can fill 2 consecutive weeks of daily lessons or be drawn out over a longer period. Using readings, handouts, and overheads, the teacher covers key concepts. Students are encouraged to think about the key concepts through small-group activities, class discussions, and homework assignments. The program also includes schoolwide components such as group discussions, seminars, and assemblies, and can be combined with service learning.

entries.title: Caring School Community (CSC) (Character Education)
entries.desc: Caring School Community™ (CSC) is a modified version of a program formerly known as theChild Development Project.CSCis a multiyear school improvement program that involves all students in grades K–6. The program aims to promote core values, prosocial behavior, and a schoolwide feeling of community. The program consists of four elements originally developed for theChild Development Project: class meeting lessons, cross-age “buddies” programs, “homeside” activities, and schoolwide community. Class lessons provide teachers and students with a forum to get to know one another, discuss issues, identify and solve problems collaboratively, and make a range of decisions that affect classroom life. Cross-age buddies activities pair whole classes of older and younger students for academic and recreational activities that build caring cross-age relationships and create a schoolwide climate of trust. Homeside activities include short conversational activities that are sent home with students for them to do with parents or caregivers and then to discuss back in their classroom. The activities incorporate the families’ perspectives, cultures, and traditions, thereby promoting interpersonal understanding. Schoolwide community-building activities bring students, parents, and school staff together to create new school traditions.

entries.title: Little Books (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: TheLittle Booksare a set of books designed for interactive book reading between parents and children or between teachers and students. The books use thematic topics familiar to children. They are written with high-frequency words and use simple phrases and sentences. The books also have strong links between illustrations and text.

entries.title: High School Redirection (Dropout Prevention)
entries.desc: High School Redirectionis an alternative high school program for youth considered at risk of dropping out. The program emphasizes basic skills development (with a particular focus on reading skills) and offers limited extra-curricular activities. The schools operate in economically disadvantaged areas and serve students who have dropped out in the past, who are teen parents, who have poor test scores, or who are over-age for their grade. To foster a sense of community, the schools are small, and teachers are encouraged to act as mentors as well as instructors.

entries.title: Positive Action (Character Education)
entries.desc: Positive Action, at its core, teaches the philosophy that you feel good about yourself when you do positive actions and there is always a positive way to do everything. It is illustrated by the the Thoughts-Actions-Feelings about self Circle where positive thoughts lead to positive actions, which in turn lead to positive feelings about oneself. It also teaches the positive actions for physical, intellectual, social and emotional areas—the whole self. It is a Pre-K-12 school-based program that aims to promote good behavior while disrupting problem behaviors, improves academics, and develops social-emotional and character skills while improving mental and physical health and self-concept. Lessons are scripted and use a wide variety of strategies: classroom discussion, role-play, games, songs, journals, manipulatives and activity sheets and text booklets.

entries.title: Twelve Together (Dropout Prevention)
entries.desc: Twelve Togetheris a 1-year peer support and mentoring program for middle and early high school students that offers weekly after-school discussion groups led by trained volunteer adult facilitators. Each peer discussion group consists of about 12 participants who are a mix of students at high- and low-risk of academic failure. Group discussions are based on topics of student interest, usually focusing on personal, family, and social issues. The program also offers homework assistance, trips to college campuses, and an annual weekend retreat.

entries.title: Dialogic Reading (Early Childhood Education)
entries.desc: Dialogic Reading is an interactive shared picture book reading practice designed to enhance young children’s language and literacy skills. During the shared reading practice, the adult and the child switch roles so that the child learns to become the storyteller with the assistance of the adult, who functions as an active listener and questioner.

entries.title: Bilingual Cooperative Integrated Reading and Composition (BCIRC) (English Language Learners)
entries.desc: TheBilingual Cooperative Integrated Reading and Composition (BCIRC)program, an adaptation of theCooperative Integrated Reading and Composition (CIRC)program, was designed to help Spanish-speaking students succeed in reading Spanish and then making a successful transition to English reading. In the adaptation, students complete tasks that focus on reading, writing, and language activities in Spanish and English, while working in small cooperative learning groups. The intervention focuses on students in grades 2–5.

entries.title: Talent Search (Dropout Prevention)
entries.desc: Talent Searchaims to help low-income and first-generation college students (those whose parents do not have 4-year college degrees) complete high school and gain access to college through a combination of services designed to improve academic achievement and increase access to financial aid. Services include test taking and study skills assistance, academic advising, tutoring, career development, college campus visits, and financial aid application assistance.

entries.title: Financial Incentives for Teen Parents to Stay in School (Dropout Prevention)
entries.desc: Financial incentives for teen parents are components of state welfare programs intended to encourage enrollment, attendance, and completion of high school as a means of increasing employment and earnings and reducing welfare dependence. The incentives take the form of bonuses and sanctions to the welfare grant related to school enrollment, performance, and completion. The programs typically provide case management and social services to supplement financial incentives.

entries.title: Phonological Awareness Training (Early Childhood Education)
entries.desc: Phonological Awareness Trainingis a general practice aimed at enhancing young children’s phonological awareness abilities. Phonological awareness refers to the ability to detect or manipulate the sounds in words independent of meaning and is considered a precursor to reading.Phonological Awareness Trainingcan involve various training activities that focus on teaching children to identify, detect, delete, segment, or blend segments of spoken words (i.e., words, syllables, onsets and rimes, phonemes) or that focus on teaching children to detect, identify, or produce rhyme or alliteration.

entries.title: Phonological Awareness Training plus Letter Knowledge Training (Early Childhood Education)
entries.desc: Phonological Awareness Training plus Letter Knowledge Trainingis a general practice aimed at enhancing young children’s phonological awareness, print awareness, and early reading abilities. Phonological awareness, the ability to detect or manipulate the sounds in words independent of meaning, is considered to be a precursor to reading. Phonological awareness training (without letter knowledge training) can involve various training activities that focus on teaching children to identify, detect, delete, segment, or blend segments of spoken words (i.e., words, syllables, onsets and rimes, phonemes) or that focus on teaching children to detect, identify, or produce rhyme or alliteration. The added letter knowledge training component includes teaching children the letters of the alphabet and making an explicit link between letters and sounds.

entries.title: The Expert Mathematician (Middle School Mathematics)
entries.desc: The Expert Mathematicianis designed to help middle school students develop the thinking processes for mathematical applications and communication. A 3-year program of instruction,The Expert Mathematicianuses a software and print materials package with 196 lessons that teach theLogoprogramming language. Each lesson ranges from 40–120 minutes. A test of unit concepts is administered at the end of each instructional unit. The curriculum covers general mathematics, pre-algebra, and algebra I.

entries.title: Vocabulary Improvement Program for English Language Learners and Their Classmates (VIP) (English Language Learners)
entries.desc: TheVocabulary Improvement Program for English Language Learners and Their Classmates (VIP)is a vocabulary development curriculum for English language learners and native English speakers in grades 4–6. The 15-week program includes 30–45 minute whole class and small group activities that aim to increase students’ understanding of target vocabulary words included in a weekly reading assignment.

entries.title: Instructional Conversations and Literature Logs (English Language Learners)
entries.desc: The goal ofInstructional Conversationsis to help English learners develop reading comprehension ability along with English language proficiency. Acting as facilitators, teachers engage students in discussions about stories, key concepts, and related personal experiences, allowing students to appreciate and build on each others’ experiences, knowledge, and understanding.Literature Logsrequire students to respond in writing to prompts or questions related to sections of stories. These responses are then shared in small groups or with a partner.

entries.title: Too Good For Violence (TGFV) (Character Education)
entries.desc: Too Good for Violencepromotes character values, social-emotional skills, and healthy beliefs in elementary and middle school students. The program includes seven lessons per grade level for elementary school (K–5) and nine lessons per grade level for middle school (6–8). All lessons are scripted and engage students through role-playing and cooperative learning games, small group activities, and classroom discussions. Students are encouraged to apply these skills to different contexts.Too Good for Violencealso includes optional parental and community involvement elements.

entries.title: Enhanced Proactive Reading (English Language Learners)
entries.desc: Enhanced Proactive Reading, a comprehensive, integrated reading, language arts, and English language development curriculum, is targeted to first-grade English learners experiencing problems with learning to read through conventional instruction. The curriculum is implemented as small group daily reading instruction, during which instructors provide opportunities for participation from all students and give feedback on student responses.

entries.title: Facing History and Ourselves (Character Education)
entries.desc: Facing History and Ourselvesaims to promote core character education values and to help middle and high school students develop moral reasoning skills. Students examine historical events; in particular, the events that led to World War II and the Holocaust. Teachers participate in professional development seminars and apply the content and approaches to their own teaching or school program.Facing History and Ourselvesalso includes optional schoolwide components (such as guest speakers and videos).

entries.title: Lessons in Character (Character Education)
entries.desc: Lessons in Characteris designed to promote elementary and middle school students’ knowledge about core character education values and, through that knowledge, shape children’s positive behaviors and support academic success. It consists of 24 lessons organized around weekly themes, writing activities, and class projects. Teachers introduce the theme with a story that shows a value in action; students then engage that topic with a variety of activities. The program also includes daily oral language development and weekly writing assignments, optional parts of the program’s implementation.

entries.title: Too Good for Drugs (TGFD) (Character Education)
entries.desc: Too Good for Drugs™ is designed to promote elementary and middle school students’ life skills, character values, resistance skills to negative peer influence, and resistance to the use of illegal drugs, alcohol, and tobacco. The program is based on classroom discussions and structured activities that center on interactive learning and skill-building exercises. Students engage in role-play and cooperative learning games and are encouraged to apply the skills to different contexts.Too Good for Drugs™ also includes the optional elements of parental and community involvement.

entries.title: DaisyQuest (Early Childhood Education)
entries.desc: DaisyQuestis a software bundle that offers computer-assisted instruction in phonological awareness, targeting children 3–7 years old (or preschool to grade 2). The instructional activities, framed in a fairy tale involving a search for a friendly dragon named Daisy, teach children how to recognize words that rhyme; words that have the same beginning, middle, and ending sounds; and words that can be formed from a series of phonemes presented separately. Activities also teach children how to count the number of sounds in words.

entries.title: Too Good for Drugs and Violence (TGFD & V) (Character Education)
entries.desc: Too Good for Drugs and Violenceis designed to promote high school students’ pro-social skills, positive character traits, and violence- and drug-free norms. The curriculum consists of 14 core lessons, as well as an additional 12 lessons that can be infused into other subject areas (such as English, science, and social studies). Teachers participate in 10 staff development lessons. The program includes optional elements of family and community involvement.

entries.title: Fast ForWord® (English Language Learners)
entries.desc: Fast ForWord® is a computer-based reading program intended to help students develop and strengthen the cognitive skills necessary for successful reading and learning. The program, which is designed to be used 30 to 100 minutes a day, five days a week, for 4 to 16 weeks, includes two components.

entries.title: Reading Mastery (English Language Learners)
entries.desc: Reading Mastery is designed to provide systematic reading instruction to students in grades K–6. Reading Mastery can be used as an intervention program for struggling readers, as a supplement to a school’s core reading program, or as a stand-alone reading program, and is available in three versions. During the implementation of Reading Mastery, students are grouped with other students at a similar reading level, based on program placement tests. The program includes a continuous monitoring component.

entries.title: National Institute for School Leadership (NISL) (School Leadership Review Protocol )
entries.desc: The NISL program (formerly the NISL Executive Development Program) is a professional development program that aims to develop effective, strategic school leaders focused on preparing schools to provide high-quality instruction in a supportive learning environment. NISL is organized around 12 two-day professional development course units that are offered over 12–15 months. Each unit offers tools and diagnostic instruments to assess participants’ leadership skills and school climates. As part of the program, school leaders learn how to apply NISL’s instructional coaching model with teachers in their schools. The coaching model is a formal process in which school leaders work with teachers over multiple cycles of coaching to improve teacher effectiveness. The model is designed to support teachers in core content areas like English language arts, mathematics, social studies, and science.

entries.title: Project Based Inquiry Science (PBIScience) (Primary Science)
entries.desc: Project-Based Inquiry Science™ is a science curriculum for students in grades 6–8 with approximately 13 independent instructional units, each covering a topic in life science, earth science, or physical science. Students work together in small groups to conduct hands-on explorations or investigations, read relevant informational texts, reflect on what they have learned, and apply new knowledge.

entries.title: Full Option Science System (FOSS) (Primary Science)
entries.desc: Full Option Science System™ (FOSS) is a science curriculum for students in kindergarten to grade 8 with content in physical science, earth science, and life science. The curriculum consists of a series of 8- to 9-week modules in kindergarten to grade 5, and 9- or 18-week courses in grades 6 to 8.

entries.title: Accelerated Math®  (Secondary Mathematics)
entries.desc: Accelerated Math®, published by Renaissance Learning, is a software tool that provides practice problems for students in grades K–12 and provides teachers with reports to monitor student progress. Accelerated Math® creates individualized student assignments, scores the assignments, and generates reports on student progress. The software is typically used with the math curriculum being used in the classroom to add practice for students and help teachers differentiate instruction through the program’s progress-monitoring data.

entries.title: Cognitive Tutor® Algebra II (Secondary Mathematics)
entries.desc: Cognitive Tutor®, published by Carnegie Learning, is a math curricula that combines textbooks and interactive software.

entries.title: Cognitive Tutor® Integrated Math I (Secondary Mathematics)
entries.desc: Cognitive Tutor®, published by Carnegie Learning, is a math curricula that combines textbooks and interactive software.

entries.title: Cognitive Tutor® Integrated Math II (Secondary Mathematics)
entries.desc: Cognitive Tutor®, published by Carnegie Learning, is a math curricula that combines textbooks and interactive software.

entries.title: Cognitive Tutor® Integrated Math III (Secondary Mathematics)
entries.desc: Cognitive Tutor®, published by Carnegie Learning, is a math curricula that combines textbooks and interactive software.

entries.title: enVisionMATH (Primary Mathematics)
entries.desc: enVisionMATHis a core curriculum for students in kindergarten through grade 6. The program seeks to help students develop an understanding of math concepts through problem-based instruction, small-group interaction, and visual learning with a focus on reasoning and modeling. Differentiated instruction and ongoing assessment are used to meet the needs of students at all ability levels.

entries.title: University of Chicago School Mathematics Project (UCSMP) Functions, Statistics, and Trigonometry (Secondary Mathematics)
entries.desc: UCSMP is a core mathematics curriculum that emphasizes problem solving, real-world applications, and the use of technology. The curriculum is based on a student-centered approach with a focus on active learning that incorporates reading and uses a flexible lesson organization.

entries.title: University of Chicago School Mathematics Project (UCSMP) Geometry (Secondary Mathematics)
entries.desc: UCSMP is a core mathematics curriculum that emphasizes problem solving, real-world applications, and the use of technology. The curriculum is based on a student-centered approach with a focus on active learning that incorporates reading and uses a flexible lesson organization.

entries.title: University of Chicago School Mathematics Project (UCSMP) Precalculus and Discrete Mathematics (Secondary Mathematics)
entries.desc: UCSMP is a core mathematics curriculum that emphasizes problem solving, real-world applications, and the use of technology. The curriculum is based on a student-centered approach with a focus on active learning that incorporates reading and uses a flexible lesson organization.

entries.title: Saxon Geometry (Secondary Mathematics)
entries.desc: Saxon Math is a textbook series covering grades K–12 based on incremental development and continual review of mathematical concepts to give students time to learn and practice concepts throughout the year. The program is built on the premise that students learn best when instruction is incremental and explicit, previously learned concepts are continually reviewed, and assessment is frequent and cumulative. At each grade level, math concepts are introduced, reviewed, and practiced over time in order to move students from understanding to fluency.

entries.title: Saxon Advanced Math (Secondary Mathematics)
entries.desc: Saxon Math is a core curriculum for students in grades K–12 that uses an incremental approach to instruction and assessment. This approach limits the amount of new math content delivered to students each day and allows time for daily practice. New concepts are introduced gradually and integrated with previously introduced content so that concepts are developed, reviewed, and practiced over time rather than being taught during discrete periods of time, such as in chapters or units.

entries.title: Saxon Algebra II (Secondary Mathematics)
entries.desc: Saxon Math is a core curriculum for students in grades K–12 that uses an incremental approach to instruction and assessment. This approach limits the amount of new math content delivered to students each day and allows time for daily practice. New concepts are introduced gradually and integrated with previously introduced content so that concepts are developed, reviewed, and practiced over time rather than being taught during discrete periods of time, such as in chapters or units.

entries.title: Singapore Math® (Primary Mathematics)
entries.desc: Singapore Mathematics, produced in the United States under the names “Primary Mathematics” (grades 1-6), and “New Elementary Mathematics” (grades 7-12), is a year-long program designed for schools and homeschoolers alike. Based upon the rigorous mathematics curriculum used in Singapore from 1982 to 2001, it provides core curriculum and supplementary materials in the form of course books, manipulatives, teaching guides, and workbooks. The curriculum takes a “concrete-to-pictorial-to-abstract” approach which teaches mastery to each grade level in order to create a foundation for higher mathematical development. An alternative mathematics curriculum for low-performing students which focuses on the same math topics is available, and is taught at a slower pace with greater repetition by expert teachers.

entries.title: SuccessMaker® (Adolescent Literacy)
entries.desc: The SuccessMaker program is a set of computer-based courses used to supplement regular classroom reading instruction in grades K–8. Using adaptive lessons tailored to a student’s reading level, SuccessMaker aims to improve understanding in areas such as phonological awareness, phonics, fluency, vocabulary, comprehension, and concepts of print.

entries.title: Reconnecting Youth (Dropout Prevention)
entries.desc: Reconnecting Youth is an elective, credit-bearing course for students at risk of dropping out of school due to frequent absenteeism, low grades, or a history of dropping out of school. The core program element, the Personal Growth Class (sometimes called the Interpersonal Relations Class) is one semester long.

entries.title: Credit Recovery Programs (Dropout Prevention)
entries.desc: Credit recovery programs allow high-school students to recover course credit for classes they previously failed. Through in-school, online, or mixed modes, students can earn course credits to complete their diplomas or to avoid falling further behind in school.

entries.title: Shared Book Reading (Early Childhood Education)
entries.desc: Shared Book Readingencompasses practices that adults can use when reading with young children to enhance language and literacy skills. During shared book reading, an adult reads a book to an individual child or to a group of children and uses one or more planned or structured interactive techniques to actively engage the children in the text. The adult may direct the children’s attention to illustrations, print, or word meanings. The adult may engage children in discussions focused on understanding the meaning or sequence of events in a story or on understanding an expository passage. Adults may ask children questions, give explanations, and draw connections between events in the text and those in the children’s own lives as a way of expanding on the text and scaffolding children’s learning experiences to support language development, emergent reading, and comprehension. Importantly, the adult engages in one or more interactive techniques to draw attention to aspects of the text being read.

entries.title: Houghton Mifflin Reading© (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: A reading program for instruction in grades K–6. It uses Big Books (authentic literature), anthologies, Read Alouds, and audio compact discs to provide step-by-step instruction in reading. According to the developer’s website, Houghton Mifflin Reading© was developed based on the findings of the National Reading Panel. The product is designed to be used as a full-year curriculum program with instruction on developing oral language and comprehension, phonemic awareness, decoding skills (phonics, analogy, context, and word recognition), fluency, reading comprehension, writing, spelling, and grammar. Instruction is organized by a set of themes (10 for grades K–1 and 6 for grades 2–6) with selected Big Books (fiction and non-fiction literature) and other classroom activities to highlight the theme.

entries.title: Academy of READING® (Adolescent Literacy)
entries.desc: Academy of READING® is an online program, originally developed by AutoSkill International, that aims to improve students’ reading skills using a structured and sequential approach to learning. The program breaks the task of reading into manageable pieces and provides multiple opportunities for practice in five core areas—phonemic awareness, phonics, fluency, vocabulary, and comprehension.

entries.title: Carbo Reading Styles Program® (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: The Carbo Reading Styles Program® is a literacy intervention for students in grades K–12 that aims to meet the individual needs of learners through assessment and tailoring of the instruction to students’ particular reading learning styles.

entries.title: Reading Mastery (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: Reading Mastery is designed to provide systematic reading instruction to students in grades K–6. Reading Mastery can be used as an intervention program for struggling readers, as a supplement to a school’s core reading program, or as a stand-alone reading program, and is available in three versions. During the implementation of Reading Mastery, students are grouped with other students at a similar reading level, based on program placement tests. The program includes a continuous monitoring component.

entries.title: Let's Begin with the Letter People® (Early Childhood Education)
entries.desc: Let’s Begin with the Letter People®is an early education curriculum that uses thematic units to develop children’s language and literacy skills. A major focus is phonological awareness, including rhyming, word play, alliteration, and segmentation. Children are encouraged to learn in individual, small group, and whole-class settings. Both cognitive and socio-emotional development are presented as keys to learning.

entries.title: Scott Foresman-Addison Wesley Elementary Mathematics (Elementary School Mathematics)
entries.desc: Scott Foresman-Addison Wesley Elementary Mathematicsis a core curriculum for students at all ability levels in kindergarten through grade 6. The program supports students’ understanding of key math concepts and skills, and it covers a range of mathematical content across grades. The curriculum focuses on questioning strategies, problem-solving skills, embedded assessment, and exercises tailored to students of different ability levels. The program provides explicit problem-solving instruction, hands-on activities, and opportunities to extend students’ mathematical understanding through reading and writing connections.

entries.title: Voyages (Elementary School Math)
entries.desc: Developed for grades K-5 by the School District of Hillsborough County, FL, in accordance with state and district standards, Voyages uses a compact curriculum to accelerate math instruction and is comprised of two main components to meet diverse learning needs. Excursion Lessons provide teacher-led, interactive, contextual approaches to solving real-life problems through lessons which may take two to three days. Anchor Lessons focus on developing basic math skills and algebraic concepts through the use of visual and abstract representations. Textbooks and teaching guides are provided for lessons, as well as manipulatives kits and assessment tools.

entries.title: Vmath® (Elementary School Math)
entries.desc: Vmath®, distributed by Voyager Learning, is a supplemental mathematics curriculum for students in grades 2–8 who are struggling with math. The program aims to improve understanding of math concepts and performance on high-stakes assessments. A distinguishing feature of Vmath® is that it provides teachers with a specific, detailed script for each lesson. In-class instruction is supplemented with VmathLive®, a web-based program that allows students to practice their math skills outside of school.

entries.title: Second Step (Interventions for Children Identified With or at Risk for an Emotional Disturbance)
entries.desc: Second Stepis a classroom-based social skills program for students in preschool through junior high (ages 4–14 years), with a distinct curriculum for each grade. It is designed to reduce impulsive, high-risk, and aggressive behaviors and increase children’s social competence and other protective factors. The program builds on cognitive behavioral intervention models integrated with social learning theory, empathy research, and social information-processing research. It is intended to teach children to identify and understand their own and others’ emotions, choose positive goals, and successfully manage reactions when emotionally aroused.

entries.title: The Creative Curriculum® for Preschool, Fourth Edition (Early Childhood Education)
entries.desc: An early childhood curriculum that focuses on project-based investigations as a means for children to apply skills. It addresses four areas of development: social/emotional, physical, cognitive, and language.

entries.title: Ladders to Literacy (Early Childhood Education)
entries.desc: Ladders to Literacyis a supplemental early literacy curriculum published inLadders to Literacy: A Kindergarten Activity Book.The program targets children at different levels and from diverse cultural backgrounds. The activities are organized into three sections with about 20 activities each: print awareness, phonological awareness skills, and oral language skills.

entries.title: Fast ForWord® (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: Fast ForWord® is a computer-based reading program intended to help students develop and strengthen the cognitive skills necessary for successful reading and learning. The program, which is designed to be used 30 to 100 minutes a day, five days a week, for 4 to 16 weeks, includes two components.

entries.title: LANGUAGE!® (Adolescent Literacy)
entries.desc: LANGUAGE!® is a language arts intervention designed for struggling learners in grades 3–12 who score below the 40th percentile on standardized literacy tests. The curriculum integrates English literacy acquisition skills into a six-step lesson format. During a daily lesson, students work on phonemic awareness and phonics (word decoding), word recognition and spelling (word encoding), vocabulary and morphology (word meaning), grammar and usage (understanding the form and function of words in context), listening and reading comprehension, and speaking and writing.

entries.title: Sheltered Instruction Observation Protocol® (SIOP®) (English Language Learners)
entries.desc: TheSheltered Instruction Observation Protocol® (SIOP®)is a framework for planning and delivering instruction in content areas such as science, history, and mathematics to English language learners as well as other students. The goal ofSIOP®is to help teachers integrate academic language development into their lessons, allowing students to learn and practice English as it is used in the context of school, including the vocabulary used in textbooks and lectures in each academic discipline. Using this planning framework, teachers modify the way they teach so that the language they use to explain concepts and information is comprehensible to these students.SIOP®is intended to be applicable at levels of education from pre-K through community college. TheSIOP®model consists of instructional strategies that cover eight aspects of lesson design and delivery: lesson preparation, building background, comprehensible input, strategies, interaction, practice and application, lesson delivery, and review and assessment.

entries.title: Words Their Way™ (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: Words Their Way™ is an approach to phonics, vocabulary, and spelling instruction for students in kindergarten through high school. The program can be implemented as a core or supplemental curriculum and aims to provide a practical way to study words with students. The purpose of word study (which involves examining, manipulating, comparing, and categorizing words) is to reveal logic and consistencies within written language and to help students achieve mastery in recognizing, spelling, and defining specific words.

entries.title: Investigations in Number, Data, and Space® (Elementary School Mathematics)
entries.desc: Investigations in Number, Data, and Space is an activity-based, K–5 mathematics curriculum designed to help students understand number and operations, geometry, data, measurement, and early algebra. Each instructional unit focuses on a particular content area and lasts for 2–5.5 weeks. The curriculum encourages students to develop their own strategies for solving problems and engage in discussion about their reasoning and ideas. The lessons are activity-based in order to facilitate increased comprehension of basic math fundamentals. The curriculum is presented through a series of resource books called ”curriculum units” that provide teachers with guidance on implementation. One or more of the units for each year has a software program associated with it. Other materials include manipulatives, flash cards, overheads, and textbooks.

entries.title: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (Elementary School Mathematics)
entries.desc: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies is a peer-tutoring program for grades  K–6 that aims to improve student proficiency in several disciplines. During the 30-35 minute peer-tutoring sessions, students take turns acting at the tutor, coaching and correcting one another as they work through problems. The designation of tutoring pairs and skill assignment is based on teacher judgement of student needs and abilities, and teachers reassign tutoring pairs regularly.

entries.title: Tools for Getting Along (Interventions for Children Identified With or at Risk for an Emotional Disturbance)
entries.desc: Tools for Getting Alongis a 26-lesson curriculum designed to help upper elementary school teachers establish a positive, cooperative classroom atmosphere. Lessons are intended to reduce disruptive and aggressive behavior by helping students develop anger management skills. Students use problem-solving steps to generate, implement, and evaluate solutions to problems. The curriculum incorporates direct instruction, modeling, guided practice, independent practice, and skill generalization.

entries.title: Talent Development Middle Grades Program (Adolescent Literacy)
entries.desc: Talent Development Middle Grades Program (TDMG) is a whole school reform approach for large middle schools that face serious problems with student attendance, discipline, and academic achievement. The program includes both structural and curriculum reforms. It calls for schools to reorganize into small ”learning communities” of 200–300 students who attend classes in distinct areas of the school and stay together throughout their time in middle school. In addition to structural changes, schools adopting the program purchase one or more curricula that are intended to be developmentally appropriate and to engage students with culturally relevant content. For students who are behind in reading and math, the program provides additional periods devoted to these subjects that include group activities and computer-based lessons. To improve implementation, each school is assigned a team of “curriculum coaches” trained by the developer to work with school staff on a weekly basis to implement the program. In addition, teachers are offered professional development training, including monthly sessions designed to familiarize them with the program and demonstrate effective instructional approaches.

entries.title: The Spalding Method<sup>®</sup> (Beginning Reading)
entries.desc: The Spalding Method® is a language arts program for grades K–6 that uses explicit, integrated instruction and multisensory techniques to teach spelling, writing, and reading. The program and its textbook, The Writing Road to Reading, provide 32 weeks of lesson plans. Students work on program materials in spelling, writing, and reading for 90–120 minutes every day.

entries.title: Success for All® (English Language Learners)
entries.desc:
Success for All (SFA®) is a whole-school reform model (that is, a model that integrates curriculum, school culture, family, and community supports) for students in prekindergarten through grade 8. SFA® includes a literacy program, quarterly assessments of student learning, a social-emotional development program, computer-assisted tutoring tools, family support teams for students’ parents, a facilitator who works with school personnel, and extensive training for all intervention teachers. The literacy program emphasizes phonics for beginning readers and comprehension for all students. Teachers provide reading instruction to students grouped by reading ability for 90 minutes a day, 5 days a week. In addition, certified

teachers or paraprofessionals provide daily tutoring to students who

have difficulty reading at the same level as their classmates.

entries.title: The Spalding Method<sup>®</sup> (Adolescent Literacy)
entries.desc: The Spalding Method® is a language arts program for grades K–6 that uses explicit, integrated instruction and multisensory techniques to teach spelling, writing, and reading. The program and its textbook, The Writing Road to Reading, provide 32 weeks of lesson plans. Students work on program materials in spelling, writing, and reading for 90–120 minutes every day.

entries.title: Open Court Reading© (Adolescent Literacy)
entries.desc: Open Court Reading© is a reading program for grades K–6 that is designed to teach decoding, comprehension, inquiry, and writing in a three-part progression. Part One of each unit, Preparing to Read, focuses on phonemic awareness, sounds and letters, phonics, fluency, and word knowledge. Part Two, Reading and Responding, emphasizes reading literature for understanding, comprehension, inquiry, and practical reading applications. Part Three, Language Arts, focuses on writing, spelling, grammar, usage, mechanics, and basic computer skills.

entries.title: Reading Mastery (Students with Learning Disabilities)
entries.desc: Reading Mastery is designed to provide systematic reading instruction to students in grades K–6. Reading Mastery can be used as an intervention program for struggling readers, as a supplement to a school’s core reading program, or as a stand-alone reading program, and is available in three versions. During the implementation of Reading Mastery, students are grouped with other students at a similar reading level, based on program placement tests. The program includes a continuous monitoring component.

entries.title: Reading Edge (Adolescent Literacy)
entries.desc: Reading Edgeis a middle school literacy program that emphasizes cooperative learning, goal setting, feedback, classroom management techniques, and the use of metacognitive strategy, whereby students assess their own skills and learn to apply new ones. The program is a component of theSuccess for All (SFA)®whole-school reform model and provides eight levels of instruction, from beginning through eighth-grade reading levels. Students are grouped into classes based on ability, and whole-class reading instruction is delivered in daily 60-minute blocks. Instruction at the early levels uses fiction, nonfiction, and simple scripts to help students develop basic decoding skills, reading fluency, vocabulary, and comprehension. At reading level 3 and higher, students focus on developing comprehension strategies using both narrative and expository texts. All levels focus on building background knowledge and developing study skills. Although the program is often implemented in the context of theSFA®whole-school reform, this report focuses onReading Edgeas a stand-alone program in grades 4 and higher.

entries.title: The Center for Learning Technologies in Urban Schools (LeTUS) Program<sup>®</sup> (Science)
entries.desc: The LeTUS program is a three-year, project-based, technology-integrated middle school science curriculum for grades 6–8. The LeTUS program is composed of multiple units, each lasting between eight and ten weeks. Topics include global warming, water and air quality, force and motion, communicable diseases, and ecological systems. The units are designed around projects through which students learn science by conducting scientific investigations and using interactive computer software along with scientific visualization and graphing tools. Each unit stresses inquiry, student collaboration, and the use of computing and communications technologies. The sequence of units can be used in different ways, depending on standards and curriculum requirements; for example, teachers can use units at grade levels other than those suggested. Each unit can also be used independently when inserted into a different curricular context.

entries.title: Astronomy Resources for Intercurricular Elementary Science (ARIES): Exploring Motion and Forces (Science)
entries.desc: ARIES: Exploring Motion and Forcesis a physical science curriculum for students in grades 5–8 that employs 18 inquiry-centered, hands-on lessons called “explorations.” The curriculum draws upon students’ curiosity to explore phenomena, allowing for a discovery-based learning process. Group-centered lab work is designed to help students build an understanding of inertia, friction, gravity, speed, and acceleration. Students examine their prior ideas about the phenomena, formulate questions, build and use an apparatus to observe natural phenomena, make predictions, and gather data through structured experiments.Exploring Motion and Forcesis part of theARIESsequence of eight physical science units. TheARIESsequences can be used together for an overall curriculum or independently.

entries.title: Milieu Teaching (Early Childhood Education for Children with Disabilities)
entries.desc: Milieu teachingis a practice that involves manipulating or arranging stimuli in a preschool child’s natural environment to create a setting that encourages them to engage in a targeted behavior. For example, a teacher might place a desirable toy in a setting to encourage a child to request that toy (where requesting a toy is the desired target behavior). Typically,milieu teachinginvolves four strategies that a teacher will utilize to encourage a child to demonstrate a target behavior: modeling, mand-modeling, incidental teaching, and time-delay. Through adult modeling and functional consequences associated with child requests, targeted language behaviors can be improved in children who may have language delays or disabilities.
